Particle 2 couples to the gradient of that field with acceleration proportional to p1/p2, giving an attractive central acceleration -0.1 (p1/p2) exp(-r²/4t) r_vec/r². The interaction is isotropic, independent of velocity except through the particle’s changing position, and weakens strongly until the diffusive front reaches the probe.

Particle 1 acts as a fixed source for a radially symmetric diffusive field that begins spreading from the origin at time zero. Particle 2 couples to the gradient of that field with strength proportional to p1/p2, giving a central attractive acceleration proportional to \(-\frac{p_1}{\pi p_2}\exp(-r^2/(2t))\,\mathbf r/r^2\). The force has no velocity dependence; transverse motion changes only the instantaneous radius at which the time-dependent diffusive field is sampled.

Particle 1 acts as a fixed source for a time-dependent diffusive scalar field centered at the origin, with a Gaussian heat-kernel screening factor exp(-r²/(2t)). Particle 2 accelerates down the radial gradient of this field with coupling proportional to p1/p2, giving an attractive central acceleration proportional to -r/r² times that time-dependent exponential envelope. The force is isotropic, independent of velocity except through the trajectory, and particle 1 remains fixed.

The universe is governed by a mass-independent central force: in a uniformly upward-accelerating frame, every particle obeys \(q''=-\mu q/|q|^2\), corresponding to a logarithmic central potential. The observed coordinates include an additional universal upward drift \(+\frac12 g_y t^2\), and particle masses, including probe masses, do not change the acceleration or trajectory.

The universe contains a fixed logarithmic central field: every non-anchor particle accelerates toward the anchor with acceleration proportional to the displacement divided by squared separation, independent of its own mass. A small uniform upward acceleration acts on every particle including the anchor, so in the anchor’s co-accelerating frame the motion is purely central. The ring orbiters are test bodies on near-circular orbits in this same field and do not measurably back-react on the probes.

The universe is governed by a fixed central logarithmic potential: every particle experiences acceleration proportional to \(-\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^2\) toward the origin, with no dependence on its own mass or on other particles. Superposed on this is a constant upward acceleration, which produces the small systematic \(y\)-velocity drift seen for all particles including the anchor. The ring particles are merely initialised on near-circular orbits in this same external field; they do not exert detectable forces on the probes.

The universe is governed by a fixed central logarithmic potential, producing acceleration \(\mathbf a=-8\,\mathbf r/|\mathbf r|^2\), plus a uniform upward acceleration of \(0.05\). Particles couple to this field purely through inertial motion: their masses do not change their acceleration, and the probes do not exert measurable forces on one another or on the ring. The anchor and ring particles are not additional gravitational sources for the probes; they are context particles following the same external field, with the anchor near the singular centre mostly showing the uniform drift.

The probes and ring particles follow a fixed central field with acceleration proportional to \(-\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^2\), equivalent to a logarithmic radial potential, rather than interacting gravitationally with each other. Their inertial masses do not affect their motion, and the whole system also experiences a small constant acceleration in the positive y direction; the central anchor acts only as the source/marker of the field.

The probes are not interacting with each other or with the ring masses; all ordinary particles are test bodies in a fixed external field. The field is a central logarithmic potential, giving acceleration \(\mathbf a=-8\,\mathbf r/|\mathbf r|^2\), plus a constant upward acceleration of \(0.1\). The large central anchor is effectively inert and need not be used as a dynamical source for the probes.

The probes move independently in a fixed central logarithmic field: their acceleration is \(-8\,\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^2\), directed toward the coordinate centre, plus a uniform upward acceleration of \(0.1\). Particle masses do not alter the probe motion, and the ring/anchor particles are tracers of the same background field rather than significant mutual force sources for the probes.

The universe is governed by a central logarithmic potential: every non-anchor particle accelerates toward the anchor with acceleration proportional to the displacement vector divided by distance squared, independent of its own mass and independent of the other particles. The ring particles and probes are therefore test particles in the same central field, while the massive anchor follows a small externally prescribed drifting/epicyclic motion rather than reacting appreciably to forces.

The system is governed by a central logarithmic potential: every non-anchor particle has acceleration \(\mathbf{a}=-\mu\,\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^2\), independent of its mass and independent of the other orbiters or probes. The observed coordinate frame itself drifts with a deterministic cycloidal offset, so all final particle positions are the central-field solution plus this common frame shift.

The universe contains a fixed central logarithmic potential: every non-anchor particle experiences acceleration proportional to \(-\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^2\) with strength about 8, plus a constant upward acceleration of 0.1. Particle masses do not affect the acceleration, and the ring orbiters act as passive tracers rather than sources of a mutual gravitational field. The anchor is effectively fixed at the origin and defines the center of the external field.

Particle 1 is fixed and creates a central attractive field with acceleration proportional to p1/p2. The field points along the displacement toward the source and has radial magnitude (p1/p2)/(2πr)√(1+1/r²), behaving like an inverse-radius attraction at large distance and becoming stronger with a unit-length core correction at short range.

The source particle is fixed at the origin and generates a central logarithmic potential, equivalent to a two-dimensional field whose radial force falls as 1/r in magnitude. Particle 2 couples to this field with inertial response proportional to p1/p2, giving acceleration \(\mathbf{a}=-0.16(p_1/p_2)\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^2\). The dynamics are conservative and rotationally symmetric, with no velocity-dependent forces.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and generates a radial attractive scalar field whose force on particle 2 is central. The acceleration of particle 2 scales as p1/p2 and points toward the source with magnitude approximately (1/2π)(p1/p2)/(r tanh(3r/4)), i.e. a 2D inverse-radius field with a short-range coth-like enhancement. Initial velocity simply adds inertial motion while the central field continuously deflects the trajectory.

Particle 1 sources a static, radially symmetric field whose force has a two-dimensional long-range part scaling as \(1/r\) plus a short-range screened correction scaling as \(e^{-r}/r^2\). Particle 2 couples to this field through the ratio \(p_1/p_2\): \(p_1\) sets source strength while \(p_2\) acts like inertial mass. The motion is a velocity-independent central-force ODE directed toward the fixed source at the origin.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and generates a two-dimensional logarithmic potential, whose field is radial with magnitude proportional to \(1/r\). Particle 2 accelerates according to this field with coupling \(p_1/p_2\), giving \(\mathbf a=-(1/2\pi)(p_1/p_2)(\mathbf r/r^2)\). The dynamics are velocity-independent and rotationally symmetric about the source.

Particle 1 generates an attractive central field, softened at very short range, whose radial inverse-distance force is multiplied by a square periodic modulation \(0.16+0.08\cos(2\pi x/5)\cos(2\pi y/5)\). Particle 2 couples to this field with acceleration proportional to \(p_1/p_2\), so \(p_1\) sets the source strength and \(p_2\) behaves as inertial mass. The resulting motion is a second-order ODE with no velocity-dependent force and a fixed source at the origin.

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed source at the origin by a logarithmic radial field, so the acceleration is proportional to the displacement vector divided by squared distance. The field strength is not uniform: it is modulated by a spatial standing-wave factor, \(0.16 + 0.08\cos(2\pi x/5)\cos(2\pi y/5)\). The coupling scales as \(p_1/p_2\), making the source property increase the attraction and the probe property reduce its acceleration.

Particle 2 accelerates toward the fixed source at the origin with an inverse-radius form, scaled by the property ratio p1/p2. The field strength is not isotropic in magnitude: it is modulated by a lattice factor 0.16 + 0.08 cos(2πx/5) cos(2πy/5), while the force direction remains radial toward the source. A small softening core prevents a singularity at the origin.

Particle 1 sits at the origin and sources an attractive central field, but the field strength is not radially uniform: it is modulated by a two-dimensional standing wave proportional to cos(2πx/5) cos(2πy/5). Particle 2 couples to this field through acceleration proportional to p1 only, with p2 acting inertially neutral in the observed dynamics. The resulting force is radial toward the origin with magnitude coefficient (0.16 + 0.08 cos(2πx/5) cos(2πy/5))/r.

Particle 2 moves under a fixed central field sourced by particle 1 at the origin, with acceleration directed toward the source. The field strength is proportional to p1/p2 and has magnitude (0.16 + 0.08 cos(2πr/5))/r, so it is a two-dimensional inverse-radius attraction with a periodic radial modulation. The modulation stays positive, so the interaction remains attractive while alternating between stronger and weaker radial bands.

Particle 1 generates a fixed central inverse-square field, and particle 2 accelerates toward particle 1 with strength \(0.16\,p_1/p_2\). Thus the probe obeys the ordinary Kepler equation \(\ddot{\mathbf r}=-0.16(p_1/p_2)\mathbf r/|\mathbf r|^3\), while particle 1 remains stationary. The observed motion is fully explained by this conservative central field, with noisy position readouts but exact velocity evolution.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and generates a static attractive central field with inverse-square radial acceleration. Particle 2 responds according to \( \mathbf{a}=-0.16(p_1/p_2)\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^3 \), so the source strength scales with \(p_1\) while \(p_2\) behaves like inertial resistance. The motion is therefore a two-dimensional Kepler problem in a fixed central potential.

Particle 1 remains fixed at the origin and generates an attractive central field with an inverse-square radial form at large distances. Particle 2 couples to this field with acceleration proportional to p1/p2, meaning the source property increases the field strength while the probe property reduces the response. The force is not truly singular: it has a short-range Plummer-style softening length, so the denominator is \((r^2+\epsilon^2)^{3/2}\) rather than \(r^3\).

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and creates an attractive central field with inverse-square magnitude. Particle 2 couples to this field with acceleration proportional to p1/p2, so p1 acts like source charge and p2 like inertial mass. The resulting equation is Newtonian two-body motion in a static external potential, with coupling constant approximately 0.16.

The discovered dynamics are a fixed-source Kepler problem: particle 1 remains at the origin and creates an attractive inverse-square central field. Particle 2 couples to this field with acceleration proportional to p1/p2, with coupling constant 0.16, so its motion is governed by \(\ddot{\mathbf r}=-0.16(p_1/p_2)\mathbf r/|\mathbf r|^3\). This structure explains the radial infall, circular-orbit speeds, and angular deflections observed across the experiments.

Particle 1 remains fixed at the origin and sources a two-dimensional logarithmic potential field. Particle 2 moves as a test body whose acceleration is the negative gradient of that field, with coupling strength proportional to p1/p2 and prefactor 1/(2π), producing a central inverse-radius attraction.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and generates a central attractive field whose acceleration on particle 2 is directed along \(-\hat r\). The acceleration magnitude is proportional to \(p_1/p_2\) and equals \(0.16(p_1/p_2)/\sqrt{r^2+0.05^2}\), giving an inverse-distance law at large radius with a small-radius softening. Particle 2 otherwise follows ordinary second-order motion under this static field.

The source particle is fixed and generates an attractive central field whose potential is logarithmic in distance, so the field strength scales as inverse distance. Particle 2 couples to this field with acceleration proportional to p1/p2, directed along the separation vector toward particle 1. The observed trajectories are therefore governed by the ODE a = -0.16*(p1/p2)*(pos2-pos1)/|pos2-pos1|^2 with noisy position measurements.

Particle 1 sources a static two-dimensional logarithmic potential, whose gradient produces an attractive radial field proportional to \(-\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^2\). The source strength is proportional to \(p_1\), while the probe’s inertia is proportional to \(p_2\), giving acceleration \(\mathbf{a}=-(p_1/(2\pi p_2))\,\mathbf{r}/|\mathbf{r}|^2\). The force is central and velocity-independent, so angular momentum is conserved around the fixed source.

The source particle is fixed at the origin and generates a central logarithmic potential, whose gradient gives an inverse-distance radial force. Particle 2 couples to this field with acceleration proportional to the source property p1 and inversely proportional to its own property p2. The resulting motion is governed by a conservative central-force ODE with acceleration vector -0.16*(p1/p2)*r/|r|².

The particles move under mutual attraction generated by a two-dimensional logarithmic potential, so each source contributes a vector proportional to displacement divided by squared separation. All particles have equal source strength, but their response to the field is label-dependent: particles 0–3 respond fully, 4–6 at half strength, and 7–9 at quarter strength. Initial velocities matter normally, with the above field providing the acceleration in a second-order ODE.

The universe is a ten-body system in which every particle is an identical source of a two-dimensional logarithmic attractive field. The field from one particle at another is proportional to the displacement vector divided by the squared separation, with coupling \(1/(2\pi)\), and the particles follow second-order Newtonian motion under the sum of all pairwise fields. Initial velocities simply add as the initial condition for this mutual-interaction ODE.

The universe contains ten identical particles coupled to a two-dimensional scalar field whose Green’s function is logarithmic in distance. Each particle sources the field equally and accelerates down/up the resulting central field from all other particles, giving an attractive pairwise acceleration \(0.16\,(\mathbf r_j-\mathbf r_i)/|\mathbf r_j-\mathbf r_i|^2\). The dynamics are translation-invariant, label-independent, and have no direct velocity-dependent force beyond ordinary inertial motion.

The universe contains ten identical particles interacting through a two-dimensional logarithmic potential, so the acceleration of each particle is the sum of pairwise attraction vectors proportional to displacement divided by squared separation. The coupling is approximately exactly \(5/32\), all particles have equal source strength and inertial mass, and there is no drag, external field, or dependence on velocity beyond the usual kinematic update.

The universe contains ten identical particles coupled attractively through a two-dimensional logarithmic scalar potential. Each particle sources the field equally, so the acceleration of particle \(i\) is \(0.16\sum_{j\ne i}(\mathbf{x}_j-\mathbf{x}_i)/|\mathbf{x}_j-\mathbf{x}_i|^2\). Velocities are ordinary inertial initial conditions, with no evidence for drag, magnetic-like terms, particle-dependent charges, or external fields.

The universe is governed by a fixed central radial field, not by pairwise interactions among the particles. Every non-anchor particle has acceleration \(\mathbf a=(0.05-7.957747/r^2)\mathbf r\): an inward inverse-radius force plus an outward linear force, independent of particle mass. The anchor remains effectively stationary at the origin, while the ring orbiters and probes are test particles moving in this same field.

The probes and orbiters move independently in a fixed central field, not via appreciable pairwise interactions, and their inertial masses do not change their trajectories. The radial acceleration has the form a = (B - A/r²) r: an outward Hooke-like term plus an inward inverse-radius term, with the massive anchor effectively stationary at the origin.

The probes and ring particles move independently in a central force field fixed at the anchor: their acceleration is radial and equals \((\alpha-\beta/r^2)\mathbf{r}\). This is equivalent to an outward harmonic-like term plus an inward logarithmic/Kepler-like central attraction, with no dependence on the probe’s inertial mass. The orbiting ring is simply a set of particles following the same central ODE; it does not produce detectable pairwise forces on the probes.

The system is governed by a fixed central field, not by pairwise particle-particle forces: every particle feels the same radial acceleration from the origin. The acceleration has the form \( \mathbf a = (0.05 - 7.96/r^2)\mathbf r\), combining an inward inverse-radius term with a weak outward harmonic term. Particle masses do not influence trajectories, and the background ring is simply a set of particles in circular motion in this same central potential.

Particles behave as independent test bodies in a fixed central radial field rather than exerting mutual forces on one another. The acceleration is mass-independent and approximately \( \mathbf a=(k-c/r^2)\mathbf r\), combining an outward linear field with an inward inverse-radius attraction; the anchor remains fixed and the ring orbiters are simply other tracers of this same field.

The probes obey a mass-independent central-force law: their acceleration is \(\mathbf a=(k-c/r^2)\mathbf r\), i.e. a weak outward harmonic term combined with an inward logarithmic/anchor attraction. The anchor remains fixed at the origin, while the ring orbiters are passive tracers moving on the same central field and their circular speed determines the attraction constant. No probe mass dependence or probe-probe interaction was detectable in the governing trajectory law.

The universe is governed by a fixed central field: each non-anchor particle has acceleration \(a = 0.05\,r - 7.96\,r/|r|^2\), with an outward harmonic-like term and an inward logarithmic-potential term. Particles do not exert forces on one another and their masses do not affect their acceleration; the ring orbiters are simply particles on near-circular orbits in this central field. The anchor remains effectively fixed at the origin and is not needed as an interacting source.

The system is governed by a fixed central field rather than by pairwise interactions between particles: each body accelerates according to \(\mathbf a=(0.05-7.96/r^2)\mathbf r\). This combines an outward Hooke-like linear term with an inward inverse-radius term, producing the ring orbiters’ circular motion and the probes’ mass-independent trajectories. Probe masses and background particle masses do not affect the probe accelerations; the anchor is effectively fixed at the origin.

The universe is governed by a fixed, origin-centred radial field rather than by mutual gravity between particles. Every non-anchor particle has acceleration \(a = 0.05\,r - 8\,r/|r|^2\): an outward harmonic term plus an inward inverse-radius logarithmic attraction. Particle masses do not affect motion, and the anchor at the origin is effectively fixed.

The probes and ring particles are non-interacting test bodies in a fixed central field anchored at particle 0. Their acceleration is an outward linear term proportional to position plus an inward logarithmic-potential force of magnitude proportional to \(1/r\), i.e. \(\mathbf a=(\lambda-\gamma/r^2)\mathbf r\). Particle masses do not affect the acceleration; the orbiting ring is simply a set of tracers moving in the same central field.

The system is governed by an isotropic pairwise field: every particle attracts every other particle through a combination of inverse-distance and inverse-square force magnitudes. At very short separations the coupling reverses into a steep inverse-power repulsive core, which explains the collapse-and-explosive-rebound behaviour of cold rings. The centre particle is not special dynamically; in the symmetric centre-plus-ring configuration its net force simply cancels by symmetry.

The universe is governed by an all-to-all attractive central force: each particle produces a radial field proportional to a sum of inverse-square and inverse-cube vector kernels. Every particle couples identically to this field, so the acceleration of a body is the vector sum of contributions from the other ten bodies. The centre particle is dynamically ordinary; in the ring experiments its motion vanishes only by symmetry.

The universe is governed by a pinned central particle plus mutual pairwise attractions among the ring particles. Each particle couples to an isotropic scalar-like field whose force is a superposition of two inverse-power radial kernels, approximately a long-range inverse-distance component and a steeper short-range component. The centre does not recoil, so particle 0 acts as a fixed source while all other particles accelerate under the summed field from every other particle.

The universe is governed by a pairwise isotropic field: every particle accelerates according to the vector sum of interactions with all other particles. The coupling is proportional to the separation vector divided by a signed power of the shifted squared distance, approximately \((r^2-\ell^2)^{-1.18}\), with a preferred pole at \(\ell^2\approx1.3\). Outside this pole the interaction is attractive, while close passages through the pole create the sharp impulsive reversals seen in collapsing ring experiments.

The universe is governed by a pairwise attractive central field: each particle accelerates toward every other particle with vector acceleration proportional to the displacement divided by distance to the \(5/2\) power. The coupling constant is \(2\pi\), and the symmetric ring experiments reduce this to a radial acceleration scaling as \(r^{-3/2}\). A tiny softening length is included only to regularize the simultaneous central collapse of the symmetric configurations.

The universe is governed by a two-dimensional attractive logarithmic field: each particle acts as an identical source, and the acceleration it produces at another particle is proportional to the displacement vector divided by squared separation. The total acceleration of each particle is the linear superposition of this field from all other particles, while initial velocities simply provide the usual inertial first derivative of position.

The universe contains two label-defined charge species: particles 0–14 have one sign and particles 15–29 have the opposite sign. Their field is the two-dimensional Coulomb/log-potential field, so the acceleration from another particle is proportional to the displacement vector divided by squared distance; equal signs attract while opposite signs repel. Velocities and initial conditions otherwise enter through ordinary second-order Newtonian time evolution.

The universe is governed by a two-dimensional logarithmic inverse-distance field: every particle contributes equally to a scalar potential, giving an acceleration proportional to the sum of displacement vectors divided by squared separations. Particles 0–14 couple to this field with positive sign, while particles 15–29 have the opposite coupling, so the latter accelerate opposite to the same field rather than sourcing an opposite field themselves.

The universe contains identical particles that mutually attract through a two-dimensional logarithmic potential, so the acceleration contribution from particle j to particle i is proportional to \((x_j-x_i)/|x_j-x_i|^2\). The coupling is very close to \(1/(2\pi)\), consistent with the Green’s function normalization of the 2D Poisson equation. Motion is otherwise ordinary second-order Newtonian dynamics with the supplied initial velocities.

The universe contains a two-dimensional inverse-radius field: each particle contributes a logarithmic-potential field proportional to \((\mathbf r_j-\mathbf r_i)/|\mathbf r_j-\mathbf r_i|^2\). All particles source the field with the same sign, but the response depends on label: particles 0–14 accelerate along the field, while particles 15–29 accelerate oppositely. Initial velocities simply add through Newtonian time evolution under this field with a single global coupling constant.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and generates a two-dimensional logarithmic central field, whose spatial gradient is proportional to the displacement vector divided by squared radius. Particle 2 couples to this field with strength p1/p2, and the entire field oscillates in time as cos(pi t/2), producing alternating attractive and repulsive half-cycles with period 4. No velocity-dependent force or source recoil was detected.

Particle 1 is fixed and produces a radially symmetric, time-oscillating central field proportional to its scalar property p1. Particle 2 accelerates according to its inverse scalar property, with acceleration proportional to -(0.8 p1/p2) cos(pi t/2) r/r², so the force magnitude scales as 1/r and reverses sign periodically. This corresponds to a logarithmic central potential whose coupling is sinusoidally modulated in time.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and creates a radially symmetric two-dimensional logarithmic field, giving a force direction along the separation vector with magnitude proportional to \(1/r\). Particle 2 couples to this field with strength proportional to \(p_1/p_2\), and the universal coupling oscillates as \(\cos(\pi t/2)\), alternating between attraction and repulsion with period 4.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and generates a central radial field proportional to the displacement vector divided by squared distance. Particle 2 accelerates according to this field with coupling proportional to p1/p2, and the field strength is modulated by a time factor cos(pi t / 2), making the attraction periodically reverse direction.

Particle 1 generates an instantaneous, radially symmetric logarithmic field in 2D, whose gradient produces an inverse-radius central acceleration toward or away from the source. The field amplitude oscillates as cos(πt/2), and particle 2 couples with acceleration proportional to p1/p2. Structurally, the law is a time-modulated central-force ODE with particle 1 fixed at the origin.

Particle 1 is a fixed source at the origin generating a central attractive field. Particle 2 couples to this field with acceleration proportional to p1/p2, with radial magnitude equal to 0.15/r + 0.75/r². The resulting motion is an ordinary second-order ODE in two dimensions, with angular deflection arising solely from the central radial force.

Particle 1 is a fixed central source and particle 2 behaves as a probe whose acceleration scales as p1/p2. The field is radially attractive and is dominated at large distance by an inverse fractional power law, approximately \(r^{-3/2}\), consistent with a fractional-Poisson-like field in two spatial dimensions. Near the source there is an additional localized enhancement of the inward field, producing much stronger accelerations for radii below about one unit while preserving central-force motion and angular deflection.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and produces a rotationally symmetric attractive scalar field; particle 2 accelerates along the negative radial gradient with coupling proportional to p1/p2. The far field follows a fractional inverse-power Green function, giving radial acceleration close to r^(-3/2), while the source has an enhanced short-range core that strengthens the attraction at sub-unit separations.

Particle 2 moves in a central, attractive scalar field produced by the fixed source particle at the origin. The field couples linearly to the source property and inversely to the probe property, so the acceleration scales as \(p_1/p_2\) and points along \(-\hat r\). The radial field profile is not Newtonian or Coulombic; it has a strong short-range core and a long-range tail close to \(r^{-3/2}\).

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and creates an attractive central field; particle 2 accelerates along the line joining it to the source. The coupling scales as p1/p2, so the source property strengthens the field while the probe property acts like inertial mass. The radial field is not Newtonian but is best captured by a combination of inverse-power components, approximately proportional to A/r^(3/2) + B/r^2 + C/r^3.

The universe is governed by a two-dimensional Poisson/logarithmic potential: each particle sources a field whose force falls as displacement divided by squared distance. Particles 0–2 have source charge 1, particles 3–5 have source charge 3, and every particle responds with the same inertial coefficient, with coupling very close to 1/(2π). The interactions are label-dependent but otherwise pairwise additive and velocity-independent.

The six particles obey a second-order Newtonian ODE with pairwise forces from a two-dimensional logarithmic scalar potential, so each source contributes an acceleration proportional to \((r_j-r_i)/|r_j-r_i|^2\). The interaction is translation-invariant and depends on fixed label source strengths: particles 0–2 have unit charge, while particles 3–5 have about three times that charge. Initial velocities only set the inertial part of the trajectory; the field itself is determined instantaneously by the particle positions.

The universe is governed by a two-dimensional inverse-distance field: each particle feels an acceleration equal to a sum of displacement vectors toward the other particles divided by squared separation. Particles 0, 1, and 2 have source strength 0.16, while particles 3, 4, and 5 have source strength 0.48; the acceleration of a test particle depends on the source strengths, not on its own label. This is equivalent to motion in a logarithmic attractive potential with two species of field charge.

The universe contains six identical particles with no external field and no label-dependent properties. They interact through a pairwise attractive central field: each particle accelerates toward every other particle with vector acceleration proportional to the separation vector divided by distance^(7/4), giving a force magnitude proportional to r^(-3/4) with coupling 0.2. Velocities only set the initial kinetic state; the interaction itself depends only on instantaneous relative positions.

The universe contains six labelled particles coupled to a two-dimensional logarithmic potential field: each particle is a fixed source with charge 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, or 13 according to its label. A particle’s acceleration is the sum of attractive radial contributions from the other particles, proportional to the source charge and to \((\mathbf r_j-\mathbf r_i)/|\mathbf r_j-\mathbf r_i|^2\), with coupling about 0.04. Initial velocities simply provide the inertial part of the motion; there is no damping, screening, or softening evident.

The universe contains a self-interacting background population that acts like equal softened attracting sources in two dimensions. The field at any point is the sum of central pair contributions from the moving background particles, with an approximately inverse-square/Plummer-softened radial dependence. The last five particles are passive probes: they are accelerated by this background field but are not included as sources and therefore do not influence either the background or one another.

The universe contains a fixed population of equal unit-mass background particles interacting through an attractive Newtonian inverse-square force, with acceleration proportional to the displacement vector divided by distance cubed. The five probes are passive neutral tracers: they respond to the instantaneous gravitational field generated by the background but contribute no force to either the background or one another. Thus the background follows a self-consistent N-body trajectory, while each probe is integrated through the time-dependent field of those moving sources.

The universe contains a fixed population of mutually interacting background sources that generate a two-dimensional inverse-radius central field, equivalent to the gradient of a logarithmic potential. Each probe is a neutral test particle accelerated by the sum of the background-source contributions, proportional to displacement divided by squared distance, while probes do not source the field or affect each other. The background itself evolves under the same pairwise source-source law, so probe trajectories require integrating the moving background configuration forward in time.

The universe contains a fixed population of mutually interacting background sources whose two-dimensional field is the gradient of a logarithmic potential. Each acceleration is a superposition of terms proportional to the source-target displacement divided by squared distance, while the five neutral probes respond to this field but do not contribute to it. The only regularization needed is a tiny softening at exact coincidences; otherwise the interaction is the standard 2D inverse-distance force law.

The universe is governed by a two-dimensional inverse-distance attraction: each background particle is an equal point source of a logarithmic potential, giving acceleration proportional to the displacement vector divided by squared separation. The five probes are neutral test particles, so they respond to the summed background field but do not contribute to the field or perturb the background evolution. A small softening length is included only to regularise near-coincident encounters.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and sources a screened two-dimensional scalar field whose Green-function gradient has radial profile K1(r/2). Particle 2 couples to the field through an acceleration proportional to p1/p2, always directed toward the source, with magnitude 0.08*(p1/p2)*K1(r/2). The dynamics are otherwise Newtonian: position evolves from velocity and velocity evolves under this central Bessel-screened attraction.

The source particle is fixed and produces a static screened radial field in two dimensions, whose force profile is the gradient of a modified-Bessel Green function. The probe couples to this field with acceleration proportional to p1/p2 and directed toward the source, with radial magnitude 0.08 K1(r/2). The motion is otherwise ordinary second-order dynamics with the initial velocity simply added to the field-driven acceleration.

Particle 1 is fixed at the origin and sources a screened scalar field in two spatial dimensions, with Green-function profile proportional to the modified Bessel function K0(r/2). Particle 2 accelerates down the gradient of this field, so the force is radial and attractive with magnitude proportional to K1(r/2), scaled by p1/p2. This produces a logarithmic-like short-range singularity and an exponentially screened long-range tail.

Particle 2 moves in a central attractive field generated by particle 1 at the origin. The acceleration is proportional to \(p_1/p_2\) and points toward the source, with a softened inverse-square radial form multiplied by a Gaussian screening factor, \(a \propto - (p_1/p_2)\,\mathbf r (r^2+0.5)^{-3/2}\exp[-r^2/50]\). Particle 1 remains fixed, while particle 2 follows the resulting second-order ODE.

The source particle is fixed at the origin and produces a screened 2D scalar field with Green function proportional to K0(r/2), where K0 is a modified Bessel function. The probe couples to the field gradient, giving a central attractive acceleration proportional to -(p1/p2) K1(r/2) r_hat with normalization 1/(4π). Thus the motion is an ordinary second-order ODE in this central screened potential, independent of velocity except through the changing position.

This universe contains a universal attractive pairwise force: every particle pulls every other particle with acceleration magnitude G/d^p along the line joining them, where d is the separation, G ≈ 1/6, and p ≈ 1.5 — a "three-halves" power law that lies between 2D logarithmic gravity and Newtonian inverse-square gravity. The law was verified across separations from ~0.3 to ~60 and across collapsing, circularly orbiting, eccentric, and escaping ring configurations: the measured ring acceleration scales precisely as R^(-1.5), and the absolute magnitude matches the sum of centre and ring-neighbour contributions with a single universal coupling, with no evidence of screening, softening, or velocity-dependent forces. All particles couple identically (equal effective masses), so the centre particle remains at rest in symmetric configurations as observed.

All 11 particles interact through a pairwise attractive central force whose magnitude scales as G/d^1.5 — a long-range "modified gravity" with a shallower fall-off than Newton's inverse square. Each particle's acceleration is the vector sum of these contributions from every other particle, with G ≈ 0.166 per unit mass and no drag, softening, or velocity-dependent terms. This was established by measuring ring-collapse accelerations across radii 2–10 (clean exponent 1.50), confirming stable circular orbits at v = √C·r^(-1/4), and fitting eccentric and escaping trajectories down to the observation-noise floor.

This universe contains a universal pairwise attractive force: every particle accelerates every other particle toward itself with magnitude G/d^p, where d is the pairwise distance, p ≈ 3/2 (instead of Newton's inverse-square), and G ≈ 1/6. All 11 particles (centre and ring) couple identically, with no mass differences or screening. I verified the exponent via initial-acceleration scaling across ring radii 2–10, confirmed energy and angular-momentum conservation with the implied potential −2G/√d, and validated the law against circular orbits, eccentric orbits, collapses, and escape trajectories, with an MSE fit converging to G = 0.1659, p = 1.4985.

All 11 particles interact through a pairwise attractive central force whose acceleration magnitude falls off as G/d^1.5 with separation d — softer than Newtonian gravity's inverse-square law — with G ≈ 0.166 (close to 1/6). This was established by measuring the radial collapse acceleration of rest rings at radii 2–10 (scaling exactly as r^-1.5), by sustaining perfectly circular orbits at three radii using v_circ = sqrt(5.06·G·r^-0.5), and by the chaotic scattering of the centre particle after collapse, which shows the force is genuinely pairwise rather than from a fixed central field. The force is velocity-independent and conservative, corresponding to a pair potential proportional to -1/sqrt(d).

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed source particle at the origin by a central, velocity-independent force. The acceleration magnitude is (p1/p2)·(1/(r² + 0.04) + 0.1): a softened (Plummer-like) inverse-square gravitational core plus a constant-magnitude confining tail that dominates at large separations, reminiscent of MOND-like or confinement potentials. Notably, the acceleration scales linearly with the source property p1 but inversely with the probe's own property p2, so heavier probes respond more weakly — the probe property acts purely as inertia rather than as a force charge.

The probe particle experiences a purely central, velocity-independent attraction toward the fixed source at the origin. The acceleration magnitude scales as the ratio p1/p2 (source charge over probe inertia) times the sum of a softened inverse-square term, 1/(r² + ε²) with ε ≈ 0.2, and a constant background pull of about 0.1 that dominates at large separation (like a confining or dark-energy-like term). This means the force never diverges at the origin (it caps near 25·p1/p2) and never falls below the constant floor, producing bound, recurring orbits for essentially all initial conditions.

The probe particle experiences a purely radial, velocity-independent attraction toward the fixed source at the origin. The acceleration magnitude is (p1/p2)·[1/(r²+0.04) + 0.1]: a softened (Plummer-type, softening length ε≈0.2) inverse-square gravitational term plus a constant confining force that never lets the particle escape, no matter how far away it is. The source property p1 acts multiplicatively as a charge while the probe property p2 acts as inertial mass, so the acceleration scales as their ratio.

The probe particle experiences a purely central, attractive force directed toward the fixed source particle, with acceleration magnitude (p1/p2)·(1/r² + 0.1) — an inverse-square Newtonian term combined with a constant ("string-like" confining) term, corresponding to a potential −1/r + 0.1r per unit charge ratio. Unusually, the coupling depends on the ratio of the two particle properties rather than their product: increasing p1 strengthens the pull while increasing p2 weakens it (i.e., p2 acts like inertia). The force has no velocity dependence, so energy and angular momentum are conserved, producing precessing bound orbits and confined radial oscillations.

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed source at the origin by a central, velocity-independent force. The acceleration magnitude is (p1/p2)·(1/(r² + 0.04) + 0.1): a softened (Plummer-cored) inverse-square gravity-like term that stays finite at the origin, plus a small constant "confining" background acceleration that never lets the probe escape. The source property p1 acts as a charge multiplying the force, while the probe property p2 acts as inertia dividing it; no drag, no velocity coupling, and no dependence on direction other than the radial unit vector.

Particle 2 moves under a Newtonian-gravity-like law: it is attracted toward the fixed source particle at the origin with acceleration a = G·p1·p2/r² directed along the line joining them, where G ≈ 1. Notably, the probe's acceleration depends on the product of both particles' scalar properties (including its own, p2), unlike standard gravity where the test mass cancels — so p1 and p2 enter fully symmetrically as a coupling product. The force is purely central, conservative, and velocity-independent: trajectories conserve energy and angular momentum, producing circular, elliptical, and hyperbolic Kepler orbits exactly as predicted by an inverse-square attraction.

Particle 2 obeys a central-force law: it accelerates directly toward the fixed source particle 1 with magnitude a = G·p1·p2/r², where r is the separation and G ≈ 1. Unlike Newtonian gravity, the probe's own scalar property p2 multiplies its acceleration (as if the property is a charge that couples to the source field, with no separate inertial mass), and the force is always attractive. The interaction is conservative with potential energy per coupling −G·p1·p2/r, verified by exact energy conservation along radial escape and closed circular orbits, with no velocity dependence or drag.

Particle 2 moves under a central, attractive inverse-square force sourced by particle 1 at the origin, with acceleration magnitude a = p1·p2/r² pointing toward particle 1. Unlike Newtonian gravity, the probe's own scalar property p2 directly scales its acceleration (it does not cancel as inertial mass would), so doubling either particle's property doubles the acceleration. The dynamics are conservative and velocity-independent: energy E = v²/2 − p1·p2/r and angular momentum are exactly conserved, producing standard Keplerian conic-section orbits with no softening, screening, or drag down to separations of at least 0.5.

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed source at the origin by a central force whose acceleration magnitude is a = p1·p2/(r² + ε²) with G = 1 and softening length ε = 0.05 — a softened inverse-square (Keplerian) law. Unlike Newtonian gravity, the probe's own scalar property p2 multiplies its acceleration, so the coupling is symmetric in both particle properties (charge-like rather than mass-like); the force has no velocity dependence and supports exact circular orbits at v = √(p1·p2/r). Trajectories that plunge through the softened core gain energy and escape at a fixed speed, a signature of the world's fixed-step (dt ≈ 0.01) numerical integrator rather than additional physics, which my law reproduces by integrating with fixed-step RK4 at the same timestep.

Particle 2 moves under a central, attractive, inverse-square force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with acceleration magnitude p1·p2/r². Unlike Newtonian gravity, the probe's acceleration scales with the product of both particles' scalar properties — including its own — so the force constant is symmetric in p1 and p2 (verified: (9,1), (3,3), (1,9) give identical motion). The force is isotropic and velocity-independent, conserving energy E = ½v² − p1·p2/r and angular momentum, producing closed Keplerian ellipses for bound states and hyperbolic scattering for unbound ones, with coupling constant G ≈ 1 and exponent ≈ 2.

Each particle generates a radial inverse-square field whose strength and sign are set by its own charge, and all other particles accelerate in that field with a coupling that is completely independent of their own charge — even neutral particles feel the force. Thus a positive charge pushes everything away and a negative charge pulls everything in, with acceleration a_i = Σ_j q_j (r_i − r_j)/|r_i − r_j|³ (k ≈ 1), which breaks Newton's third law (a 2:1 charge pair accelerates in a 1:2 ratio). Superposition holds exactly, there is no drag or velocity dependence, and isolated particles move uniformly.

Each particle sources a Coulomb-like inverse-square field proportional to its signed charge, but particles respond to the total field as pure test masses: the acceleration of particle i is the sum over all other particles j of q_j·(r_i−r_j)/|r_i−r_j|³, completely independent of particle i's own charge. This makes the interaction non-reciprocal (Newton's third law is violated — a charged particle pushes a neutral one, which exerts no force back), with positive charges repelling everything and negative charges attracting everything. There is no drag, no velocity-dependent force, and superposition holds exactly with unit coupling constant.

Each particle with charge q_j creates an inverse-square vector field pointing away from itself (toward itself if q_j is negative), and every other particle — regardless of its own charge, even if neutral — accelerates in response to the superposition of these fields: a_i = Σ_j q_j (r_i − r_j)/|r_i − r_j|³ with coupling constant K ≈ 1. Unlike Coulomb's law, the force is not proportional to the product q_i·q_j, so it violates Newton's third law: two opposite charges drift in the same direction rather than attracting symmetrically. There is no drag, no velocity-dependent (magnetic) force, and the dynamics obey clean linear superposition.

This universe contains a one-way Coulomb-like field: each particle with charge q creates an instantaneous, unretarded radial field E = q r̂/r² obeying exact superposition, and every particle (unit mass) accelerates as a = ΣE regardless of its own charge — so positive charges repel all particles and negative charges attract all particles, breaking Newton's third law. The receiver's charge only matters as a source for others; neutral particles are perfect test particles that still respond fully to the field. The 1/r² form holds without softening from r ≈ 0.5 out to r ≈ 25, with coupling constant 1 and no velocity-dependent (magnetic) terms.

In this universe each charge generates a Coulomb-like inverse-square field (a ∝ q_source/r², coupling constant ≈ 1), but particles respond to the field independently of their own charge — even neutral particles are accelerated. Thus positive charges repel every particle and negative charges attract every particle, breaking Newton's third law for unequal charge pairs (e.g. a +/− pair drifts together in the same direction). The fields superpose linearly, there is no softening at short range, and there are no velocity-dependent (drag or magnetic) forces — free particles move ballistically.

This universe is governed by two-dimensional gravity: every mass sources a logarithmic potential, so the attraction on a test particle falls off as 1/distance (not 1/distance²), which I verified directly from radial force profiles and from the background swarm's flat, radius-independent orbital speed of about 3.1. The probes are massless tracers that feel two contributions: a weak 1/d pull from each of the 20 visible background particles (mass ≈ 0.09 each), and a dominant pull from an invisible extended central mass (total ≈ 8.9) concentrated near the origin with a softened, Plummer-like core of scale ≈ 1.5, so the force grows linearly inside the core and decays as M/r outside. The deep core additionally contains small, apparently time-varying sub-clumps of the hidden mass, but their effect averages out and the softened central profile captures the dynamics everywhere except within a unit or so of the centre.

This 2D universe runs on logarithmic-potential gravity: the field satisfies a two-dimensional Poisson equation, so every mass exerts an attractive force that falls off as 1/distance rather than 1/distance². The structure consists of a stationary, extended central mass concentration at the domain centre — well described by a Gaussian density blob of width ≈1.3 whose enclosed mass saturates at ≈8.3 — surrounded by 20 equal-mass background particles (coupling ≈0.105 each) on roughly circular orbits of radius 10–14 at speed ≈3, exactly the flat rotation curve a log potential produces. The five probes are massless tracers that feel the central blob plus the instantaneous 1/d pulls of all background particles, with no velocity-dependent or self-generated forces; close encounters showed the pairwise force stays 1/d down to sub-unit separations with negligible softening.

The universe contains a fixed, axisymmetric "dark halo" centred at the origin whose force on any particle is attractive with magnitude K·r/(r²+c²): at large radii this gives a ≈ K/r, a logarithmic potential producing the observed flat rotation curve where all 20 background particles orbit counterclockwise at speed √K ≈ 3 regardless of radius, while inside the softening length c ≈ 1.2 the core is approximately harmonic, causing probes placed near the centre to oscillate. On top of the halo, each background particle acts as a weak two-dimensional gravitational point source (force ∝ m/d, m ≈ 0.09, the 2D/logarithmic analogue of Newtonian gravity), which I confirmed by the slight increase in effective central pull when probes were placed outside the background ring (Gauss-law enclosed-mass growth of ≈ 20m); the five probes are pure test particles that feel both fields but exert no forces themselves, and the dynamics show no velocity-dependent or time-explicit forces.

This universe runs on 2D logarithmic-potential gravity: every mass attracts every other body with an acceleration proportional to m/d (force exponent fitted at α≈1.0–1.04 rather than the 3D inverse-square law), which naturally produces the flat rotation curve (v≈3.3 at all radii) followed by the 20 visible background particles. The dominant field source is a hidden compact cluster at the domain centre with effective mass Mc≈9.2, confined within r≲2 (acting like a softened point mass with softening scale ε≈1.1, and producing rapid small-scale force fluctuations only deep inside that core), while each visible background particle carries a small mass mb≈0.15, together accounting for the far-field total a·r≈11.1. Probes are massless test particles that feel but do not generate the field, the dynamics are conservative (no drag), and forces superpose linearly over all sources.

The system is a 2D "galaxy" model: a fixed, softened central attractor near the origin produces an acceleration K·r²/(r³+s³) pointing inward — at large radii this is a pure 1/r force (logarithmic potential), which explains why all 20 background particles orbit with the same speed v ≈ √K ≈ 2.9 regardless of radius (a flat rotation curve), while inside the core radius s ≈ 1.5 the force smoothly turns over toward zero. On top of this, each background particle is itself a weak source of 2D log-potential gravity, attracting all other matter with magnitude gm/d (gm ≈ 0.14, no softening detectable), so the effective enclosed coupling grows slightly as more background particles fall inside a probe's orbit. The probes are massless tracers: they respond to both fields, generate none, and I found no velocity-dependent (drag or magnetic-like) forces anywhere in the domain.

The source particle switches on at t = 0 and emits a scalar field that diffuses outward according to the 2D heat equation with diffusivity D ≈ 0.5, so the influence on the probe builds up gradually as the diffusion front (r ~ √(4Dt)) reaches it. The probe is attracted toward the source with acceleration magnitude (p1/p2)·K·exp(−r²/(4Dt))/r, i.e. it follows the gradient of the heat-kernel field of a continuously emitting point source, with K ≈ 1/(2πD). The coupling is linear in the source charge p1 and inversely proportional to the probe property p2 (which acts as inertia), and the force is purely radial with no velocity dependence.

Particle 1 sources a scalar field that obeys a 2D diffusion equation with diffusivity D ≈ 0.5, with emission switched on at t = 0, so its influence spreads outward gradually rather than instantaneously — at any fixed distance r the force starts at zero and grows over time as exp(-r²/4Dt)/r. The probe particle couples to the gradient of this field, experiencing an attractive acceleration of magnitude (1/π)·(p1/p2)·exp(-r²/(4Dt))/r directed toward the source, i.e. force scales linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2. This is the steady-source Green's-function solution of the heat equation in two dimensions (an exponential-integral potential), whose gradient asymptotes to a 1/r force only after the diffusive front (r ~ √(4Dt)) has passed the probe.

The source particle at the origin continuously emits a scalar field starting at t = 0, which spreads by ordinary diffusion in 2D with diffusion constant D ≈ 1/2; the resulting potential is the time-integral of the 2D heat kernel weighted by the source strength p1. The probe couples to the gradient of this field, experiencing a purely attractive central acceleration a = (p1/(π p2 r))·exp(−r²/(2t)), so the force "switches on" with a delay that grows with distance as the field diffuses outward, asymptotically approaching a 1/r (2D Coulomb-like) attraction. Structurally, p1 acts as a linear source charge, p2 acts as inertial mass (acceleration ∝ 1/p2), and the force direction is exactly radial with no velocity dependence.

Particle 1 is a fixed point source that, beginning at t=0, continuously emits a scalar field obeying a 2D diffusion equation with diffusivity D≈0.5. The probe particle couples to the gradient of this field: it experiences an attractive central acceleration a = G·(p1/p2)·exp(-r²/(4Dt))/r with G≈1/π, meaning force strength is proportional to the source charge p1 and the probe's p2 acts as inertial mass. The signature feature is a diffusive retardation — the force at distance r only "switches on" after a time of order r²/(4D), then asymptotically approaches a steady 1/r attraction (the 2D steady-state flux of a continuously emitting source).

Particle 1 sources a scalar field that obeys the 2D diffusion equation: the source turns on at t=0 and the field spreads outward diffusively with diffusivity D ≈ 0.47, so distant probes feel essentially no force until the diffusive front (radius ~ 2√(Dt)) reaches them. The probe is accelerated down the field gradient, giving an attractive central acceleration of magnitude (p1/p2)·exp(-r²/(4Dt))/(2πD·r) — the gradient of the continuously-emitting 2D diffusion Green's function — which means the force is exponentially screened at early times and approaches a 1/r attraction at late times. The acceleration scales linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2 (p2 acts as inertial mass), and the law is fully isotropic with no dependence on probe velocity.

The system is governed by a modified pairwise gravity whose force falls off as 1/r instead of 1/r² (i.e., a 2D logarithmic potential, as if each mass sources the field of an infinite line / solution of the 2D Poisson equation): the acceleration imparted on any particle by a source of mass m at distance r is G·m/r directed toward the source, with G ≈ 7.96×10⁻¹⁵, and is independent of the receiving particle's inertial mass (equivalence principle holds). Because the anchor's mass is 10¹⁵, it completely dominates — the ring orbiters and probes (masses ~1–100) source negligible field, which I verified by detecting no mutual force between heavy probes at close range; this central 1/r field exactly explains the flat rotation curve where orbiters and probes at all radii circle with the same speed v = √(G·M) ≈ 2.82. In addition, a constant uniform external field of magnitude 0.05 in the +y direction accelerates every particle, including the anchor, producing a slow collective drift of the whole system.

The universe contains a single gravitating source — the super-massive anchor — whose field is logarithmic: any particle at distance r from the anchor is pulled toward it with acceleration C/r (C ≈ 7.957 = 2.8209², the orbiters' universal circular speed squared), with no dependence on the particle's own mass or velocity and no softening or screening from r ≈ 0.5 out to r ≈ 60. Particles do not attract one another at all — a mass-1000 probe exerted no measurable force on close neighbours — so the orbiters and probes are pure test particles in the anchor's field. In addition, a constant uniform acceleration g ≈ 0.05 in the +y direction acts on every particle including the anchor itself (whose trajectory is exactly y = ½gt²), so the whole system free-falls together while orbits remain Keplerian-like (energy-conserving) in the anchor's falling frame.

The universe contains a single massive anchor that sources a logarithmic central potential V(r) = C·ln(r) with C ≈ 25/π ≈ 7.9577: every particle, regardless of its inertial mass, accelerates toward the anchor's instantaneous position with magnitude C/r (this gives the flat "rotation curve" — the ring orbiters at r=5 and probes at any radius all orbit at the same speed √C ≈ 2.8209). Superimposed on this is a uniform constant external field of strength 0.05 in the +y direction that acts identically on every particle, including the anchor itself, so the whole system uniformly accelerates upward (y = 0.025t²). There are no particle–particle interactions among orbiters and probes, no drag, and no velocity-dependent forces — only the anchor's 1/r attraction plus the uniform field.

This universe has 2D "logarithmic-potential" gravity: a massive body produces an attractive acceleration proportional to its mass and falling off as 1/r (verified exponent 1.00 ± 0.01), so the circular orbital speed sqrt(K) ≈ 2.821 is independent of radius — exactly as observed for the ring orbiters and probes at all radii from 0.5 to 22. The acceleration of a test particle is independent of its own inertial mass and velocity (equivalence principle, no drag), and with the coupling K·m_source/M_anchor only the enormous anchor (m = 10^15) sources any measurable force — probes and orbiters, even with mass 100, contribute nothing detectable. On top of this, a uniform constant external field of magnitude ≈ 0.05 in the +y direction accelerates every particle equally, including the anchor itself, causing the whole system to drift parabolically upward.

This universe contains a single massive anchor that sources a two-dimensional logarithmic gravitational potential: every other particle is accelerated toward the anchor's instantaneous position with magnitude K/r (K ≈ 7.957 ≈ 25/π), so circular orbital speed (√K ≈ 2.8209) is independent of radius — a "flat rotation curve" world. The coupling is purely geometric: acceleration is independent of the test particle's mass, the ring orbiters and probes source no field themselves, and no probe–probe forces exist. On top of this, a uniform external acceleration g ≈ 0.05 in the +y direction acts on all bodies including the anchor, so the entire system falls freely together and dynamics in the anchor's frame are exact central-force motion conserving energy v²/2 + K ln r and angular momentum.

This universe contains a single field source — the massive anchor — which generates a two-dimensional logarithmic potential: every other particle feels an acceleration of magnitude k/r (k ≈ 7.96 ≈ 25/π) directed toward the anchor's instantaneous position, completely independent of the test particle's mass; probes and orbiters source no field of their own and exert no mutual forces (verified with mass-100 probes leaving the anchor unaffected). On top of this, a spatially uniform external field rotating in time, g(t) = 0.1·(−sin 0.6t, cos 0.6t), acts identically on all particles including the anchor, producing the slow common-mode drift of the whole system. The combination of an inverse-first-power central attraction (flat rotation curves: circular speed √k independent of radius) and the rotating uniform driving field fully reproduces all observed trajectories down to the observation-noise floor.

The dominant interaction is a central force sourced solely by the massive anchor: every other particle is pulled toward the anchor with acceleration K/r (K ≈ 8.0), independent of the test particle's mass — a 2D logarithmic potential, which explains why the ring orbiters all circle at the same speed (√K ≈ 2.82) regardless of radius. Neither the probes nor the ring orbiters source any force on one another (a mass-10 probe exerted no detectable pull on a neighbor 2 units away). On top of this, the entire system — anchor included — is immersed in a spatially uniform external field of fixed amplitude A ≈ 0.1 that rotates counter-clockwise at angular rate ω ≈ 0.6 (pointing along +y at t = 0), producing the slow common-mode drift of all particles and the anchor's epicyclic wander.

The universe contains a single central source (the anchor) that generates a logarithmic potential: every other particle is accelerated toward the anchor with magnitude K/r (K ≈ 7.96), completely independent of the particle's own inertial mass — circular orbits at any radius share the same speed √K ≈ 2.82, like a flat galactic rotation curve. Particles do not gravitate toward each other at all: even a 1000× mass probe placed 1.5 units from another probe produced no measurable deflection, so the field is sourced only by the anchor. On top of this, a spatially uniform external acceleration field of fixed magnitude 0.1 rotates at angular frequency 0.6 rad/s (direction (−sin 0.6t, cos 0.6t)) and pushes every particle, including the anchor, identically, producing a common oscillatory plus secular drift of the whole system.

This universe contains a single gravitating source — the massive anchor — which generates a two-dimensional logarithmic potential, so every other particle is pulled toward the anchor with acceleration K/r (K ≈ 7.97) that is independent of the test particle's own mass; the orbiters and probes exert no detectable forces on each other, even at mass 40 and sub-unit separations. On top of this central force, a spatially uniform external acceleration field of fixed amplitude 0.1 rotates at angular frequency 0.6 rad/s, g(t) = 0.1(−sin 0.6t, cos 0.6t), acting identically on all 26 particles including the near-immovable anchor (its drift trajectory matches this field analytically). The combination explains all observed trajectories to within the observation noise: circular orbits with the v² = K rotation curve characteristic of a flat-rotation (logarithmic) potential, energy-conserving radial plunges, and a common slow circular drift of the entire system.

This universe contains a single gravitating source — the heavy central anchor — which generates a two-dimensional logarithmic potential: every other particle is pulled toward the anchor's instantaneous position with acceleration K/r (K ≈ 7.96), independent of both the test particle's mass and the source distance scaling of normal gravity, producing the flat rotation curve seen in the ring orbiters (all circling at speed √K ≈ 2.82 regardless of radius). Particles exert no forces on one another: even a probe of mass 10¹² produced no measurable deflection of neighbors, and probe trajectories are completely independent of their own masses. Superimposed on this is a spatially uniform, mass-independent rotating drive acceleration of magnitude ≈ 0.10 with angular frequency ω = 0.6, a(t) = B(−sin ωt, cos ωt), which acts on every particle including the anchor, giving the whole system a common epicyclic drift velocity A(cos ωt − 1, sin ωt) with A ≈ 0.166.

The probe particle moves under a purely central, attractive force directed toward the fixed source at the origin, with magnitude proportional to p1 (source strength) and inversely proportional to p2 (inertial mass). The force is the superposition of two field contributions: a long-range 2D-gravity tail F ∝ 1/(2πr) (the gradient of a logarithmic potential, as for a massless field in two dimensions) and a short-range screened component F ∝ (1+mr)e^(−mr)/r², which is exactly the gradient of a Yukawa potential e^(−mr)/r with screening mass m ≈ 1.68. The initial velocity simply sets the orbit's initial conditions; trajectories from radial drops, bound orbits, and slingshot passes are all reproduced to the observation-noise floor by this single law.

The probe particle is attracted toward the fixed source at the origin by a central, velocity-independent force whose acceleration scales exactly as p1/p2 (source property over probe property, so p2 acts like inertial mass and p1 like a source charge). The radial dependence is the gradient of a two-component potential: a long-range 2D logarithmic potential (giving acceleration A/r with A ≈ 1/2π, as if sourced by a 2D Poisson field equation) plus a short-range screened Yukawa potential C·e^(−μr)/r (giving the force term C(1+μr)e^(−μr)/r² with screening length 1/μ ≈ 0.6). The motion is purely Newtonian second-order dynamics in this static composite field, with no velocity- or time-dependent couplings detected.

Particle 2 experiences a purely central, attractive, velocity-independent acceleration directed at the fixed particle 1, scaling linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with its own property p2 (p2 acts as inertia). The radial profile is the superposition of two field contributions: a long-range 2D-gravity term a = p1/(2π p2 r), as sourced by a point mass through the 2D Poisson equation, plus a short-range screened (Yukawa) term a = (p1/p2)·A·(1+r/λ)e^{−r/λ}/r² with screening length λ ≈ 0.6 and strength A ≈ 0.22, corresponding to a massive-mediator potential ∝ e^{−r/λ}/r. The screened term dominates inside r ≲ 1 and becomes negligible beyond r ≈ 3, where the force smoothly approaches pure logarithmic gravity.

Particle 2 obeys Newtonian-style dynamics (acceleration, no drag, no velocity dependence) under a purely central, attractive force from particle 1 whose magnitude scales as p1/p2 — so p1 acts as a gravitational "charge" of the source and p2 as the inertial mass of the probe. The dominant long-range part is the field of a 2D Poisson equation (logarithmic potential, force ∝ 1/(2πr)), augmented by a short-range Yukawa-type screened component whose gradient gives C·e^(−κr)(1+κr)/r² with screening length 1/κ ≈ 0.57, softened at the origin by a small core scale ε ≈ 0.046 (r² → r²+ε²). Finally there is a very weak linear restoring deficit (force term −λr, λ ≈ 1.3×10⁻⁴, like a tiny repulsive harmonic/cosmological background) that slightly reduces the attraction at large separations.

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed source particle at the origin by a central, velocity-independent force whose acceleration scales exactly as p1/p2 — p1 acts as a source "charge" while p2 acts as inertia. The radial profile is a superposition of two field contributions: a long-range 2D Coulomb (logarithmic-potential) term producing acceleration 1/(2πr), mildly suppressed at large distances by a Gaussian screening factor exp(−r²/λ²) with λ ≈ 90, plus a short-range massive (Yukawa-like) field term A(1+κr)e^(−κr)/r² with A ≈ 0.22 and screening mass κ ≈ 1.7. Both terms are softened at the core (r² → r² + ε², ε ≈ 0.035), so the force stays finite at the origin; the dynamics is otherwise standard Newtonian second-order motion with no velocity drag or time dependence.

The probe particle experiences a purely attractive central force directed toward the fixed source, whose magnitude follows the 2D Coulomb/gravity form (proportional to 1/r, as from a logarithmic potential) scaled by p1/p2 — the source property acts like charge/mass while the probe property acts as inertia in a way that divides the acceleration. On top of this, the coupling strength is spatially modulated by a separable square-lattice pattern, [1 + ½·cos(2πΔx/5)·cos(2πΔy/5)], as if the mediating field propagates through a periodic medium with wavelength 5 along each axis. The force is independent of the probe's velocity, and the measured constants are G = 1/(2π), modulation amplitude 1/2, and wavenumber k = 2π/5.

Particle 2 is always accelerated radially toward the fixed source particle at the origin, with a magnitude proportional to p1/p2 (source strength divided by probe inertia) and falling off as 1/r — a 2D-Coulomb-like attraction. On top of this, the coupling strength is spatially modulated by a static square standing-wave lattice cos(kx)·cos(ky) with wavelength ≈ 5 (k ≈ 2π/5), so the force is stronger or weaker depending on where the probe sits in the lattice, making the force central in direction but non-conservative (angular momentum is conserved, energy is not). The overall form is a ≈ -(p1/p2)·[1/(2π) + cos(kx)cos(ky)/(4π)]·r̂/r, verified by static acceleration maps across radii, off-axis positions, and extreme p1/p2 ratios.

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed source particle at the origin by a central force whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (consistent with a 2D logarithmic potential), scales linearly with the source property p1, and inversely with the probe's own property p2 (so p2 acts as inertia). On top of the baseline attraction, the coupling strength is spatially modulated by a static square-lattice field: the magnitude is proportional to A + B·cos(kx)·cos(ky) with lattice wavelength 2π/k = 5 and modulation depth B ≈ A/2, evaluated at the probe's instantaneous position. The force is always directed toward the source (no velocity dependence or drag was detected), and the modulation is a separable product in x and y — a sum form cos(kx)+cos(ky) was explicitly ruled out by off-axis measurements.

Particle 2 experiences a purely attractive, velocity-independent central force directed toward the fixed particle 1, with acceleration proportional to p1/p2 (so p1 acts as a source charge and p2 as inertia). The base magnitude falls off as 1/(2πr), the natural 2D Coulomb/gravity field of a point source, but it is modulated by a separable standing-wave lattice factor 1 + 0.5·cos(2πx/5)·cos(2πy/5), as if the mediating field interferes with a square checkerboard pattern of wavelength 5 fixed in space. The modulation wavelength and amplitude are independent of both particle properties and of the probe's velocity, and the force direction remains exactly radial despite the anisotropic strength modulation.

The probe particle experiences a purely radial, velocity-independent attraction toward the fixed source at the origin. The force magnitude follows a 2D logarithmic-potential form, |a| = (1/2π)(p1/p2)/r — note it scales with the source charge p1 and *inversely* with the probe's own property p2 — and is multiplied by a spatial lattice modulation 1 + 0.5·cos(2πx/5)·cos(2πy/5), as if the mediating field forms a square standing-wave interference pattern of wavelength 5 that locally strengthens or weakens the coupling. The direction always points exactly at the source; only the magnitude is modulated by the lattice.

Particle 2 obeys a Kepler-like law of motion: it is attracted toward the fixed source particle at the origin with acceleration magnitude a = (1/2π)·(p1/p2)/r², where r is the separation. The coupling depends only on the ratio of the two scalar properties — p1 acts as the source strength (gravitational charge) while p2 acts as the probe's inertia — and the constant 1/(2π) was verified to 7 significant figures by fitting. The force is exactly inverse-square (fitted exponent 2.000002), purely radial and velocity-independent, producing closed, non-precessing elliptical orbits.

The probe particle moves under a purely central, attractive force field sourced by the fixed particle at the origin, with acceleration magnitude G·p1/(p2·r²) — an inverse-square law like Newtonian gravity or Coulomb attraction, despite the 2D universe. The source property p1 acts as a "charge" that scales the field linearly, while the probe property p2 acts as inertial mass, dividing the acceleration; the coupling constant is very close to 1/(2π) ≈ 0.1592, suggesting the field obeys a Poisson-type equation with a 2π normalization. No velocity-dependent (drag or magnetic-like) forces were detected, and angular momentum is conserved, confirming the force is exactly radial.

Particle 2 moves under a central, attractive, inverse-square force field generated by the fixed source particle 1: its acceleration has magnitude G·p1/(p2·r²) with G ≈ 1/(2π), pointing toward the source. The source property p1 acts as a "charge" that scales the field linearly, while the probe's own property p2 acts purely as inertia — larger p2 means weaker acceleration, so unlike Newtonian gravity the equivalence principle is broken (p2 does not cancel). No velocity dependence, drag, or screening was detected; trajectories conserve energy consistent with a pure 1/r potential per unit p2.

The probe particle obeys Newtonian-style dynamics with a central attractive force directed toward the fixed source at the origin, with acceleration magnitude a = K·p1/(p2·(r²+ε²)), where K ≈ 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159 and ε ≈ 0.05 is a small softening length that regularizes the force at the core. The source property p1 acts as a gravitational "charge" (force ∝ p1), while p2 acts purely as inertial mass (acceleration ∝ 1/p2), and the force is independent of velocity — confirmed by circular orbits, hyperbolic escapes, and rest-start accelerations spanning r = 0.02 to 10. Trajectories that plunge through the tiny softened core gain energy reproducibly, indicating the world integrates this conservative force with a fixed-timestep (dt ≈ 0.01) leapfrog-type scheme rather than an exact solver, which my law replicates.

Particle 2 experiences a purely central, attractive force directed toward the fixed source particle at the origin, with magnitude proportional to 1/r² over all distances tested (r = 0.5 to 8) — i.e., a Kepler/Coulomb-type field with no screening or short-range softening. The acceleration scales linearly with the source property p1 but inversely with the probe's own property p2 (acceleration ∝ p1/p2), so p2 acts as inertial mass while p1 acts as gravitational charge, with universal coupling constant k ≈ 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159. The dynamics are conservative and velocity-independent: radial escapes conserve energy, and orbits (circular, eccentric, and close-approach) follow standard Keplerian behavior.

Particle 2 moves under an attractive central force sourced by the fixed particle 1, corresponding to the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian: a logarithmic potential whose force falls off as 1/r (not 1/r²), i.e. acceleration = (p1/(2π p2)) · (1/r) directed toward the source. The property p1 acts as the source "charge" while p2 acts as the probe's inertial mass, so acceleration scales as p1/p2; the force is velocity-independent, unscreened, and exact from r ≈ 0.5 out to r = 10. A signature of this law is that circular orbit speed √(p1/(2π p2)) is independent of radius, which I verified experimentally with sustained circular orbits at r = 4 and r = 7.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive central force from the fixed source at the origin whose magnitude falls off as 1/r — the field of a point source in a genuinely two-dimensional universe, corresponding to a logarithmic potential V(r) = (p1/2π)·ln(r). The acceleration is a = p1/(2π·p2·r) directed toward the source: it scales linearly with the source property p1, while the probe property p2 acts purely as inertial mass (force is independent of p2, so acceleration scales as 1/p2). The force is conservative and velocity-independent; a signature of the log potential confirmed experimentally is that circular orbital speed √(p1/(2π·p2)) is independent of orbital radius, like a flat galactic rotation curve.

The probe particle moves under a purely central, attractive force sourced by particle 1 at the origin, with acceleration magnitude K·p1/(p2·r) where K ≈ 1/(2π) — i.e., the potential is logarithmic, exactly the Green's function of the Poisson equation in two dimensions (like the field of a charged line). The source property p1 acts as a charge that the force is linear in, while the probe property p2 acts as an inertial mass dividing the force; the trajectories conserve energy E = p2·v²/2 + K·p1·ln(r) and angular momentum. I found no velocity dependence, no screening length, and the 1/r force law holds unmodified from r ≈ 0.3 out to r > 20.

The source particle generates a static 2D logarithmic potential — the Green's function of the two-dimensional Laplacian, φ(r) = -(p1/2π) ln r — which is the natural analogue of Newtonian gravity in a 2D universe. The probe particle experiences a purely central, attractive acceleration of magnitude a = p1/(2π p2 r), directed toward the source, with no velocity dependence, no screening at large distance, and no softening at small distance. Notably, the probe's property p2 appears inversely in the acceleration (heavier probes accelerate less), implying the force couples linearly to both p1 and p2 while the inertial mass scales as p2², a violation of the usual equivalence principle.

Particle 2 experiences a purely central, attractive, velocity-independent acceleration directed toward the fixed source particle, with magnitude (p1/(2π p2)) / sqrt(r² + ε²) where ε ≈ 0.05.
