At large separations this reduces to the 1/r force of a two-dimensional logarithmic gravitational potential (the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian sourced by p1, with coupling constant 1/2π), while at short range the force saturates at a finite value G·p1/(p2·ε) rather than diverging, indicating a softened/regularised core. The probe's property p2 acts as an inertial mass (acceleration ∝ 1/p2), the source property p1 acts as a gravitational charge (acceleration ∝ p1), and angular momentum and energy are conserved, consistent with conservative central-force dynamics.

The particles interact via a 2D logarithmic (Coulomb-in-2D) field: each particle sources an identical potential whose gradient gives an attractive pairwise force of magnitude k/r with k ≈ 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159, independent of distance scaling other than 1/r and with equal coupling strength for all particles. The structural feature is in the inertia: the ten particles fall into three mass groups — particles 0–3 have unit mass, particles 4–6 have mass ≈ 2, and particles 7–9 have mass ≈ 4 — so identical forces produce group-dependent accelerations. Newtonian dynamics (a = F/m) with this force law reproduced all observed trajectories, including long multi-body runs with nonzero initial velocities, down to the position-noise floor.

The particles interact through a two-dimensional logarithmic potential field (the 2D Coulomb/gravity analogue): every pair attracts with a force whose magnitude falls off as 1/r, directed along the line joining them, with universal coupling G/(2π) ≈ 1/(2π). All particles carry equal coupling charge, but they fall into three inertial-mass groups — particles 0–3 have mass 1, particles 4–6 have mass ≈2, and particles 7–9 have mass ≈4 — so identical forces produce group-dependent accelerations. The dynamics are purely Newtonian second-order with no velocity dependence (no drag), confirmed by moving-particle experiments that showed no damping or velocity-coupled forces.

The particles interact via an attractive central force whose magnitude between any pair is G/r with G ≈ 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159 — exactly the force from a logarithmic potential, i.e. each particle sources a true 2D Poisson (gravitational) field with unit charge regardless of its mass. The particles have three inertial mass classes (particles 0–3 have m=1, 4–6 have m=2, 7–9 have m=4), so a particle's acceleration is the summed pairwise force divided by its own mass; notably, the source strength does not scale with mass, breaking the equivalence principle. The distance exponent was verified to be exactly 1 via ring-scaling experiments (R = 1.5, 3, 6) and free-parameter fitting (p = 1.0002), and the model reproduces all diverse trajectories down to the observation-noise floor.

The particles obey a 2D "logarithmic gravity": every particle sources an attractive field with identical unit charge, generating a pairwise force of magnitude G/r (the Green's-function form of the 2D Poisson equation, with G ≈ 1/(2π) suggesting the potential is φ = ln r with unit coupling). The particles differ only in inertial mass, organized in three groups — particles 0–3 have mass 1, particles 4–6 mass 2, and particles 7–9 mass 4 — so acceleration of particle i is (G/m_i)Σ_j r̂_ij/r_ij. Fitting confirmed the force exponent is exactly 1 (not inverse-square), the source strengths are equal across all masses (equivalence-principle violation: gravitational charge is uniform while inertia varies), and there is no drag, retardation, or velocity-dependent coupling.

The particles interact through an attractive two-dimensional logarithmic-potential field (the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian): each particle sources the field with equal unit strength, producing an acceleration field of magnitude κ/√(r²+ε²) with κ ≈ 1/(2π) and a small softening length ε ≈ 0.05 that regularizes close encounters; at large separation the force falls off as 1/r exactly. The key structural feature is that while all particles source the field identically, they respond with different susceptibilities (effectively different inertial masses): particles 0–3 respond with coefficient 1, particles 4–6 with 1/2, and particles 7–9 with 1/4. Newton's third law is therefore broken in acceleration space — a light-response particle paired with a full-response one accelerates 4× less — and the equations of motion are a_i = c_i κ Σ_j r̂_ij/√(r_ij²+ε²).

The universe contains a single field source — the huge-inertia anchor at the centre — which generates a purely central, mass-independent acceleration field a(r) = B·r − A/r with A ≈ 8 and B ≈ 0.05: an attractive 2D logarithmic-potential term (force ∝ 1/r, as from a point source of the 2D Laplace equation) combined with a weak linear outward "anti-spring" term, making r ≈ √(A/B) ≈ 12.6 an unstable equilibrium radius inside which orbits are bound and outside which particles escape. All particles (orbiters and probes) obey the equivalence principle — their accelerations do not depend on their own masses — and neither the ring orbiters nor the probes source any field themselves: tests with very heavy (m = 100) probes placed close to test particles produced no detectable pairwise force, and trajectories pass smoothly through the orbiter ring; energy v²/2 + A·ln r − (B/2)r² is conserved along all trajectories.

The universe contains a single static central field sourced by the massive anchor at the origin: every other particle feels a radial acceleration a(r) = 8/r − 0.05·r, i.e. a logarithmic-potential (2D-gravity-like) attraction combined with a linear repulsive "dark energy" term that dominates beyond the equilibrium radius r ≈ 12.65 and drives distant particles into exponential escape. The acceleration is completely independent of the test particle's mass (equivalence principle holds), and particles exert no measurable forces on each other — even a mass-10000 probe at 0.6 separation produced no deflection of a neighbour — so the orbiters and probes are all non-interacting test particles orbiting in the anchor's field, with the ring orbiters on circular orbits at speed √(8 − 0.05r²).

This universe contains a single static central field sourced only by the heavy anchor at the origin: every particle feels a purely radial acceleration a(r) = B·r − C/r (B ≈ 0.0500, C ≈ 7.96), i.e. a 2D-gravity-like logarithmic attractive potential C·ln(r) combined with an inverted-harmonic outward term −½B·r². The coupling is universal — acceleration is completely independent of a particle's inertial mass, and particles (including the ring orbiters and even very heavy probes) exert no forces on one another; the motion is conservative with no velocity dependence or drag. The two terms balance at an unstable equilibrium radius r = √(C/B) ≈ 12.6: inside it particles are bound on rosette-like orbits around the anchor, while outside it they are expelled with asymptotically exponential radial growth at rate √B.

This universe contains a single static central field anchored at the massive central particle: every other particle feels a purely radial acceleration a(r) = −A/r + B·r with A ≈ 8.0 and B ≈ 0.05, i.e. a 2D logarithmic (1/r) attraction toward the anchor combined with a linear outward "anti-spring" term, giving an equilibrium circle at r = √(A/B) ≈ 12.65 where the field vanishes. The acceleration is completely independent of the test particle's inertial mass (an equivalence-principle-like coupling), and there are no particle–particle interactions at all — probes do not attract each other regardless of mass or separation, and the ring orbiters do not source any field. The ring orbiters simply execute circular orbits in this central field (v² = A − B·r² at r = 5), energy is conserved, and the dynamics show no velocity-dependent or rotational (magnetic-like) forces.

This universe contains a single static central force field sourced by the immobile anchor at the origin: every particle feels a radial acceleration a(r) = k·r − C/r (with k ≈ 0.05 and C ≈ 8), i.e., a 2D logarithmic attractive potential combined with an inverted-harmonic (linear repulsive) term that dominates beyond r = √(C/k) ≈ 12.6, making distant orbits unstable and divergent. The acceleration is completely independent of a particle's inertial mass (equivalence-principle-like behavior), and particles exert no forces on each other — the ring orbiters and probes do not source any field, as verified by clustered heavy probes and mirror-symmetry tests. The dynamics are purely positional (no drag, no magnetic/Coriolis terms), conserving energy and angular momentum, with the ring orbiters sitting on circular orbits at exactly the speed v = √(C − k·r²) predicted by this law.

This universe contains a single central field sourced by the anchor at the origin: every particle, regardless of its inertial mass, experiences a radial acceleration a(r,t) = 0.05·e^(0.05t)·r − 8/r directed along the line to the anchor — an attractive component falling off as 1/r (a 2D logarithmic-potential gravity) plus an outward harmonic ("dark-energy"-like) term whose coupling grows exponentially in time, like an inflating cosmological constant. There are no particle–particle interactions: the ring orbiters and probes move independently in this shared field, with a static equilibrium radius near r ≈ √(8/0.05) ≈ 12.6 at t = 0 that shrinks as the repulsion inflates, eventually flinging all distant matter outward exponentially. Probe mass, velocity-dependent forces, and orbiter influence were all tested and found absent.

The universe is governed by a single central field generated by the huge-inertia anchor at the origin: every particle, regardless of its own mass or velocity, feels a radial acceleration a(r,t) = c0·(1+beta·t)·r − k/r directed along the line to the anchor, with k ≈ 8.0, c0 ≈ 0.05 and beta ≈ 0.06. This is an attractive 2D-Coulomb (1/r) pull combined with an outward anti-harmonic (linear-in-r) "expansion" term whose coupling strengthens linearly with time, so an unstable equilibrium ring exists near r ≈ 12.6 at t = 0 and slowly shrinks, driving everything to eventually fly outward. Probe–probe and orbiter–probe interactions are negligible — the dynamics are entirely a one-body problem in the anchor's time-growing field.

This universe contains a single dominant central source (the anchor) that generates a purely central, test-mass-independent force field; there are no detectable particle–particle interactions even between very massive probes placed close together. The radial acceleration on every particle is a(r,t) = −C/r (a logarithmic-potential attraction, much shallower than Newtonian 1/r²) plus a repulsive "anti-spring" term k(t)·r whose stiffness grows quadratically in time, k(t) = k₀(1+αt)², acting like an accelerating cosmological expansion. This creates an unstable equilibrium radius (initially r≈12.6) that shrinks over time: inner particles orbit boundedly on rosette/circular orbits while outer particles are swept outward at ever-increasing rates, which exactly reproduces both the ring orbiters' circular speeds and the runaway probe trajectories.

This universe contains a single massive anchor at the origin that sources a central force field; all other particles (orbiters and probes) are pure test particles whose acceleration is independent of their own mass, and they exert no detectable forces on each other. The radial acceleration is a(r) = α·r − β/r with α ≈ 0.0501 and β ≈ 7.96: a 2D logarithmic-potential gravitational attraction (force ∝ 1/r, as from a point source of the 2D Laplace equation) superposed with a linear outward "anti-harmonic" term, giving an equilibrium radius near r ≈ 12.6 and dynamically stable circular orbits only inside r ≈ 8.9. The dynamics are conservative and exactly central — angular momentum and the energy E = v²/2 − αr²/2 + β·ln r are conserved, with no drag or velocity-dependent forces.

All particles (regardless of their inertial mass) experience a purely central acceleration directed along their position vector from the anchor at the origin: an attractive component of magnitude A/r (corresponding to a 2D logarithmic gravitational potential, A ≈ 8) plus a repulsive component B·e^(kt)·r — a linear "cosmological expansion" term whose coupling grows exponentially in time (B ≈ 0.05, k ≈ 0.05). There are no particle–particle interactions: the ring orbiters and other probes exert no measurable force on a probe, the force is independent of particle mass and velocity, and the time-growing repulsion causes initially bound orbits to spiral outward and eventually escape exponentially.

All 11 particles interact through a pairwise, conservative, attractive central force whose acceleration magnitude is G/d^1.5 (G ≈ 6.4 per particle), i.e. a power law between Coulomb gravity (1/d²) and a constant force, corresponding to the potential φ(d) = −2G/√d. This was established by circular-orbit tests: the required tangential speed satisfied v²·√R = 32.5 to within 0.3% across radii 3–15 (32.5 = 5.06·G, where 5.06 is the geometric sum over the symmetric 11-particle ring), and eccentric orbits matched the predicted periapsis/apoapsis from energy and angular-momentum conservation in this potential. Rings released at rest undergo singular collapse through the centre, where the simulator's integrator injects energy and the particles scatter outward chaotically — a numerical artifact rather than a feature of the law.

The system consists of 11 equal particles interacting through a pairwise attractive central force that is the sum of three inverse power laws: f(r) = g1/r + g2/r² + g3/r³ (with g1≈1.37, g2≈5.9, g3≈4.0), i.e., a Newtonian 1/r² gravity term augmented by a long-range 1/r (logarithmic-potential) component and a short-range 1/r³ component. The force is velocity-independent and the dynamics are standard Newtonian (acceleration equals the vector sum of pairwise forces), verified by exact circular-orbit predictions at several radii and by the radial collapse acceleration profile, whose effective power-law exponent smoothly drifts from ≈1.8 at small separations to ≈1.2 at large ones. A ring released from rest collapses through the centre and slingshots outward, with the late-time coasting speed matching the energy integral of this three-term potential.

All 11 particles interact through a pairwise attractive central force whose magnitude is the sum of two power laws: F(d) = A/d + B/d^p, with A ≈ 1.43, B ≈ 9.0, and p ≈ 2.2. The 1/d term corresponds to a 2D logarithmic potential and dominates at large separations (it makes the circular-orbit speed nearly independent of radius and decelerates escaping particles like 1/r forever, giving asymptotically constant escape speeds), while the steeper B/d^2.2 term dominates at short range and drives the violent collapse-and-slingshot behaviour of rings released from rest. No velocity dependence, screening, or non-central component was detected — the force is conservative, central, and acts identically between all particle pairs including the central one.

All 11 particles (centre included, with identical coupling) interact through a purely attractive, velocity-independent pairwise central force whose acceleration magnitude is the sum of two power laws in separation d: a steep near-Newtonian term A/d^2.22 (A ≈ 9.2) plus a long-range logarithmic-potential tail B/d (B ≈ 1.45). This was established by measuring initial collapse accelerations of static rings over radii 2–15 (the net inward acceleration shows a smoothly varying log-slope incompatible with any single power law but fit to ~0.3% by the two-term form), and confirmed by stable circular orbits at the predicted speeds and by the slow 1/d deceleration of particles escaping to distances of hundreds of units. There is no drag, no velocity coupling, and no distinguished central particle — the dynamics are simple pairwise gravity-like attraction with this modified two-term radial profile.

The universe contains a 2D Coulomb-like field obeying a 2D Poisson equation: every particle sources the field with identical unit strength, producing a pairwise force that falls off as 1/r (logarithmic potential), confirmed by Gauss-law tests with concentric rings (zero field inside a uniform ring, exterior field equivalent to a point source at the center). Particles come in two species: particles 0–14 couple with positive sign (accelerated toward sources) while particles 15–29 couple with negative sign (accelerated away from all sources), with a common coupling constant C ≈ 0.159 ≈ 1/(2π). Forces are purely positional — rotating-ring experiments showed no velocity-dependent (magnetic or drag) terms, and accelerations superpose linearly over all sources.

All 30 particles act as equal-strength sources of a 2D logarithmic potential, so each source exerts a force whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (like 2D gravity), with coupling constant G ≈ 1/(2π). The structural twist is in how particles respond: indices 0–14 carry a positive response charge and accelerate toward the net field gradient (attraction), while indices 15–29 carry a negative response charge and accelerate in the opposite direction (repulsion), even though all particles source the field with the same positive sign. There is no velocity dependence or screening; the dynamics are a pure second-order pairwise 1/r force law with this index-based sign asymmetry.

All 30 particles source an identical 2D logarithmic potential (force magnitude falling as 1/r, slightly softened at short range as r/(r²+ε²) with ε≈0.03), with coupling constant c≈0.159≈1/(2π). The interaction is non-reciprocal: particles 0–14 are universally attracted to every other particle, while particles 15–29 are universally repelled, so momentum is not conserved (e.g. an attracted/repelled pair perpetually chases itself). I verified the 1/r form via Gauss's-law shielding inside a uniform ring, confirmed no velocity dependence or drag, and that the sign depends only on the receiving particle's fixed index, not on local density or geometry.

Every particle is an identical, unsigned source of a 2D logarithmic ("2D gravity"/Coulomb) field: the field from each source decays as 1/d with coupling 1/(2π), with a short-range Plummer softening of scale ε ≈ 0.033 that regularises close encounters. The asymmetry lies entirely in the response: particles with indices 0–14 carry response charge +1 and are attracted toward every other particle, while particles 15–29 carry response charge −1 and are repelled by every other particle — i.e. the acceleration of particle i is a_i = s_i · (1/2π) Σ_j (r_j − r_i)/(|r_j − r_i|² + ε²). This violates Newton's third law (a "+" and a "−" particle both feel forces in directions set only by their own sign), which I verified through ring, line, blob-drift and pair experiments that all quantitatively match this single law.

All 30 particles source a 2D Coulomb (logarithmic) potential with identical unit charge, so each pair interacts with force magnitude k/r along the line connecting them, with k ≈ 1/(2π) — the natural Green's-function normalization for the 2D Laplacian. The coupling is non-reciprocal and depends only on the receiving particle's species: particles 0–14 are universally attracted toward all other particles (they fall down the field gradient), while particles 15–29 are universally repelled (they climb the gradient), so an A–B pair chases/flees in the same direction. I verified there is no drag, no velocity dependence, exact linear superposition, and no screening — the pure 1/r force holds from r ≈ 0.7 out to r = 20.

The probe particle experiences a central force directed toward the fixed source at the origin, with magnitude C·(p1/p2)·cos(ωt)/r — i.e. a 2D Coulomb/logarithmic-potential field (1/r falloff rather than 1/r²) whose coupling oscillates uniformly across all of space with angular frequency ω = π/2 (period 4) and amplitude constant C ≈ 0.796 ≈ 2.5/π. The acceleration is proportional to the source property p1 and inversely proportional to the probe property p2 (which acts like inertia), with no velocity dependence and no retardation: the field's temporal phase is globally synchronized, so the force at all radii flips sign simultaneously, alternating between attraction and repulsion. A small secular ponderomotive drift observed in nearly stationary probes is fully explained as the second-order effect of this oscillating 1/r force and required no additional terms.

The fixed source particle at the origin produces an attractive central force on the probe whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (the gradient of a 2D logarithmic potential) and whose strength oscillates globally in time as cos(ωt) with ω ≈ π/2, with the same phase at every distance — i.e., no retardation or wave propagation. The coupling is proportional to the source property p1 and inversely proportional to the probe property p2, so the probe's acceleration is a = -K(p1/p2)cos(ωt) r̂ / r with K ≈ 0.79. The probe is otherwise a free Newtonian particle: when the force time-averages to zero over each period, the probe drifts ballistically with small oscillatory perturbations superimposed.

The probe particle experiences a central force directed toward the fixed source whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (not 1/r²), is proportional to the source property p1, inversely proportional to the probe's own property p2 (so it acts like a charge-to-mass ratio), and — crucially — oscillates coherently in time as cos(πt/2). The oscillation has angular frequency ω = π/2 (period 4) with no spatial phase lag, meaning the entire field pulses in unison rather than propagating as a wave; over each period the net impulse nearly cancels, leaving only a small second-order (ponderomotive) outward drift that I verified quantitatively. The acceleration law is a₂ = −0.8·(p1/p2)·cos(πt/2)·r̂/r, which reproduced every measured trajectory down to the observation-noise floor.

The source particle at the origin generates a central force field with 2D-Coulomb (logarithmic potential) geometry — force magnitude falls off as 1/r — but the coupling oscillates globally in time as cos(ωt) with ω = π/2 (period 4), with no spatial phase lag, i.e. a standing temporal oscillation rather than a traveling wave. The probe's acceleration is a = -K·(p1/p2)·cos(ωt)·r̂/r with K ≈ 0.8: linear in the source property p1, inversely proportional to the probe property p2 (which acts as an inertial mass), and alternating between attraction and repulsion. Because the force amplitude depends on r, the oscillation produces a measurable second-order ponderomotive drift pushing the probe slowly outward, which the data quantitatively confirms.

The source particle at the origin produces a radially directed force field whose magnitude decays as 1/r (not 1/r² as in our universe) and whose sign oscillates uniformly in time as cos(ωt) with ω = π/2, so the field is alternately attractive and antiattractive everywhere simultaneously (no propagation delay or spatial phase). The probe's acceleration is a = −0.8·(p1/p2)·cos(πt/2)·r̂/r: it couples linearly to the source property p1 and inversely to its own property p2, which acts like an inertial mass. The oscillation produces near-periodic radial breathing of the probe about its starting radius, plus a slow secular (ponderomotive-like) outward drift arising from the field gradient over each oscillation cycle.

Particle 1 sources a static, velocity-independent central field that attracts the probe with acceleration proportional to p1 (source strength) and inversely proportional to p2 (which acts as inertial mass): a = (p1/p2)·g(r) directed toward the origin. The radial profile g(r) is piecewise: inside a sharp shell at r ≈ 0.75 the field behaves like a 2D Coulomb field, g·r ≈ 5.95 constant (with a small exponential deficit at very short range), while outside the shell it abruptly drops to a much weaker mixed law g ≈ 0.137/r + 0.80/r² + 0.57/r⁶, combining a logarithmic-potential (2D gravity) term, a 3D-Newtonian term, and a short-range van-der-Waals-like correction. The transition between the two regimes is very sharp (logistic width ≈ 0.03), as if the source carried a compensating screening shell at fixed radius R ≈ 0.754.

The probe particle experiences a purely radial, velocity-independent attraction toward the fixed source at the origin, with acceleration scaling as p1/p2 (source charge over probe inertia). The force law has two regimes: inside a sharp boundary at r ≈ 0.76 it behaves like a 2D logarithmic-potential field with acceleration A/r (A ≈ 5.94), while outside it is a superposition of a Newtonian inverse-square term (≈0.78/r²), a weaker 2D Coulomb term (≈0.14/r), and a steep short-range 1/r⁶ contribution (≈0.6/r⁶). The crossover between regimes is very sharp (width ≈ 0.02), effectively a piecewise law, and its location does not depend on the particle properties.

Particle 2 experiences a purely radial, velocity-independent attraction toward the fixed source, with acceleration proportional to the source property p1 and inversely proportional to its own property p2 (so the force itself is independent of the probe's property). The field has two distinct zones: within a screening radius r0 ≈ 0.74 it follows a 2D-Coulomb (logarithmic potential) form g ≈ 5.95/r, softened at very small radii by a factor (1 − e^{−r/0.082}) so the force vanishes at the origin; beyond r0 the field rapidly crosses over (a sharp, stretched-exponential transition of width ~0.1) to a weaker outer field g = 0.83/r² + 0.139/r, i.e. a Newtonian inverse-square term plus a long-range 1/r correction. This structure resembles a massive mediator confined inside a screening bubble, with conventional gravity plus a 2D logarithmic tail dominating outside.

The probe particle experiences a purely central, attractive, velocity-independent force directed toward the fixed source, with magnitude proportional to p1 (source charge) and acceleration inversely proportional to p2 (which acts as inertial mass), so acceleration scales exactly as p1/p2. The radial field profile is g(r) = A/(r·ln(r/r0)) at large radii — a 2D-gravity-like 1/r force further suppressed by a logarithmic screening factor — which is capped at a maximum strength Fmax/r below a critical radius r ≈ 0.74 where the log denominator would diverge, and softened by a Plummer-like factor r²/(r²+s²) deep in the core so the force vanishes linearly at the origin. Angular momentum is conserved (no drag or tangential forces), and the dynamics depend only on the ratio p1/p2, not on the individual values.

The source particle at the origin generates a static, velocity-independent central field that attracts the probe with acceleration proportional to p1/p2 (the source property strengthens the force; the probe property acts like inertia). In the far field the acceleration magnitude is a sum of a Newtonian inverse-square term (~0.81/r²) and a long-range 1/r tail (~0.14/r), the latter resembling the field of a logarithmic potential. Inside a core radius of about r0 ≈ 0.72 the field crosses over to a much stronger pure 1/r law (~5.9/r); the crossover is smooth and well described by a power-law blending weight (r0/r)^6.6 capped at 1, so the inner region is exactly 1/r and the transition is fairly sharp.

The particles obey two-dimensional Newtonian gravity: every particle sources a logarithmic potential whose field gives a purely attractive, central acceleration of magnitude Gm_source/r (a 1/r force law rather than the 3D 1/r²). The acceleration a particle feels is the linear superposition of contributions from all other particles and depends only on the source masses — receivers of different mass fall identically (equivalence principle) — with no velocity dependence and no short-range softening. The population has two mass species: particles 0, 1, 2 have source strength Gm ≈ 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159, and particles 3, 4, 5 are exactly three times stronger, Gm ≈ 3/(2π) ≈ 0.477, suggesting unit and triple masses coupled through the 2D Green's function normalization 1/(2π).

This universe obeys two-dimensional Newtonian gravity: each particle sources a logarithmic potential (the Green's function of the 2D Poisson equation), so every pair attracts with a force that falls off as 1/r rather than 1/r². The acceleration of particle i is a_i = Σ_j G·m_j·û_ij/r_ij, independent of its own mass (equivalence principle), with no softening, drag, or velocity dependence. The six particles split into two mass groups — particles 0–2 are light (G·m ≈ 0.159 ≈ 1/2π) and particles 3–5 are three times heavier (G·m ≈ 0.477 ≈ 3/2π) — consistent with unit and triple masses coupled through G = 1/(2π).

The system is governed by two-dimensional Newtonian gravity: each particle sources a logarithmic potential (the Green's function of the 2D Poisson equation), so every pair attracts with a force proportional to m_j/r rather than m_j/r². The coupling constant is G ≈ 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159, exactly the prefactor of the 2D Poisson Green's function, and accelerations depend only on the source's mass (equivalence principle holds). The six particles split into two species: particles 0–2 carry unit mass while particles 3–5 carry exactly three times that mass, and there is no velocity dependence or short-range softening in the force law.

The six particles interact through true two-dimensional Newtonian gravity: each particle sources a logarithmic potential phi = -G m ln(r) (the Green's function of the 2D Poisson equation), so the attractive acceleration on any particle from a source of mass m_j falls off as G m_j / r — exactly 1/r with no softening at small r (tested down to r=0.25) and no screening at large r (tested out to r=50 and r≈2800). The coupling is G ≈ 1/(2π); particles 0–2 carry unit mass while particles 3–5 carry mass ≈ 3, but acceleration depends only on the source mass (equivalence principle holds), forces superpose linearly across all pairs, and there is no velocity dependence in the force.

The six particles interact through two-dimensional Newtonian gravity, i.e. a logarithmic pair potential whose force falls off as 1/r rather than 1/r². The acceleration a particle feels from neighbour j is attractive, directed along the separation vector, with magnitude k_j/r that depends only on the source's mass — the equivalence principle holds and there is no velocity dependence or softening. Particles split into two mass classes: particles 0–2 have coupling ≈ 1/(2π) and particles 3–5 have coupling ≈ 3/(2π), consistent with masses {1,1,1,3,3,3} under a gravitational constant G = 1/(2π) (the natural 2D Green's-function normalisation of ∇²φ = ρ).

The 30 background particles divide into three equal populations by index: indices 0–9 carry a weak positive source charge (g≈+0.15), indices 10–19 a strong positive charge (g≈+0.55), and indices 20–29 a negative charge (g≈−0.33). Each charged particle generates a central force field whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (the gradient of a 2D logarithmic potential, with a tiny short-range softening), attractive for positive charges and repulsive for negative ones; every particle — background or probe — accelerates in this summed field with identical, velocity-independent coupling and unit mass. The five probe particles are pure test particles: they feel the field of the background population but contribute no field of their own, so probe trajectories are determined by integrating the collective softened-1/r field of the self-interacting three-class background.

This universe contains 30 background particles that act as the only sources of a 2D Coulomb-like field: each source j exerts a central force of magnitude q_j/r (the field of a 2D logarithmic potential) on every other particle, directed toward the source if q_j is positive and away if negative. The coupling is non-reciprocal and receiver-independent — a particle's acceleration is simply the sum of q_j/r contributions from all sources, regardless of the particle's own charge, and the 5 probe particles generate no field at all. The background charges are structured by index into three equal groups: particles 0–9 carry a weak positive charge (≈0.16), particles 10–19 a strong positive charge (≈0.52), and particles 20–29 a negative charge (≈−0.29), so distant probes feel a strongly cancelled net field while near-field forces follow a clean unsoftened 1/r law.

This universe contains 30 background source particles that each generate a 2D "logarithmic-gravity" field: the acceleration imparted on any particle by source j is q_j/r directed toward the source (force ∝ 1/r, the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian), with no dependence on the receiving particle's own properties or velocity. The sources fall into three groups of ten with distinct coupling strengths — roughly +0.14, +0.44, and −0.28 — so two-thirds of the population attracts while one-third repels, producing asymmetric (non-reciprocal-looking) pair dynamics and a net far-field monopole of strength ≈3.2 that supports flat-rotation-curve circular orbits (v ≈ 1.8 at any radius). The 5 probes are passive, chargeless tracers: they feel the background's field but exert no forces themselves, and the background evolves self-consistently from rest under its own mutual log-potential interactions.

The 30 background particles are point sources of a 2D Coulomb field (logarithmic potential), each producing a radial field of magnitude q/r. Their charges are quantized in units of approximately 1/(2π) and come in three groups by particle index: the first ten carry −1 unit, the next ten −3 units, and the last ten +2 units. Every particle — background or probe — responds identically to the local field (acceleration equals the field, with no dependence on its own charge, mass, or velocity), which makes the dynamics non-reciprocal: oppositely charged pairs chase one another, while the neutral probes are pure test particles that generate no field of their own.

The 30 background particles are sources of a two-dimensional Poisson (logarithmic-potential) field: each carries a quantized charge, with the first ten at strength +1, the next ten at +3, and the last ten at −2, in units of approximately 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159. Every particle — background or neutral probe — accelerates with the local field itself, a = Σⱼ sⱼ (rⱼ − r)/|rⱼ − r|², i.e. a 1/r force law (positive charges attract, negative repel) with no dependence on the test particle's velocity or identity, while probes generate no field of their own. A small short-range softening length (ε ≈ 0.03) regularizes the force at sub-0.1 separations, and reported positions carry modest Gaussian observation noise.

The source particle generates a screened (Yukawa-type) scalar field obeying the 2D screened-Poisson equation, whose Green's function is φ(r) = (p1/2π)·K0(r/λ) with screening length λ = 2. The probe particle feels an attractive central force F = (p1·p2'? no —) of magnitude (p1/4π)·K1(r/2), and its acceleration is this force divided by p2, i.e. a = (p1/p2)·(1/4π)·K1(r/2) directed toward the source — so p2 acts as an inertial mass while only p1 acts as charge. The force is velocity-independent, isotropic, and the constants were verified to high precision (prefactor exactly 1/4π, λ exactly 2) across distances 0.5–8 and property values 0.1–10.

Particle 1 sources a static 2D screened scalar field (massive/Yukawa field obeying the screened Poisson equation), whose point-source potential is proportional to the modified Bessel function K0(m·r) with screening mass m ≈ 0.5. Particle 2 couples to the field gradient, experiencing a purely attractive central acceleration of magnitude (p1/p2)·(m/2π)·K1(m·r) — at short range it behaves like an unscreened 2D Coulomb force (∝ 1/r) and at long range it decays exponentially as e^(−mr)/√r. The acceleration is linear in the source property p1, inversely proportional to the probe property p2 (p2 acts as inertial mass while the force coupling is p1-only in the acceleration ratio), and is independent of the probe's velocity.

The source particle generates a static screened scalar field in 2D — the Green's function of a massive (Klein–Gordon/Yukawa) field equation — giving an attractive potential proportional to K₀(r/λ) with screening length λ = 2, so the force falls off exponentially beyond r ≈ 2 rather than as a power law. The probe's acceleration is a = −(p1/p2)·K₁(r/λ)/(4πλ)·r̂: the field strength couples linearly to the source charge p1, while the probe's response scales inversely with its own property p2, implying the coupling charge is p1·p2 but the inertial mass grows as p2². The force is purely central, conservative, and velocity-independent — moving-probe scattering trajectories were fully reproduced by this static field law with constants λ = 2.00 and prefactor 1/(4π).

Particle 1 sources a static screened scalar field obeying the 2D Klein-Gordon/Helmholtz equation (-∇² + μ²)Φ = p1·δ²(x), whose Green's function is the modified Bessel potential Φ(r) = -(p1/2π)·K0(μr) with screening length 1/μ = 2. Particle 2 couples to this field with charge p2 equal to its inertial mass, so its acceleration is a purely attractive central force a = (p1/p2)·(1/4π)·K1(r/2) directed toward the source — proportional to the source strength p1, inversely proportional to the probe property p2, and exponentially suppressed at large separations. The force is conservative and velocity-independent: no drag or magnetic-type deflection was detected, and trajectories (radial falls, bound orbits, and fast flybys) all match this single screened-Yukawa law within observation noise.

Particle 1 sources a static 2D screened scalar field (a massive/Klein-Gordon field satisfying ∇²φ − φ/λ² = −source with screening length λ = 2), whose Green's function in 2D is the modified Bessel function K₀(r/λ). The probe particle feels a purely attractive central acceleration of magnitude (1/4π)·(p1/p2)·K₁(r/2) directed toward the source — proportional to the source charge p1 but inversely proportional to the probe property p2, indicating the probe's inertia grows faster than its coupling. The force is velocity-independent (no drag), conservative, and was verified across separations r = 0.25–10, where it exhibits the characteristic K₁ behavior: a ~1/r divergence at short range and exponential e^(−r/2)/√r decay at long range.

The discovered system is a rotating multi-body configuration consisting of a central particle surrounded by 10 ring particles equally spaced at a fixed radius. All particles maintain constant x-y positions while experiencing uniform z-direction acceleration (perpendicular to the rotation plane). This acceleration arises from the centrifugal effects of the rotating mass distribution and is proportional to v_tan²/R, where v_tan is the initial tangential velocity of the ring and R is the ring radius. The ring particles are dynamically stabilized by rotation, with the centrifugal acceleration coefficient k_cf ≈ 0.487 determined through ODE integration using RK45 solver.

The discovered system is a **rotationally symmetric configuration of 11 particles** where one central particle remains fixed and 10 particles form a rigid ring that undergoes collective radial oscillation while rotating. The physics is governed by **angular momentum conservation** combined with a **linear repulsive force** proportional to 1/R between particles. As the ring contracts under centrifugal forces, particles repel each other with increasing strength, eventually reversing the collapse and driving ballistic expansion. This creates the characteristic collapse-then-escape dynamics observed, where the system exhibits periodic or quasi-periodic radial motion with conserved angular momentum.

The discovered system involves 11 particles where the centre particle attracts the 10 ring particles through an inverse-square force law (F ∝ -1/r²), similar to gravitational or Coulomb interactions. Additionally, a velocity-dependent damping force (F ∝ -β·v) causes tangential velocities to decay exponentially while the radial infall continues. The centre particle remains fixed at the origin throughout, serving as the source of the attractive field.

This universe implements a system of 11 independent 2D harmonic oscillators, where each particle (centre + 10 ring particles) experiences a linear restoring force F = -k*r proportional to its distance from the origin. The system exhibits classical harmonic motion with k ≈ 1.857: particles with zero initial velocity spiral radially inward before reversing, while particles with initial angular momentum trace elliptical orbits. The centre particle remains at the equilibrium point (origin), and all particles evolve independently without inter-particle interactions.

The discovered system is a Newtonian gravitational N-body problem with 11 particles: one central mass and 10 particles arranged symmetrically on a ring. The central mass exerts inverse-square gravitational attraction (F ∝ 1/r²) on the ring particles, while all particles repel each other via a weak, short-range cubic power law (F ∝ 1/r³). This repulsion prevents particle overlap and maintains the ring structure. The system exhibits Keplerian orbital mechanics: when tangential velocity matches v = √(GM/r), the ring particles maintain stable circular orbits; lower velocities cause collapse toward the centre, while higher velocities lead to outward expansion and escape. The fitted parameters (GM ≈ 2.0, repulsion ≈ 0.1, power ≈ 3.0) fully characterize the dynamics.

Particle 2 is governed by a central inverse-square attractive force law of the form F = -G·p1/r², where r is the distance to particle 1. This force acts radially inward and causes particle 2 to accelerate toward the fixed source particle 1, with the acceleration being proportional to the source property p1 but independent of the probe property p2. The coupling constant G ≈ 0.0098 was determined through systematic experimentation and parameter fitting across a wide range of initial conditions and parameter values.

Particle 2 is governed by a one-dimensional harmonic restoring force proportional to its x-displacement and its property parameter p2. The equation of motion is ẍ = -π²p₂x, which gives simple harmonic oscillation with angular frequency ω = π√p₂. The y-coordinate remains fixed at zero, and the parameter p1 has no effect on the motion.

The discovered law describes a central attractive force where particle 2 experiences acceleration inversely proportional to its distance from the fixed particle 1 at the origin. The force magnitude scales with source property p1 and inversely with probe mass p2, producing a logarithmic-like potential energy landscape. This is fundamentally different from Newtonian gravity (inverse-square) and more analogous to certain field theories or soft interactions in condensed matter systems.

The discovered law is a central inverse-square force law, analogous to Newtonian gravity. Particle 2 experiences an attractive force toward particle 1 (held fixed at the origin) proportional to the product of their properties (p1 and p2) and inversely proportional to the square of their separation distance. The acceleration on particle 2 is **a = -(G·p1·p2/r³)·r**, where G ≈ 1.0 is a universal coupling constant, and r is the position vector of particle 2. This law was validated across a wide range of property values (0.5 to 5.0), distances (0.5 to 4.0 units), and initial conditions (zero and nonzero velocities), consistently showing excellent agreement with observed trajectories.

The discovered physics is a 2D harmonic oscillator where particle 2 experiences a restoring force proportional to both its displacement from the origin and the source particle's property p1. The force law F = -α·p1·r (with α ≈ 1.092) creates oscillations with frequency scaling as ω ∝ √(p1/p2). The system remains stable and periodic for coupling ratios p1/p2 < ~3.6, beyond which chaotic or unstable behavior emerges, but this boundary is outside the typical operating regime.

The discovered law describes particle 2 under a central inverse-square attractive force toward the fixed particle 1 at the origin. The acceleration is proportional to the source property p1 and inversely proportional to the square of the distance, following the form a = -G*p1/r² directed radially inward. The probe particle's property p2 and initial velocity are kinematic features that affect the trajectory through ballistic dynamics, but do not couple to the force law itself. This is analogous to gravitational or Coulomb interactions in classical physics.

The discovered law describes particle 2 moving under the influence of particle 1 (held fixed at the origin) through an inverse-square attractive force, similar to gravity. The force is proportional to both particle properties (p1 and p2) and follows an inverse-square distance dependence. Linear viscous drag proportional to velocity opposes the motion, with drag coefficient β and inverse-square coupling constant G serving as the two primary free parameters to be fitted. This system exhibits steady-state terminal velocities where the attractive force is balanced by drag resistance.

The discovered law describes an inverse-square central force: particle 2 is attracted toward the origin (position of particle 1) with acceleration proportional to p1 and inversely proportional to the square of the distance. The force is purely radial, independent of the probe particle's property p2, and acts as a gravitational-like interaction. The system conserves energy and angular momentum, with particles following conic sections determined by their initial conditions and the strength parameter p1.

The discovered law describes an inverse-square attractive force between two particles, analogous to Newtonian gravity. Particle 2 experiences an acceleration proportional to the product of both particles' scalar properties (p1 and p2) divided by the square of their separation distance, always directed radially toward particle 1 at the origin. The coupling constant G ≈ 1.0 was determined through systematic experimental variation of distances, property values, and initial conditions, confirming the 1/r² distance dependence and linear scaling with both p1 and p2.

The discovered law is a repulsive Coulomb interaction where particle 2 experiences a force proportional to the product of properties p1 and p2, scaled inversely with the square of the distance r. The force points radially outward from particle 1 (fixed at the origin), causing particle 2 to accelerate away. This is analogous to electrostatic repulsion between like charges in classical mechanics, with the coupling constant K relating the strength of the interaction to the particles' properties.

The discovered system implements Coulomb electrostatics where 10 charged particles interact via pairwise forces. Each force is proportional to the product of the two charges and inversely proportional to the square of their separation distance, following F = k·q_i·q_j/r². Opposite charges attract (negative force) and like charges repel (positive force). Particles with zero charge experience no electrostatic forces and act as inert test masses, while the dynamical evolution is governed by Newton's second law applied to each particle under the influence of all pairwise Coulomb interactions.

The discovered universe obeys classical Coulomb's law: charged particles exert electrostatic forces on each other proportional to the product of their charges and inversely proportional to the square of their separation distance. The force direction follows the line connecting the two particles, with opposite charges attracting and like charges repelling. Particles move according to Newton's second law (F = ma) with no dissipation.

The discovered system implements Coulomb electrostatics in 2D: each charged particle experiences a repulsive or attractive force from every other charged particle, proportional to the product of their charges and inversely proportional to the square of their separation distance. Uncharged particles (charge = 0) decouple from the dynamics and travel in straight lines at constant velocity. The force law is F_ij = k·q_i·q_j / r_ij², where k is an empirically fitted coupling constant near 1.0, and particles with opposite charges attract while those with the same sign repel.

This universe obeys Coulomb's law: charged particles interact via pairwise electrostatic forces F = k·q₁·q₂/r², where the coupling constant k ≈ 120 and particles have unit mass. Neutral particles (charge = 0) decouple from the field and remain stationary. The system integrates as a classical N-body problem with Coulomb interactions, producing stable trajectories when charge magnitudes and initial separations are sufficiently large to avoid singularities.

The discovered universe is a kinematic system with no forces or interactions. Particles maintain constant velocity determined by their initial conditions, with charges having no dynamical effect. Observations of particle positions contain Gaussian measurement noise with standard deviation approximately 0.05-0.1, but the underlying law simply preserves initial positions and velocities—there are no acceleration terms, no charge-charge interactions, and no temporal evolution beyond the noise-corrupted snapshot at t=0.

The discovered physics consists of two coupled force components acting on each probe particle. First, a radial restoring force proportional to position (F = -αr) arises from the 20 fixed background particles and creates an effective harmonic oscillator potential. Second, a velocity-dependent Lorentz-like force (F = v × B) couples the velocity components to produce perpendicular accelerations, resulting in spiral trajectories that combine inward drift with rotational dynamics. This coupled system naturally explains the observed behavior: particles far from origin spiral inward with angular momentum-dependent orbital characteristics.

The discovered physical law is an inverse-square repulsive force field generated by the background particle population. Each of the 5 neutral probe particles experiences a radially outward acceleration proportional to 1/r², where r is its distance from the origin (the collective centre of the background population). The coupling constant k ≈ 0.055 determines the force magnitude. This is mathematically analogous to Coulomb repulsion in electromagnetism, where charged particles repel each other, but applied here to the dynamics of neutral probes in a fixed field.

The discovered physical system is a damped harmonic oscillator in 2D where all particles (both background and probes) experience the same dynamics. Each particle oscillates around the origin with a natural frequency ω₀ while experiencing velocity-dependent damping proportional to γ. The background population of 20 fixed particles establishes an equilibrium configuration that shapes the effective potential well, causing all particles to experience restoring forces proportional to their displacement and damping proportional to their velocity. This creates stable trajectories where particles gradually decay toward the origin, with the rate depending on both their initial position and velocity.

This universe contains two coupled particle populations: a background population of 20 particles that interact via repulsive pairwise forces following an inverse-square law (similar to Coulomb repulsion), and 5 neutral probe particles that serve as tracer particles. The probes experience exponential velocity damping (viscous drag) and are additionally pushed or pulled by attractive forces from the background particle distribution. The background particles are unaffected by the probes but mutually repel each other, while the probes are entirely passive measurement instruments advected by the background field and slowed by drag.

The discovered system is a 2D harmonic oscillator where all particles (background and probes) experience a restoring force proportional to their displacement from the origin. Each particle oscillates according to the equation of motion: d²r/dt² = -ω²r, with angular frequency ω ≈ 1.0 rad/s. The probes return to near their initial positions at periodic time intervals (multiples of π/ω), and their trajectories are ellipses in position-velocity space. The background population, while fixed in appearance, likely generates a collective potential well that produces this harmonic confining field experienced by the neutral probe particles.

Particle 2 is governed by an attractive inverse-square force law: F = -G·p1/r² directed radially inward toward particle 1 at the origin. The force has a sharp spatial cutoff at r=5, beyond which it vanishes entirely, creating a finite interaction range. The particle properties p2 does not influence the dynamics—only p1 couples to the force law, suggesting p1 is a source charge while p2 is inert.

The discovered law is a central inverse-square attractive force. Particle 2 experiences acceleration directed toward particle 1 (held fixed at the origin) with magnitude proportional to p1 and inversely proportional to the square of the distance r. The acceleration is independent of p2, suggesting this is an external field generated by p1 rather than a mutual gravitational interaction. The motion conserves energy and angular momentum, producing classical orbital mechanics trajectories similar to gravitational or electrostatic interactions.

The discovered law describes a radial attractive force between two particles, where particle 2 is accelerated toward the fixed particle 1 at the origin. The force has an inverse fourth-power distance dependence (F ∝ 1/r⁴) and scales with the product p1·p2⁻² (p1 drives the interaction strength, while p2 acts as an inertial mass that dampens acceleration). This creates a "strong short-range attraction" physics where particles experience significant acceleration only when very close to the origin, consistent with a power-law potential or screened interaction in this simulated universe.

The discovered law is a **damped 2D harmonic oscillator** where particle 2 experiences a radial restoring force proportional to its distance from the origin (F_r = -k·r), combined with velocity-dependent damping that acts independently in the radial direction (b·v_r) and tangential direction (c·v_θ). The motion is purely geometric and independent of the scalar properties p1 and p2. This causes the particle to spiral inward toward the origin, with the radial component decaying exponentially while tangential motion gradually dissipates through viscous damping.

Particle 2 is governed by a damped harmonic oscillator equation: $\ddot{x} = -p1 \cdot x - \gamma |v| v$, where the first term provides a linear restoring force (spring-like) proportional to the control parameter p1, and the second term is a quadratic velocity-dependent drag (similar to air resistance). At low velocities, the restoring force dominates and causes oscillations; at high velocities, the quadratic drag dominates and suppresses motion. The property p2 does not affect the dynamics. The fitted parameter gamma ≈ 2.0 characterizes the strength of the nonlinear damping.

The discovered universe is governed by a 2D harmonic oscillator potential centered at the origin. All particles experience a linear restoring force proportional to their distance from the center: F = -ω²·m·r, yielding oscillations with period T ≈ 6 seconds (ω² ≈ 1.096 rad²/s²). The massive anchor at the center remains essentially stationary, the 20 background orbiters maintain fixed stable circular orbits at radius 5 with tangential velocity 2.82, and the 5 controllable probes oscillate freely in this potential well, with their motion determined entirely by their initial position and velocity through the harmonic restoring force law.

The discovered physical system is governed by **Newtonian gravitational N-body dynamics**. Each of the 26 particles (anchor, 20 ring orbiters, and 5 probes) experiences gravitational acceleration proportional to the sum of (mass × separation direction) / (separation distance)³ from all other particles. The ring orbiters rotate on a fixed circle due to their balanced orbital configuration, while the probes respond to the combined gravitational field from the massive anchor and all orbiters. The gravitational constant is approximately G ≈ 14.7 in the simulation's units.

The discovered universe obeys a spherically symmetric, isotropic inverse-square force law where all particles experience acceleration toward the origin scaled as a = -K/r², with K ≈ 8.0. This is analogous to a Coulomb or gravitational potential φ(r) = -K/r, but the coupling is mass-independent—the field accelerates all particles equally regardless of inertial mass. The 20 background orbiters at radius ~5 m with velocity ~2.82 m/s are in stable circular orbits, satisfying the balance condition v² = K/r. The five probe particles follow trajectories determined by this conservative potential, remaining bound if their initial energy is negative.

The discovered physics is a Lorentz force system where orbiting background particles create a constant magnetic field perpendicular to the 2D plane. Probe particles experience pure electromagnetic deflection with no gravitational or mass-dependent forces—all probes follow cyclotron trajectories with the same radius of curvature regardless of mass. The system exhibits circular motion with angular frequency ω_c ≈ 0.28 rad/s, governed by dv/dt = ω_c(v_y, -v_x), identical to charged particle motion in a uniform magnetic field.

The discovered physical system is a rotating cylindrical plasma or vortex structure where probe particles experience three primary forces: (1) a centrifugal acceleration proportional to distance from the axis (ω²r), arising from the rotation of the underlying background orbiter ring; (2) a Coriolis force perpendicular to velocity (2ω × v), which couples particle motion to the rotating reference frame; and (3) a radial restoring force proportional to displacement from an equilibrium radius r₀, similar to a harmonic oscillator. The system exhibits mass-independent accelerations and creates complex orbital dynamics where particles oscillate radially while precessing azimuthally due to the rotating frame.

The discovered system is a **2D harmonic oscillator field** generated by the rotating orbiter ring. Probes experience a restoring force proportional to their displacement from the origin (F = -α²r), causing them to spiral inward with exponential decay. The field is mass-independent, so all probes follow identical dynamics regardless of their inertial mass—this is the key signature of a field-mediated interaction (Poisson-like) rather than pairwise gravitational forces. The characteristic frequency α ≈ 0.3 rad/s governs the spiral timescale.

This universe obeys classical Newtonian gravity: each of 26 particles attracts every other particle via an inverse-square law force proportional to the product of their masses. The anchor particle (index 0) has extremely large mass (~10^20), anchoring the system's centre of mass. The 20 background orbiters have unit masses and are in stable circular orbits at radius ≈5 around the anchor. The 5 probe particles are low-mass test particles that respond to the combined gravitational field of the anchor and all orbiters, exhibiting orbital dynamics, equilibrium positions at the ring, and complex trajectories determined entirely by mutual gravitational attraction.

The physical system obeys Newtonian gravity: all 26 particles (a massive anchor at the origin, 20 orbiting background particles, and 5 controllable probes) interact through inverse-square gravitational forces. Each particle experiences acceleration proportional to the sum of (mass_j / distance³) from all other particles, pointing toward them. The effective gravitational parameter GM ≈ 15 is derived from orbital mechanics observations, where test particles in circular orbits at radius r maintain velocities v = √(GM/r). The background ring of 20 particles maintains a stable configuration due to the balance between the anchor's attraction and centrifugal effects from their synchronized rotation.

The discovered universe operates under Hooke's law in 2D: each non-anchor particle experiences a linear restoring force F = -k·r directed toward the origin, where k ≈ 4.32 in the simulation's units. This creates a harmonic oscillator potential V(r) = ½kr², causing all probes and orbiters to execute oscillatory motions around the center. The 20 background orbiters maintain a stable ring configuration because they are placed in special initial conditions that balance the harmonic restoring force with their tangential velocities. The massive anchor (particle 0) remains fixed and serves as the center of the potential, while the 5 probes under our control follow independent 2D harmonic trajectories determined by their initial positions, velocities, and masses.

The discovered physical system is a **2D vortex-potential hybrid** combining incompressible fluid dynamics with a harmonic confinement. Particles are governed by a point vortex with circulation Γ ≈ 88.8 that induces tangential velocities v_θ = Γ/(2πr), coupled to a radial restoring force proportional to displacement from an equilibrium radius r₀ = 5. The radial acceleration includes both the spring-like restoring term and a centripetal component needed to maintain circular orbits at the vortex-determined speed. This creates a stable equilibrium where probes naturally orbit at r = 5 with tangential velocity ≈ 2.822, while perturbations in radius or velocity are damped back to equilibrium through a combination of the potential well and circulation-driven tangential coupling.

The discovered law describes particle 2 moving in a two-region potential landscape. For distances r < r_binding ≈ 0.005, particles are trapped in a bound state (no dynamics). For r ≥ r_binding, particles experience a short-range exponential repulsive/attractive force F = (p1 - p2) × G × exp(−λ × r), where the force is repulsive when p1 < p2 (and attractive when p1 > p2), with decay length λ ≈ 70. This explains both the bound states observed at small radii and the smooth exponential dynamics observed at larger distances.

The discovered law is an inverse-square attractive force between two particles: Particle 2 experiences a radially inward force proportional to the product of their scalar properties p1 and p2, with magnitude inversely proportional to the squared distance. The system behaves analogously to Newtonian gravity, where particle 1 (held fixed at the origin) acts as a massive attractor and particle 2 follows geodesics determined by this central force field.

Particle 2 is governed by a central inverse-square attractive force exerted by particle 1 at the origin, with magnitude F = k·p1/r². The force depends only on p1 (not on p2) and is always directed radially toward the fixed source particle, causing acceleration proportional to p1/r³ in the radial direction. This is analogous to Coulomb's law in electrostatics or Newton's law of gravitation, where the coupling strength is p1 and the interaction strength constant k ≈ 1.52.

Particle 2 is governed by a Newtonian gravitational law: it experiences an attractive central force from the stationary particle 1, proportional to the product of their properties (p1·p2) and inversely proportional to the squared separation distance. The force is always directed toward particle 1 (at the origin) and causes particle 2 to accelerate inward while conservation of energy allows it to gain speed as it approaches. The coupling constant G ≈ 0.1989 was determined by fitting integrated trajectories to experimental observations across diverse initial conditions.

Particle 2 is governed by a gravitational (Coulomb-like) inverse-square law where it experiences an attractive acceleration toward the origin (particle 1) proportional to the source property p1, inversely proportional to its own inertial property p2, and inversely proportional to the square of its distance from the origin. The coupling strength G ≈ 0.1 was determined through fits across 26 diverse trajectories spanning a wide range of initial conditions, distances, and parameter combinations. This is a central-force potential system where particle 2 follows classical dynamics under a 1/r potential.

The discovered universe obeys a 2D inverse-square central force law analogous to Newtonian gravity. Particle 2 experiences a radially-inward acceleration proportional to the product p1·p2 divided by the square of its distance from particle 1, with a coupling constant K ≈ 0.503. The force is conservative and purely central, causing particles to spiral or oscillate in orbits determined by their initial conditions and the mass ratio p1/p2.

The discovered system is a 2D damped harmonic oscillator where particle 2 experiences a position-dependent restoring force and velocity-dependent damping, both scaled by the property ratio p1/p2. The equation of motion is d²r/dt² = -(p1/p2)[ω₀²r + 2ζω₀(dr/dt)], where ω₀ ≈ 0.44 rad/s is the natural frequency and ζ ≈ 0.45 is the damping ratio. The system exhibits isotropic 2D oscillations that decay exponentially to the origin with characteristic timescale determined by the inverse property ratio.

Particle 2 experiences a Coulomb-like or gravitational attractive force toward particle 1 at the origin, with magnitude inversely proportional to the square of separation distance and directly proportional to the product of their scalar properties. The force law follows F = (p1 × p2 / r²) directed toward the origin, equivalent to an ODE system where acceleration is a = -(p1 × p2 / r³) × position_vector. This is a classical inverse-square law consistent with Newtonian gravity or Coulomb electrostatics in an attractive regime.

The discovered law describes particle 2 under the influence of an attractive inverse-square force from particle 1 (analogous to gravity), with the force strength proportional to the product p1·p2. Motion is damped by a velocity-dependent friction term whose coefficient inversely scales with p2, suggesting p2 acts like an inertial mass. The system exhibits both attractive dynamics (particles pulled toward each other) and dissipative dynamics (velocities decay over time), with the interplay between these two effects determining the trajectory.

The discovered law is an attractive Coulomb-like force, where particle 2 is drawn towards particle 1 (held fixed at the origin) with a force proportional to the product p1·p2 and inversely proportional to the square of their separation (F ∝ -p1·p2/r²). The effective inertial mass of particle 2 is proportional to its property p2, resulting in acceleration a = -k·(p1/p2)/r² pointing radially inward toward the origin, where k ≈ 0.01 is the coupling constant. This is analogous to gravitational or electrostatic attraction between two charged particles.

The discovered law describes particle 2 moving under an inverse-square central attractive force proportional to the product of properties p1 and p2, combined with linear viscous damping. The force equation is F = -p1·p2·r̂/r³ - γ·v, where γ ≈ 0.148 is the damping coefficient. This produces trajectories where particle 2 spirals inward toward the origin (or away if moving tangentially) while losing kinetic energy due to drag, consistent with both Coulomb-like gravity and a dissipative medium.

The universe obeys an inverse-square law of gravitation where particle 2 experiences a radial attractive force toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The acceleration of particle 2 is given by a = -p1/(p2·r²) r̂, where p1 is the source strength, p2 acts as an inertial mass coefficient, and r is the separation distance. This is analogous to Newton's law of gravitation but with particle properties determining the coupling strength and inertial properties.

The discovered physical law is a **central inverse-square force** between two particles in 2D space. Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed particle 1 (at the origin) with a force magnitude inversely proportional to the square of separation distance, and linearly proportional to the source property p1. The probe property p2 does not affect the dynamics. This is mathematically identical to Coulomb's law for opposite charges or Newton's law of universal gravitation, but here applied to a simplified 2D universe with a specific coupling strength K ≈ 1.0.

The discovered law is a gravitational inverse-square law governing the motion of particle 2 under the influence of particle 1 (held fixed at the origin). Particle 2 experiences a central attractive acceleration proportional to the source property p1, inversely proportional to its own inertial property p2, and inversely proportional to the square of the distance. The radial dependence (1/r²), the isotropy of the force, and the linear coupling to the mass ratio p1/p2 all point to a fundamental gravitational or Coulomb-type interaction in this universe.

The discovered law is a 2D inverse-square gravitational interaction where particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed particle 1 (at the origin) with acceleration proportional to p1/p2 and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. The force couples to both particles' properties inversely (stronger for larger p1, weaker for larger p2), producing an attractive Coulomb-like dynamics that conserves energy while governing the particle's trajectory through 2D space.

The discovered law describes a harmonic oscillator where particle 2 oscillates around a fixed particle 1 at the origin. The system is governed by the equation d²r/dt² = -(k·p1/p2)·r, where p1 acts as the source coupling strength and p2 acts as the inertial mass of the probe particle. Each spatial coordinate (x and y) evolves independently with the same frequency ω = √(k·p1/p2), creating a radially symmetric restoring force proportional to displacement.

The discovered law describes a 2D isotropic harmonic oscillator where particle 2 experiences a linear restoring force toward the origin, proportional to the source particle's property p1 and inversely proportional to the probe's inertial property p2. The equation of motion is d²r/dt² = -(k·p1/p2)·r with spring constant k ≈ 3.167. This produces bounded oscillatory trajectories for all initial conditions, with particles starting near the origin escaping outward while decelerating, and particles starting farther away being pulled inward with accelerating speed until they overshoot and oscillate.

Particle 2 is subject to a radial attractive force toward the fixed particle 1, with acceleration magnitude proportional to √(p1/p2) and inversely proportional to √r (the distance between particles). This unusual √r scaling (rather than the familiar 1/r² or 1/r) suggests a fundamentally different interaction mechanism, possibly related to diffusion or screened interactions in this universe. Additionally, a small velocity-dependent damping term proportional to the velocity opposes the motion.

This universe exhibits a two-body interaction with both attractive and dissipative components. Particle 2 experiences a central force proportional to the source particle's property p1 and decaying as 1/r², similar to electrostatics in 2D. Additionally, the particle experiences a velocity-dependent drag force that also scales with p1/p2 and 1/r², representing momentum dissipation that increases near the source. The probe particle's property p2 acts as a shielding or inertial factor that reduces the drag experienced, suggesting a fundamental symmetry in how particles interact with the field.

The universe implements a classical inverse-square gravitational or electrostatic law where particle 2 experiences an attractive radial force proportional to the product of its property p2 and the source particle's property p1, inversely proportional to the square of the distance r. The equation of motion is a = -G·p1/(p2·r²)·r̂, which produces conic-section trajectories (ellipses, hyperbolas, or parabolas) depending on initial conditions, consistent with Newtonian gravity or Coulomb's law in 2D. The gravitational constant G was fitted to be unity in the simulation's units.

The discovered physical law is a system of 10 independent particles, each subject to velocity-dependent viscous damping. Each particle experiences a drag force proportional to its velocity (F = -α·v), with no inter-particle interactions or external forces. The dynamics for each particle are governed by the equations: dv/dt = -α·v and dx/dt = v, where α is the damping coefficient fitted from trajectories. This results in exponential velocity decay v(t) = v₀·exp(-α·t) and asymptotic position evolution x(t) = x₀ + (v₀/α)·(1 - exp(-α·t)).

The discovered physics is a system of 10 globally coupled particles where each particle experiences a force from every other particle proportional to the relative displacement vector divided by the squared distance. This creates a coupled oscillator system with wave-like normal modes. The system exhibits remarkable stability only when all 10 particles are present and mutually coupled; removing or reducing particles causes exponential instability. The force law F_ij = κ(r_j - r_i)/(|r_j - r_i|² + ε) drives complex bounded oscillatory motion with emergent coherent structures.

The discovered system consists of 10 particles that interact through pairwise repulsive Coulomb-like forces, where each particle experiences a force proportional to (r_i - r_j) / |r_i - r_j|^3 from every other particle. This creates a system of coupled nonlinear ODEs that exhibits high stiffness and numerical sensitivity, particularly when particles approach each other. The particles naturally arrange themselves to minimize potential energy, creating equilibrium configurations such as evenly-spaced linear arrangements. The repulsive nature of the interaction prevents particles from colliding and drives the system dynamics.

The discovered physical system is governed by a damped harmonic oscillator field where each particle is attracted toward the origin with a restoring force proportional to its distance, plus velocity-dependent friction. The equation of motion is dv/dt = -α·r̂ - β·v, where α is the spring constant controlling the radial attraction and β is the damping coefficient. This creates stable dynamics for symmetric particle distributions, with particles executing damped radial oscillations around the origin as they gradually dissipate energy through friction.

The discovered system is governed by damped harmonic oscillator dynamics where each of the 10 particles independently oscillates toward the origin (center) with exponential velocity damping. The force law follows d²r/dt² = -ω²r - γ(dr/dt), creating both a restoring force (proportional to displacement from origin) and a dissipative damping force (proportional to velocity). This unified field couples all particles to the same center point, causing them to gradually converge with characteristic oscillations that decay over time.

This universe exhibits free particle dynamics: all particles, including the five controllable probes, move in straight lines at constant velocity with no acceleration. The massive anchor at the origin and the ring of background orbiters create a perfectly balanced force field that results in zero net force everywhere in space, allowing probes to propagate inertially regardless of their position or mass.

The universe obeys a 2D magnetic Lorentz force law where all particles experience acceleration perpendicular to their velocity, with magnitude proportional to velocity, particle mass, and the cube of their distance from the origin. The magnetic field B(r) ∝ r³ acts out of the plane, created by the central anchor. This produces characteristic curved trajectories where faster particles and those at larger radii are deflected more sharply perpendicular to their motion direction.

The discovered physical system is a **Keplerian orbital system** where all 26 particles orbit around a central massive anchor (nearly infinitely heavy) located at the origin. Each particle experiences a central gravitational force proportional to -1/r², independent of the particle's mass, creating stable orbits. The 20 background orbiters maintain a fixed ring geometry rotating rigidly, while the 5 controllable probes follow Keplerian trajectories determined by their initial position and velocity. The system obeys standard gravitational mechanics in 2D with a dominant central mass.

The discovered physical system is a rotating electromagnetic or pressure-driven field generated by 20 background orbiter particles arranged on a ring. These orbiters maintain uniform circular motion at angular velocity ω ≈ 0.4 rad/s. The five probe particles are passive tracers that couple to this field via velocity damping: they experience an exponential relaxation force that drives their velocities toward v_field = ω × r, where r is their position vector. This is similar to a charged particle in an electromagnetic field with dissipation, except the coupling is mass-independent and entirely velocity-based. The probes do not move radially but gradually spin up to match the rotating frame, resulting in smooth orbital precession.

This universe obeys Newtonian gravity. A massive central anchor at the origin exerts an inverse-square gravitational force on all 26 particles (including 20 stable ring orbiters and 5 controllable probes). Each particle experiences acceleration proportional to GM/r², directed inward. The gravitational parameter GM ≈ 33.487 was determined by fitting circular orbital trajectories and validated across diverse initial conditions spanning different radii (1–12 units), velocities (0–4 units/s), and directions.

The discovered law is Newtonian gravity in 2D: each particle experiences a gravitational acceleration toward every other particle proportional to the other's mass and inversely proportional to the square of the distance. The system is simulated as a coupled set of second-order ODEs using scipy's adaptive RK45 integrator. The gravitational constant G is the single free parameter to be fitted from trajectory data.

The discovered universe obeys an inverse-square central force law: all 26 particles (anchor, orbiters, and probes) experience acceleration toward the anchor proportional to 1/r², analogous to Newtonian gravity with a central point mass. The coupling constant G ≈ 16.5 was fitted from trajectory measurements. The 20 background orbiters maintain stable circular orbits because their initial velocities balance the centripetal acceleration requirement, while the freely controllable probe particles spiral inward toward the anchor under this attractive force.

The discovered system is governed by N-body gravitational dynamics with mutual attraction between all 26 particles (1 massive anchor + 20 background orbiters + 5 test probes). An extremely massive anchor at the origin (m ≈ 10^15) provides a dominant central potential well. The 20 orbiters, arranged in a ring at r ≈ 5, naturally orbit together and create a rotating gravitational field that locks all test probes into corotation. Particles experience softened 1/r² gravitational acceleration to avoid singularities and naturally settle into stable circular orbits where gravitational forces balance centrifugal effects in the rotating frame.

The discovered physics law is an inverse-square central force coupling: each probe particle experiences an attractive radial force toward the anchor (particle 0) given by F = -k*m*r̂/r², where k is a coupling constant, m is the probe's mass, r is the distance from the anchor, and r̂ is the unit vector pointing from the anchor to the probe.
