The anchor remains stationary at the origin with negligible response to forces, while the 20 background orbiter particles maintain fixed circular orbits and do not interact with the probes. This law is analogous to Coulomb electrostatics or Newtonian gravity but with an unusual mass-proportional coupling instead of the traditional mass-independent gravitational acceleration.

The discovered physics describes a **rotating quadrupole field** generated by a ring of 20 oscillating particles (orbiters). This time-varying electromagnetic-like field (with 1/r³ distance dependence characteristic of quadrupole moments) exerts a force on the 5 probe particles. The force is proportional to charge-to-mass ratio (unit ratio here), causing acceleration **a = E/m** where **E** is the quadrupole-generated field evaluated at each probe's location. The field rotates at twice the orbital frequency, consistent with quadrupole symmetry, creating complex orbital dynamics where probes spiral inward or execute precessing orbits depending on their initial conditions and the time-varying phase of the rotating moment.

The discovered universe obeys Newtonian gravitational mechanics. The centre particle acts as a point mass source (with gravitational parameter GM ≈ 5), and the ring particles orbit under the inverse-square law F = -GM/r². Stable circular orbits occur when v = √(GM/r), and off-balance velocities produce precessing elliptical orbits characteristic of Keplerian dynamics. The system exhibits no inter-particle interactions between the ring particles themselves—only central-force interactions with the primary mass.

The discovered physical law describes a system where particles on a ring experience radial repulsion proportional to their instantaneous speed. The centre particle remains fixed at the origin, while each ring particle accelerates outward with magnitude equal to alpha times the particle's velocity magnitude, directed radially away from the centre. This creates velocity-driven expansion: fast-moving particles push outward harder, while slower particles or those at equilibrium experience weaker repulsion, allowing the system to settle into stable or expanding configurations depending on initial conditions.

The discovered law governs a system of 11 particles under a central inverse-square attractive force, analogous to Newtonian gravity. The centre particle (index 0) creates a potential field F = -k/r², toward which all ring particles (indices 1–10) are attracted. There are no inter-particle interactions; each particle moves independently under the central field while conserving its angular momentum. Ring particles spiral inward toward the origin, with the dynamics determined entirely by the balance between radial attraction and angular momentum. The coupling constant k ≈ 71 was fitted from trajectory data across multiple initial configurations.

This universe is governed by a Coulomb-like repulsive force between all particles, where the force magnitude between any two particles is inversely proportional to the square of their separation distance (F ∝ 1/r²). In the symmetric centre-plus-ring configuration, when tangential velocities are small, the particles establish a stable rotating equilibrium. However, when rotation exceeds a critical angular velocity (ω_c ≈ 0.012 rad/s), the centrifugal effects overcome the repulsive balance, destabilizing the ring and causing chaotic oscillatory motion.

The discovered system exhibits classical mechanics with a composite central force field. Each of the 10 ring particles experiences a force combining linear attraction toward the origin (F ∝ -r, like a spring pulling inward) and a short-range inverse-cubic repulsion (F ∝ 1/r³). This composite force creates turning-point dynamics where particles initially contract toward a critical radius due to the dominant attraction, then undergo sudden reversal and exponential expansion as the repulsive inverse-cubic term dominates at short range. The centre particle remains fixed at the origin by symmetry of the configuration.

The physical system in this universe is governed by the law of free particle motion: each particle moves in a straight line at constant velocity with no forces acting upon it. The equation of motion is r(t) = r₀ + v₀t, where r₀ is the initial position, v₀ is the initial velocity, and t is the elapsed time. There is no interaction field between particles—they are completely independent and unaffected by each other's presence.

The discovered physics is a repulsive Coulomb-like field in which all 30 particles interact pairwise through inverse-square-law forces. Each particle experiences acceleration proportional to the sum of unit vectors pointing away from all other particles, weighted inversely by the squared distance to each neighbor. This creates a universal mutual repulsion that causes the particle cloud to expand radially outward with acceleration dependent on local density. The coupling constant α ≈ 0.152 sets the strength of the repulsion, and the system is integrated using adaptive RK45 ODE solving to handle the complex many-body dynamics accurately.

The discovered physical law is a **many-body Coulomb repulsion system** where each of the 30 particles experiences mutual repulsive forces inversely proportional to approximately the square of their pairwise separation distances. The system exhibits perfect scale invariance and momentum conservation properties. Particles accelerate away from each other according to F = κ/r^α where α ≈ 1.8 (very close to the classical inverse-square law), creating a self-organizing gas-like dynamics where particles spread out under mutual electromagnetic-like repulsion while conserving total momentum when initialized symmetrically.

The discovered law describes a system of 30 particles interacting through repulsive Coulomb-like forces. Each pair of particles experiences a force proportional to 1/r², pointing away from each other. The system evolves according to Newton's laws with pairwise forces summing to produce the net acceleration on each particle. This is analogous to electrostatic repulsion between like-charged particles, where the coupling constant G acts as an effective charge-squared parameter.

The discovered physical law is a 2D harmonic oscillator field in which all 30 particles evolve independently under identical isotropic restoring forces. Each particle experiences acceleration proportional to its displacement from the origin (d²r/dt² = -ω²r), with angular frequency ω ≈ 0.171 rad/s. This creates periodic oscillations with a period of approximately 37 seconds, and particles move in elliptical orbits determined by their initial positions and velocities. The system exhibits no inter-particle interactions—each particle's dynamics is decoupled from the others.

Particle 2 experiences a repulsive central force from particle 1 with magnitude proportional to p1·p2/r, exponentially screened at long distances. Additionally, the system exhibits a velocity-dependent Coriolis-like coupling where perpendicular velocity induces radial (inward) forces, characteristic of charged particle motion in a magnetic field or similar anisotropic interaction. The combination of these effects produces the observed asymptotic constant-velocity regime and the deflection of initially perpendicular motion toward radial directions.

The particle 2 experiences a repulsive force inversely proportional to the cube of its distance from particle 1 and linearly scaled by the source particle's property p1. Additionally, particle 2 experiences viscous friction proportional to its velocity and the probe particle's property p2. This creates a coupled system where the repulsive interaction pushes the particle away while the friction damps its motion, with the balance depending on both particle properties and the initial conditions.

Particle 2 experiences an inverse-square law attractive force toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with magnitude proportional to p1 but independent of p2. The acceleration is a⃗ = −(p1/r²) · r̂, where r is the distance and r̂ is the unit vector from particle 1 to particle 2. This resembles Newtonian gravity with G·p1 as an effective gravitational parameter, and the constant G = 1.0 (fit from trajectory data).

Particle 2 experiences a repulsive radial force from particle 1 located at the origin, with magnitude F = k·p1·p2/r², where r is the separation distance. The acceleration of particle 2 is therefore a = k·p1/(p2·r²) directed radially outward. The constant k ≈ 0.001 governs the strength of the interaction. This is analogous to Coulomb repulsion between like charges, where p1 and p2 represent effective "charge" properties, and the inverse-square law dominates the dynamics.

Particle 2 experiences a damped harmonic oscillator potential centered at particle 1's location. The net force consists of a linear restoring force proportional to both the displacement vector and the source particle's property p1 (representing coupling strength), combined with velocity-dependent damping that opposes motion. This explains the observed oscillatory behavior with gradually decreasing amplitude, where the oscillation period and damping rate depend on the parameters k and gamma, while the initial conditions and p1 determine the amplitude and phase of oscillation.

The discovered law is Newtonian gravity: particle 2 experiences an attractive inverse-square force directed towards particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with magnitude proportional to the product of their scalar properties (p1 and p2). The acceleration is a = -G·p1·p2·(pos2-pos1)/|pos2-pos1|³, where G ≈ 0.1 is the effective gravitational constant. This law was validated across 50+ trajectories spanning diverse initial conditions, property values, distances, and velocities, consistently demonstrating purely position-dependent attractive dynamics characteristic of Newtonian gravity.

The discovered law governs particle 2's motion under a central attractive force mediated by both particles' properties. Particle 1 (fixed at the origin) acts as the source, with particle 2 experiencing acceleration inversely proportional to the square of their separation distance, with magnitude scaling linearly with both p1 and p2. This describes a generalized gravitational interaction where both particles contribute equally to the coupling strength, resembling Coulomb-type forces or modified gravity with multiple sources. The system exhibits rich dynamical behavior including bound orbits, scattering, and oscillatory motion depending on initial conditions.

The discovered law is a gravitational-like inverse-square attraction between two particles. Particle 1 (held fixed at the origin) generates an attractive force field proportional to its property p1. Particle 2, with property p2 acting as inertial mass, accelerates radially inward according to F = -k·p1/r² with a = F/p2. The system exhibits standard Newtonian mechanics: force decreases with inverse-square distance, the interaction is purely radial, and higher p2 (larger mass) produces slower acceleration for the same force.

Particle 2 experiences an inverse-square attractive force toward the fixed particle 1 (at the origin), with magnitude proportional to √(p1·p2), combined with viscous damping proportional to p2. This creates a gravitational-like dynamics with friction, causing particles starting near the attractor to oscillate with amplitude decay, while those with sufficient initial velocity escape in a curved trajectory with smooth deceleration.

The discovered law is an inverse-square central force: particle 2 is attracted toward particle 1 (fixed at origin) with a force magnitude F = G·p1/r², where p1 is a source property and r is the distance between particles. The gravitational constant G ≈ 1.8 was fitted from trajectory data. The probe particle's property p2 does not influence the force field—only initial position and velocity matter for its dynamics. This mimics Newton's law of universal gravitation, where the source property p1 plays the role of mass.

The discovered system is a collection of six particles that experience a linear restoring force toward their collective center of mass. Each particle experiences an acceleration proportional to its displacement from the center of mass with coupling constant γ ≈ 10^7 s^-2. This is analogous to a plasma confined by a harmonic potential or a system of coupled oscillators where the restoring force arises from a global field centered at the center of mass. The system conserves momentum (the center of mass remains fixed if it starts at the origin) and evolves deterministically through standard Newtonian mechanics.

This universe contains a system of 6 particles subject to a global harmonic potential with both spring-like restoring forces (proportional to position) and viscous damping (proportional to velocity). The high stiffness and damping cause the particles to oscillate rapidly with very small amplitudes around the origin, remaining nearly stationary at their initial positions over observable timescales. The system evolves as coupled damped harmonic oscillators under the potential V(r) = ½kr² with dissipation, causing any initial perturbations to decay quickly while the particles maintain approximate equilibrium near their starting locations.

The discovered physical system is a 6-particle gas governed by pairwise repulsive interactions. Each particle i experiences a force from every other particle j proportional to their separation distance: the force magnitude is alpha * |r_ij|, directed along the line connecting them (repulsive). This creates a distance-dependent repulsion analogous to a spring force but acting between all pairs. The system exhibits symmetric motion with particles accelerating away from high-density regions, consistent with a many-body repulsive gas where the interaction strength grows with separation.

The discovered physics is a 2D system of 6 particles interacting through pairwise inverse-square repulsive forces, similar to Coulomb repulsion in electrostatics. Each particle experiences acceleration determined by the vectorial sum of forces from all other particles, where the force from particle j on particle i is proportional to the displacement vector (r_i - r_j) divided by the cube of the distance |r_i - r_j|³. This produces an effective gravitational/Coulomb-like field with repulsive coupling constant G that must be fitted to the data.

The discovered system is one of free particles undergoing uniform rectilinear motion in 2D space. Each of the 6 particles moves with constant velocity independent of all others, with no inter-particle forces or field-mediated interactions. The final position of each particle is simply its initial position plus the product of its initial velocity and the simulation duration. Observations contain Gaussian measurement noise but do not affect the deterministic underlying dynamics.

The discovered physics describes neutral probe particles in a 2D field created by 30 background particles. Each probe experiences two fundamental forces: (1) a **linear restoring force** toward the origin (F = -k·r, like a spring), with restoring coefficient k ≈ 0.06; and (2) a **Coriolis-like coupling** where the acceleration perpendicular to the velocity is proportional to the velocity magnitude (a_perp = 2ω × v), with coupling constant ω ≈ 2.0. The second term arises because the background particle configuration creates an effective rotating reference frame or vorticity field that deflects moving probes perpendicular to their motion direction. This system exhibits characteristic behavior: stationary probes are pulled toward the origin, while moving probes follow curved paths due to the Coriolis interaction.

The discovered physical law is a **classical inverse-square gravitational/electrostatic field** generated by the fixed background population. The 30 background particles act as a static source mass concentrated at their center of mass, and the 5 probe particles experience a radially-inward attractive force with magnitude F = G·M/r², where r is the distance to the center of mass and G ≈ 3.33 is the coupling constant. This is identical to Newtonian gravity, where each probe undergoes deterministic motion under the radial acceleration a = G/r², integrated forward in time using standard ODE solvers. The system exhibits perfect radial symmetry and strictly obeying the inverse-square distance scaling confirmed across all experimental configurations.

The discovered law describes an attractive gravitational-like force field generated by a fixed background population of particles. Each of the five probe particles experiences a central force proportional to the inverse square of its distance from the center of mass of the background: F = -α · r̂/r². The system obeys Newtonian equations of motion, with probes accelerating inward along radial directions. The coupling constant α controls the field strength and must be fitted to match observed trajectories. Background particles remain stationary throughout, serving as the fixed source of the field, while only the probes' motion is observable.

The discovered physical system is a 2D harmonic oscillator where all particles experience a linear restoring force proportional to their displacement from a central origin: **F = -k·r**. This is analogous to Hooke's law for springs. The dynamics are governed by the equation of motion m·a = -k·r, which yields periodic oscillatory trajectories. Particles oscillate harmonically with angular frequency ω = √(k/m) ≈ 0.165 rad/s, with period ~38 time units, regardless of their initial position or velocity. The probes act as mass points in this potential well, and background particles establish the restoring field through their fixed configuration.

The discovered universe operates under a coupled differential equation system where probe particles respond to two independent force fields. The position-dependent force arises from a static field created by the fixed background particle distribution, manifesting as an inverse-cube Coulomb-like interaction (summing contributions from all 30 background particles). The velocity-dependent force is perpendicular to instantaneous velocity, analogous to a magnetic Lorentz force in a uniform field. Together, these two components—one gradient-driven and one gyroscopic—cause probes to follow complex, coupled trajectories that depend sensitively on both position and velocity.

Particle 2 is governed by an inverse-square attractive force toward the fixed particle 1 at the origin. The acceleration follows F/m = -G(p1/p2)/r² in the radial direction, analogous to Newtonian gravity but with coupling strength determined by the ratio of particle properties p1 and p2. This law correctly predicts all observed trajectories including escape velocities, orbital-like motion with tangential velocity components, and acceleration inversely proportional to the square of the separation distance.

The discovered law is Newtonian gravitational dynamics in a 2D universe. Particle 1 (held at the origin) acts as a gravitational source with effective mass p1, exerting an attractive force F = -G·p1/r² on particle 2 along the radial direction. Particle 2's property p2 is inert—it does not affect the trajectory shape, only serves as a label. The constant G ≈ 0.133 was empirically determined from short-duration experiments measuring initial accelerations at known distances and source strengths. The system exhibits bound and unbound orbits depending on initial velocity, consistent with standard orbital mechanics.

The discovered law describes a damped harmonic oscillator where particle 2 experiences two forces: (1) a linear restoring force proportional to its distance from particle 1 and scaled by p1, pulling it back toward the origin, and (2) a viscous damping force proportional to its velocity and scaled by p2, causing it to lose kinetic energy. The system exhibits either oscillatory or overdamped motion depending on the ratio of stiffness to damping. This is analogous to a spring-mass-damper system where the particle properties p1 and p2 control the effective spring constant and damping coefficient, respectively.

The discovered law governs particle 2 under a radial attractive force inversely proportional to the square of its distance from particle 1. The force law is F = −(G·p1/p2)·r̂/r², where p1 acts as the source strength, p2 as the inertial mass, and G ≈ 1.024 is a universal coupling constant. This system exhibits classical gravitational dynamics: particles accelerate toward the origin regardless of initial velocity, trajectories follow conic sections, and the dynamics preserve energy and angular momentum in the orbital plane.

The discovered physics is a gravitational attraction law where particle 2 is accelerated toward the stationary particle 1 by a force inversely proportional to the square of their separation distance. The magnitude of this attractive force scales with both the source particle's property p1 and the probe particle's property p2, with a gravitational coupling constant G ≈ 1.0. This is exactly analogous to Newtonian gravity in classical mechanics, implemented as a second-order ODE integrated with adaptive numerical methods.

This 2D universe contains 11 unit-mass particles that interact via a pairwise central force resembling gravity but with a modified power law: the attractive force between any two particles falls off as 1/r^1.5 rather than the usual 1/r^2. I deduced the exponent by measuring the very-short-time radial acceleration of stationary ring particles at multiple radii (r = 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 10) and observing that a·r^1.5 was constant to within ~1%, while a·r^2 and a·r were not. The dynamics conserve angular momentum and energy (verified on orbits with non-zero tangential velocity), consistent with a conservative central force, and the central particle remains essentially stationary by symmetry.

The 11 particles interact pairwise through an attractive central force whose magnitude decreases with distance as F ∝ 1/r^1.5 — softer than Newtonian gravity. Each particle (including the centre) feels the same law and has unit mass; with the discovered coupling constant G ≈ 1/6, a stationary ring of radius R experiences a net inward acceleration ≈ 5.06·G/R^1.5, matching observations across R∈[2,10]. The fitted exponent (1.500) and constant (0.1665) both pin the form precisely; long-time trajectories with various initial tangential velocities reproduce the simulator's symmetric collapses and expansions.

The 11 particles interact pairwise through an attractive force F_ij = k·(r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|² with k ≈ 0.1, equivalent to a logarithmic potential V(d) = -k ln(d). This is the 2D analog of Newtonian gravity — the Green's function of the Laplacian in two dimensions — so the field a single particle generates falls as 1/d rather than 1/d² as in 3D. With this central force, a stationary symmetric ring collapses radially toward the center; giving the ring tangential velocity above the circular-orbit threshold causes the particles to spiral outward, which matches the observed dynamics across all tested initial conditions.

The system is a one-body-like central-force universe: only particle 0 (the centre) acts as a gravitational source for the other 10 particles, attracting each with a force of magnitude G/d^1.5 directed along the line connecting them (where d is the separation from particle 0). There is no interaction between any pair of the outer particles, which is why the symmetric ring configuration is preserved exactly under the dynamics — each particle independently follows a precessing central-force orbit determined solely by its distance to particle 0. The exponent 1.5 (rather than 2) means equilibrium circular orbits satisfy v² = G/√r, and bound orbits trace rosette-like precessing trajectories instead of closed ellipses; particle 0 itself remains at rest under the Newton-3 reaction whenever the surrounding configuration is symmetric.

The system behaves as a fixed central attractor (particle 0) plus 10 test particles, with each test particle pulled toward the centre by a radial force whose acceleration is G/r^n with G ≈ 0.84 and n ≈ 1.5 (a 1/r^{3/2} force law, intermediate between Newtonian gravity and a Coulomb-like 1/r law). Static rings collapse to the centre on a timescale T ∝ r₀^{5/4}, and orbital energetics with the corresponding potential V(r) = -2G/√r reproduce the observed bound and unbound trajectories. The mutual interactions among the ring particles are negligible (or strictly absent): only the centre acts as a source of force, and the centre itself is essentially fixed at the origin.

Particle 2 experiences a purely radial central acceleration directed toward the fixed source particle 1, with magnitude (p1/p2)·(1/(r²+α) + β). The factor p1/p2 means particle 1's scalar property acts as a "charge" while particle 2's acts as inertia. The 1/(r²+α) term is a softened inverse-square attraction (gravitational-like but regularised at the origin by α ≈ 0.04), and the additive constant β ≈ 0.1 represents a uniform radial background pull that dominates at large separations — analogous to a confining or cosmological-constant-like term that prevents the force from vanishing far from the source.

The probe particle moves under a central, attractive force directed toward a fixed source at the origin. The radial acceleration has the form (p1/p2)·(A + B/(r² + ε²)): a constant long-range component A ≈ 0.1 (a uniform "pull" felt even at infinity) added to a softened inverse-square core B/(r²+ε²) with B ≈ 1 and a small regularisation length ε² ≈ 0.04 that prevents the force from diverging at the origin. The source "charge" p1 couples linearly to the field strength, while the probe's parameter p2 acts as inertial mass (acceleration ∝ 1/p2), so doubling p2 halves the response while doubling p1 doubles it.

The probe particle is attracted radially toward the fixed source at the origin. The acceleration magnitude is (p1/p2)·(1/r² + α), where α ≈ 0.1 represents a constant uniform background field, and the 1/r² term is a standard Newtonian inverse-square attraction. The probe couples to the force through 1/p2 (inertia), while the source strength scales linearly with p1; the force is always directed along the line connecting the two particles, toward the source.

Particle 1 (fixed at origin) generates a static central field that acts on the probe particle 2 with a force magnitude (p1/p2)·(A/r² + B) directed from particle 2 toward particle 1. The 1/r² piece is a standard Newton/Coulomb-like attraction dominating at short range, while the constant additive term B is distance-independent and dominates at large separations (verified out to r≈50, where acceleration plateaus near B). The probe responds with an effective inertia ∝ p2 and couples linearly to the source charge p1, so the acceleration scales as p1/p2.

The probe particle (particle 2) appears to be attracted toward the fixed source particle (particle 1) by a central inverse-square force whose magnitude scales linearly with the source's property p1, analogous to Newtonian gravity in 2D. The probe's own property p2 does not influence its dynamics in any of my experiments. The data showed bounded oscillatory orbits whose magnitude grew with smaller separations and with larger p1, consistent with a Kepler-like attractive 1/r² potential; a small softening length is included to regularise the singularity near the source.

The system is a Newtonian-gravity-like 2D universe: particle 1 sits fixed at the origin and exerts an attractive central force on particle 2 whose magnitude follows the inverse-square law F = G·p1·p2/r², directed along the line between the two particles. The scalar properties p1 and p2 act as gravitational/charge couplings multiplicatively, so the acceleration of particle 2 is a = -G·p1·p2·(r2-r1)/|r2-r1|³ and is independent of any "mass" of particle 2 (i.e., p2 enters the force the same way p1 does, exactly as masses do in Newtonian gravity once we treat a = F/m with m∝p2). Observation noise on positions and velocities varied substantially between sessions, but the underlying law is the clean radial inverse-square attraction with strength G≈1.

The probe particle experiences a central attractive radial force pointed toward the fixed source particle at the origin. The force magnitude follows a power-law decay with distance (F ∝ 1/r^n), with the coupling depending multiplicatively on the two particle properties p1 and p2. Empirically, the dynamics exhibits strong sensitivity to initial conditions (small changes in r₀ or initial velocity produce very different terminal velocities), suggestive of near-singular behavior at the origin which I regularized with a softening parameter ε. The functional form is consistent with attractive radial dynamics that decays at large distances (particles starting far away show negligible motion) while exhibiting strong acceleration in the intermediate range r∈[1,3].

The dynamics of particle 2 follow standard two-dimensional Newtonian inverse-square gravity sourced by particle 1: the acceleration is directed radially toward particle 1 with magnitude G·p1/r². The probe's own property p2 plays no role in its motion (test-particle limit, as if its inertial and "gravitational" masses cancel). Verified by: (i) the energy/velocity at radius r matching the 1/r potential conservation law in radial infall experiments, (ii) exact circular orbits when v_tangential = √(p1/r) (e.g. r=4, v=0.5 traced a circle of period 2π·r/v), and (iii) linear scaling of the acceleration with p1.

Particle 2 moves under a central attractive force directed toward particle 1, which is pinned at the origin. The acceleration follows an inverse-square law in distance with a coupling that is symmetric in the two scalar properties (G·p1·p2/r²), as evidenced by experiments where swapping p1 and p2 produced identical trajectories. A small softening length ε is included to regularise the singularity at the origin, since particles in the data appear to pass through the origin and continue with finite velocity rather than diverging.

The system implements a 2D Newtonian-style gravitational attraction: particle 2 is pulled toward the fixed particle 1 with acceleration of magnitude G·p1·p2/r², directed along the line between them. The coupling is symmetric and bilinear in the two scalar properties (verified by p1↔p2 swap giving identical trajectories), and energy/angular-momentum conservation along with the v² = 2k(1/r − 1/r₀) relation were verified across many trajectories at moderate separations. Near r→0 the simulator exhibits numerical singularity artefacts (large kicks producing apparent escape velocities), which I model with a small softening length ε so the equations of motion remain regular.

The system is governed by a scalar field where each particle sources a Coulomb-like 1/r potential proportional to its own (signed) charge q_j, but particles are passively transported by the gradient of this field independent of their own charge. The acceleration on particle i is a_i = Σ_{j≠i} q_j (r_i − r_j) / |r_i − r_j|^3 — a positive q_j repels every other particle isotropically (1/r² strength) while a negative q_j attracts them. This violates Newton's third law: forces between two particles are generally not equal and opposite (e.g. a charged particle exerts force on a neutral one, but feels none back), giving a non-conservative, asymmetric many-body dynamics.

The universe contains 10 particles interacting through an inverse-square central force whose source is a signed scalar charge. Unlike Coulomb's law, the acceleration of particle i depends only on the charges of the OTHER particles (the "sources"), not on its own charge or mass: a_i = Σ_j q_j (r_i − r_j) / |r_i − r_j|³. A zero-charge particle therefore exerts no influence on others but is still accelerated by the field of charged particles, breaking Newton's third law in this universe.

The system behaves like a gravity-style inverse-square interaction but with a crucial asymmetry: the acceleration of particle i is determined only by the signed charges of the OTHER particles, not by its own charge. Specifically, a_i = G·Σ_{j≠i} q_j (r_i − r_j)/|r_i − r_j|³, with G ≈ 1. Positive source charges push test particles away (repulsive field) and negative sources pull them in; because a test particle's own charge does not enter, even neutral (q=0) particles are accelerated by charged ones, and Newton's third law is violated for unequal-charge pairs.

The 10 particles interact through an inverse-square central field with a peculiar twist: each particle's acceleration depends only on the charges of the OTHER particles (the "sources"), not on its own charge. Specifically, a_i = Σ_j q_j (r_i − r_j)/|r_i − r_j|^3, so positive charges repel everything and negative charges attract everything, like a one-sided Coulomb/gravitational field. This explicitly breaks Newton's third law (e.g. a particle with q=0 feels forces but exerts none, and a (+,−) pair both accelerate in the same direction), and is consistent with a scalar field sourced by signed charges where the test-particle response is universal rather than charge-weighted.

The system implements a non-reciprocal inverse-square interaction: each particle feels an acceleration determined solely by the source charges of the OTHER particles, not by its own charge. Specifically, a_i = Σ_{j≠i} q_j · (r_i - r_j)/|r_i - r_j|^3, so positive charges push every other particle (including neutral ones) outward radially, while negative charges attract them. Because the receiver's charge does not appear in the formula, equal-and-opposite charges do not produce equal-and-opposite forces — Newton's third law fails and momentum is not conserved, a distinctive structural feature of this universe.

This 2D world implements logarithmic ("planar") gravity: the gravitational potential of a point mass goes as Φ(r) = G·m·ln(r), so the force on a test particle is radial with magnitude G·m/r — falling as 1/r rather than the 1/r² of three-dimensional Newtonian gravity. There are 20 background point masses (each with mass m ≈ 0.55, giving total GM ≈ 11) that interact pairwise through this 1/r force, plus 5 massless probe particles that experience the summed field but do not source it. Far-field measurements at r = 50, 100, 200, 300 cleanly verified the 1/r law, with probe accelerations satisfying a·r ≈ 11 independent of distance.

The simulated universe appears to be a 2D Newtonian gravitational N-body system where every particle (background and probe alike) attracts every other particle with a softened inverse-square law F = G·(r_j - r_i)/(|r_j - r_i|² + ε²)^(3/2). The 20 background particles have non-zero initial velocities and evolve self-consistently among themselves; the probes are passive test particles whose trajectories are determined by the time-varying gravitational field of the moving background. The two free parameters are the gravitational coupling G and a softening length ε that regularises close encounters.

The universe contains 20 background particles plus 5 probes interacting through a 2D gravitational field whose force decreases as 1/r (i.e., a logarithmic potential, the natural Green's function of the 2D Laplacian). Each particle generates an attractive force on every other particle of magnitude G·m/r directed along the connecting line, with all background masses approximately equal (G·m_total ≈ 11.1 was measured from far-field probes at distances 100–500). The probes are passive: they feel the field of the background but exert no reciprocal force, so the background dynamics evolve independently as a self-gravitating cluster while the probes are advected through the field.

This universe exhibits 2D logarithmic gravity. Each of the 20 background particles acts as a point source of a logarithmic potential, φ_i = -m·ln(r_i), so the gravitational force between any two interacting bodies has magnitude G·m_i·m_j/r — falling off as 1/r rather than the 1/r² of standard 3D Newtonian gravity. The five probe particles are massless tracers: they experience the same pair-wise force from each background source but contribute nothing to the field, while the 20 background particles are mutually self-gravitating under the same law. Far-field calibration of single isolated probes at r = 20 to r = 200 showed |a|·r ≈ 11 (constant), confirming the 1/r dependence; the per-particle source strength comes out to roughly 0.55, consistent with G≈1, m≈0.55 (or any equivalent product G·m).

The simulated universe behaves as a 2D self-gravitating N-body system. The 20 background particles attract one another with a Newtonian inverse-square force a_i = G·Σ_{j≠i} (r_j − r_i)/|r_j − r_i|³ where each background body carries unit mass and the gravitational coupling is G ≈ 4. The five probes are massless tracers that experience the same attractive field but contribute nothing back, so the field they feel comes only from the (dynamically evolving) background population, while their own positions are obtained by integrating Newton's second law along their trajectories.

The simulated universe appears to govern particle 2's motion by a third-order equation of motion: the *jerk* (time derivative of acceleration), rather than acceleration itself, is sourced by a Newton-like central pull from particle 1. The pull has magnitude k·(p1/p2)/r^n directed toward the origin, with initial acceleration set to zero. This explains the otherwise puzzling observations: particles released from rest start with zero acceleration and only slowly build up velocity (matching the v∝t³-like growth seen at large times), particles moving at large speed continue with little deflection (because the high-frequency response is suppressed by the integrating structure), and the strength scales linearly with the source charge p1 and inversely with the probe's "inertia" parameter p2.

The source particle (particle 1) sits at the origin and injects a scalar field into the 2D plane starting at t=0. The field obeys the 2D heat/diffusion equation with diffusivity D, so its value at distance r and time t is the heat kernel phi(r,t) = (p1 / (4 pi D t)) * exp(-r^2 / (4 D t)). The probe particle (particle 2) has inertia p2 and accelerates down the field's spatial gradient, yielding a radial pull a = -(p1 / (2 pi D p2 r)) * exp(-r^2/(4 D t)); this naturally explains the observed time delay (force is exponentially small until t ~ r^2/(4D)), the linear scaling in p1, the 1/p2 mass-like scaling, and the long-time approach to a 2D log-potential-like force.

The probe particle experiences a one-sided, velocity-coupled radial force from the source particle: when (and only when) the probe is moving inward (v·r̂ < 0), it receives a radial acceleration along r̂ with magnitude proportional to (p1/p2)·|v_r|, with no distance dependence. The force vanishes for outward or purely tangential motion, behaving as a "rectified anti-damping" — small inward velocities grow exponentially while outward and perpendicular components propagate freely. The coupling p1/p2 indicates p1 acts like a source strength and p2 like an inertial mass scaling the response.

Particle 1 acts as a constant point source (strength p1) of a scalar field obeying the 2D heat/diffusion equation, switched on at t=0. The Green's function gives a field φ(r,t) ∝ p1·E1(r²/(4Dt)), so the gradient produces a radial attractive force on the probe of magnitude (K·p1)/(2π·D·r)·exp(-r²/(4Dt)). The probe's mass p2 enters Newton's second law: a = F/p2. The exp(-r²/(4Dt)) factor captures the diffusive "arrival" of the field — the force starts near zero and asymptotes to the steady-state 1/r form as t→∞.

Particle 1 acts as a point source of a scalar field that propagates by 2D diffusion (heat equation) rather than instantaneously. The field at particle 2's location is the time-integrated 2D heat kernel from the source, which produces a radial acceleration proportional to exp(-r²/(4Dt))/r — vanishing at early times (the "front" of diffusing influence hasn't arrived) and approaching a 1/r logarithmic-potential force at late times. The probe particle accelerates inward with inertial mass p2 and coupling strength p1, giving a = -k·p1·exp(-r²/(4Dt))/(p2·r^(n+1)) where the exponential Gaussian-style retardation explains the strongly time-dependent build-up of force we observed.

The universe contains a single massive 'anchor' at the origin that sources a 2D logarithmic gravitational potential V(r) = K·ln(r), giving every other particle a radial acceleration of magnitude K/r toward the anchor — crucially, independent of either the source mass (it's a fixed coupling K ≈ 7.96, not G·M) or the test particle's own mass. This is confirmed by probes at different radii showing a·r = constant, and by probes of mass 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8 at different positions showing identical a = K/r. On top of this central field acts a small uniform background acceleration g ≈ 0.05 in the +y direction (a constant gravitational "wind"); the 20 ring orbiters move under the same law and act as test particles whose own gravitational influence on the probes is negligible.

The dynamics are dominated by a single very heavy "anchor" particle that produces a 2D logarithmic-potential force on every other particle: the acceleration on a probe at displacement r from the anchor is −K·r̂/r (i.e. force per unit mass falls off as 1/r, not 1/r²). Probe acceleration is independent of the probe's own mass, and the orbiters and probes do not measurably source the field — only the anchor does. Superimposed on this central field is a small uniform external acceleration (≈ +0.05 in the y-direction) acting on all probes regardless of position.

The simulated world has a 2D logarithmic-gravity field sourced solely by the heavy anchor at the origin. Every other particle (ring orbiters and probes alike) feels acceleration a = −k r̂ / r toward the anchor — a 1/r force law corresponding to a logarithmic potential φ(r) = k ln r — with coupling k ≈ 7.957. Strikingly, neither source masses (orbiters) nor test masses (probes) enter the law: ring orbiters do not gravitate on probes nor on each other (confirmed by probes near and inside the ring still obeying a·r = −k cleanly), and probes of different mass at the same position acquire identical accelerations. The orbiter ring is in a circular orbit with v² = k, consistent with the centripetal balance for this 1/r force.

The universe contains one dominant "anchor" mass that sources a logarithmic gravitational potential — particles feel a central acceleration of magnitude K/r (instead of the Newtonian K/r²) directed toward the anchor, which produces circular orbits at uniform speed v = √K (≈2.82, hence the ring orbiters' constant tangential speed). On top of this, every particle experiences an additional uniform background acceleration g (essentially a constant gravity-like field, primarily in the +y direction with magnitude ≈ 0.05). Inertial masses cancel from the equations of motion entirely — the lighter particles' masses do not matter for trajectories, only the anchor's role as the central source matters, so contributions from the orbiter-orbiter pairwise terms are negligible compared to the anchor's logarithmic field plus the uniform background.

The universe contains a single central "anchor" mass that sources a 2D logarithmic gravitational potential — i.e. a force whose magnitude falls as 1/r and whose direction is purely radial toward the anchor. The coupling to test particles is independent of the test particle's inertial mass (acceleration ≈ K/r with K ≈ 8, regardless of probe mass). Superposed on this central field is a spatially uniform background acceleration of magnitude g_y ≈ 0.05 pointing in +y, which acts identically on every particle (probes and orbiters alike) — analogous to a uniform gravitational "wind". Probes do not appreciably interact with each other or with the smaller orbiters; only the anchor's central field plus the uniform background determine probe trajectories.

The universe contains a single source — the massive anchor at the origin — that generates a 2D logarithmic gravitational potential. Every other particle (ring orbiters and probes alike) feels an acceleration a = −K r̂ / r pointing toward the anchor, with K ≈ 7.95 fixed by the world (independent of both the source mass and the test particle's mass, which is why probes of any mass follow identical trajectories and why the ring orbiters at r=5 sit on circular orbits with v² = K). Particles do NOT attract one another — only the anchor sources the field — so all 26 trajectories decouple into independent two-body problems with the anchor.

The simulated universe is a 2D world with logarithmic gravity: the gravitational potential of a point mass scales as Φ ∝ G·m·ln(r), producing an attractive force that decays as 1/r rather than 1/r² (consistent with Gauss's law in 2D). Every particle accelerates toward every other by a_i = Σ_j G·m_j·(r_j-r_i)/|r_j-r_i|², so the test particle's own (inertial) mass cancels — gravity is universal. The anchor's enormous mass (~10¹⁵) sets G·M_anchor ≈ 8, giving radius-independent circular speed v = √8 ≈ 2.828 — exactly the orbital speed at which the 20 ring particles travel — while the ring orbiters contribute only tiny perturbations.

The universe has a 2D logarithmic-potential gravity: each particle generates an attractive acceleration field that falls off as 1/r (rather than 1/r² as in 3D Newtonian gravity), so the force vector from source j on target i is -G·m_j·(r_i - r_j)/|r_i - r_j|². Accelerations from all sources sum linearly, but crucially there is no division by the target particle's own mass — every particle experiences the same acceleration field regardless of its inertia. The anchor's gigantic mass (~10¹⁵) makes it dominate the field everywhere while its own response to other particles is negligible; the ring orbiters at radius 5 follow nearly circular orbits sustained by this 1/r central force with G ≈ 8×10⁻¹⁵.

The central anchor (particle 0) is the sole significant source of force in this universe. It generates a logarithmic gravitational potential Φ(r) = K·ln(r), producing a radial acceleration a = -K·r̂/r (a 1/r force law) on every test particle, independent of the test particle's mass. Empirically K ≈ 8. The 20 ring orbiters have masses many orders of magnitude smaller than the anchor's effective coupling and contribute negligibly to probe trajectories; what initially looked like a uniform +y gravitational field turned out to be a systematic ~0.1·t bias in the reported velocities (visible even on the immovable anchor itself), not a real physical effect.

This universe contains a single very-massive "anchor" that sources a 2D logarithmic gravitational potential — i.e. a radial force whose acceleration magnitude falls off as 1/r (so that a·r = K ≈ 8 everywhere). All other particles, regardless of their own inertial mass, experience the same acceleration field a = -K·r̂/r pointing toward the anchor; the orbiters with masses 1, 2, 4 circle the anchor at the universal orbital speed √K ≈ 2.83 at radius 5. Inter-particle gravity between non-anchor particles is absent (or negligibly small): probes do not feel the orbiters or each other, only the anchor's logarithmic well.

Particle 2 experiences a purely radial attractive force from the fixed source particle 1. The force per unit probe-property p2 is (p1)*(A*exp(-r/L)/r^2 + B/r): a short-range Yukawa-like piece with screening length L≈1 and coefficient A≈0.28, plus a long-range 1/r tail with coefficient B≈1/(2π)≈0.159 — characteristic of a 2D logarithmic Coulomb potential. The probe's mass is proportional to p2, so acceleration scales as p1/p2.

The probe particle moves in a central attractive field generated by a fixed source. The acceleration has two pieces: a long-range 2D-Coulomb-like term A/r (which would arise from a logarithmic potential in 2D, the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian) and a short-range Yukawa-style exponential correction B·exp(-r/L)/r² that boosts the force at small separations. The source charge is p1 and the probe's inertial mass is p2, so acceleration = (p1/p2)·[A/r + B·exp(-r/L)/r²] directed toward the source. The fitted constants are roughly A ≈ 1/(2π), B ≈ 0.35, and screening length L ≈ 0.8–1.0.

Particle 2 in this 2D universe is attracted to particle 1 by a purely radial acceleration that scales as p1/p2 (so p1 acts as a charge and p2 as inertial mass). The acceleration magnitude is A·(1 + K·exp(-β r))/r with A ≈ 1/(2π): at large distances it reduces to the 2D Coulomb / massless-log force 1/(2π r), while at short distances an exponentially screened (Yukawa-like) component dominates and pulls the particle in much more strongly. This is consistent with a scalar field theory whose propagator is the sum of a massless 2D Green's function (logarithmic potential, giving the long-range 1/r force) and a massive Yukawa-type contribution that decays exponentially with characteristic length 1/β.

The source particle (charge p1, fixed at origin) generates a central field that pulls the probe (inertial mass p2) inward. The radial acceleration magnitude follows a = (1/(2π)) · (p1/p2) · coth(r)/r, which behaves like a 1/r² Newtonian-style force at short distances (where coth(r) → 1/r) and crosses over to a logarithmic-potential 1/r force at large distances (where coth(r) → 1). This functional form is the field profile of a screened source on a periodic/cylindrical 2D geometry, with the 1/(2π) prefactor being the natural 2D Green's-function normalization.

Particle 2 feels a purely radial attractive force from the fixed particle 1, with acceleration magnitude (p1/p2)·[A/r + B·exp(−r/L)/r²]. The long-range 1/r piece (A ≈ 0.16) is the 2D-Coulomb / logarithmic-potential tail — confirmed by the asymptote a·r → 0.16 across r = 4–10 — meaning p1 acts like a 2D point charge sourcing a log potential. The short-range exp(−r/L)/r² piece (B ≈ 0.27, L ≈ 1) is a screened Yukawa-like core that dominates at small r (where measured a·r² approaches a finite constant) and dies off exponentially by r ≈ 3, with p1 acting as source charge and p2 as inertial mass.

The probe particle (particle 2) experiences a central attractive force directed toward the fixed source particle (particle 1) at the origin. The acceleration magnitude scales linearly with the source's property p1 and inversely with the probe's property p2, decaying as 1/r² with separation — a Newtonian inverse-square law with a coupling constant G ≈ 0.6 in this universe. The data also showed weak oscillatory residuals at intermediate distances that hint at an additional wave/field contribution, but the dominant clean signal at small to moderate separations is captured by the inverse-square radial form.

Particle 1 sources a scalar field that propagates outward as a damped oscillation in radius, A·cos(k·r+φ)/r^α, producing a central (radial) force on the probe particle 2. The force is non-conservative and shows a four-fold angular modulation (cos(4θ) pattern from the (x⁴+y⁴−6x²y²)/r⁴ term), indicating that the source emits anisotropically along the coordinate axes rather than isotropically. The probe's response scales linearly with the source coupling p1 and inversely with its own "inertia" p2.

The source particle (p1) creates a 2D logarithmic potential well V(r) ∝ p1·ln(r)/p2, yielding an attractive radial acceleration of magnitude G·p1/(p2·r) on the probe. This is the natural Coulomb-like solution to the 2D Poisson equation, where charges couple linearly through p1 (source strength) and the probe responds inversely through its own p2 (effective inertia). Additionally, the probe experiences an isotropic linear drag −γv from an ambient medium, which damps tangential motion (e.g. reduces orbital speed over time) without affecting the radial law itself.

Particle 2 experiences a central attractive acceleration toward the origin (where particle 1 sits). The magnitude is an inverse-square law in r, scaled linearly by p1 and inversely by p2, but modulated by a Cartesian standing-wave factor [A + B·cos(k·x)·cos(k·y)] with k = 2π/5. This product-of-cosines factor introduces angular asymmetries and radial oscillation: along the axes the modulation is cos(kr), while at off-axis points the two-coordinate factorization produces a pattern reminiscent of a checkerboard background field that the particle couples to via 1/r².

Particle 2 is attracted radially toward the fixed particle 1, but the force strength is not rotationally symmetric: it is modulated by a periodic anisotropic factor cos(kx)·cos(ky) in Cartesian coordinates with wavenumber k = 2π/5. The acceleration has the form a = −(p1/p2)·[α + β·cos(kx)·cos(ky)]·r̂/r, with α = 0.16, β = 0.08; particle 1's property p1 acts as a source charge (linear coupling) and p2 acts as an inertia/screening factor (inverse coupling). Physically this resembles a particle moving in the gradient of a logarithmic potential threaded by a periodic standing-wave background — the strength of the attraction depends on which lobe of the 5-unit-period checkerboard the probe happens to be in.

Particle 1 sources a static central inverse-square force field on particle 2. The acceleration of particle 2 points radially toward particle 1 with magnitude p1/(2π·p2·r²): the source charge p1 acts as the strength, the probe charge p2 appears as inertia in the denominator (so p2 behaves like a mass divided into the force), and the 1/(2π) prefactor is the universal coupling constant. The force is purely conservative and velocity-independent — no magnetic/Lorentz coupling was detected — and the sign of p1 controls attraction vs repulsion.

The source particle (p1) generates a static central potential whose gradient produces an attractive inverse-square acceleration on the probe particle, with magnitude K·p1/(p2·r²) and direction along −r̂ (toward the source). The scalar p1 acts as a "charge" of the source, while the probe's scalar p2 functions as inertial mass, so that the force on the probe scales as K·p1/r² and dividing by mass p2 yields the acceleration. The fitted prefactor K ≈ 0.16 sets the overall coupling strength, and the dynamics reduce to standard Keplerian orbits in this central field.

The discovered law is a Newtonian-style inverse-square central force: particle 2 is attracted to the fixed particle 1 with acceleration magnitude G·p1/(p2·r²) pointing radially toward particle 1. Here p1 plays the role of a "gravitational charge" of the source while p2 plays the role of inertial mass of the probe (it appears in the denominator of the acceleration, just as mass would in F=ma with F∝p1). The gravitational constant G is approximately 0.16 in these units, and the law contains no velocity dependence or screening—it is pure 1/r² attraction in 2D.

Particle 1 acts as a static source generating a central inverse-square attractive field in the 2D plane, with field strength scaling linearly with its scalar property p1 and a universal coupling constant of 1/(2π). Particle 2 responds to this field with acceleration a = -(1/(2π))·(p1/p2)·r̂/r², where p2 plays the role of inertial mass (the force is proportional to p1/p2). The 1/(2π) prefactor is suggestive of a 2D Green's-function normalization, though the radial dependence is 1/r² rather than the 1/r typical of pure 2D Poisson — so this universe behaves like 3D gravity/Coulomb embedded in a 2D plane with a flux-normalized coupling.

The source particle creates a static central potential at the origin, and the probe particle is attracted along the line connecting them with an acceleration of magnitude G·p1/(p2·r²). This is structurally a Newtonian inverse-square attraction where p1 plays the role of source charge/mass and p2 acts as the probe's inertia (force ∝ p1, acceleration ∝ 1/p2). No velocity-dependent forces were detected; the fitted coupling constant G is approximately 0.16.

Particle 1 sits at the origin and sources a 2D logarithmic potential φ(r) = (p1/(2π)) ln(r) — the Green's function of the Laplacian in two dimensions. Particle 2 couples to this field through its inertia p2, experiencing an attractive radial force F = -p1/(2π r) r̂ and hence acceleration a = -p1/(2π p2) · r̂/r. This is the genuine 2D analogue of Newtonian gravity (force ∝ 1/r rather than 1/r²), and the prefactor 1/(2π) is the universal 2D Green's-function constant.

Particle 1 sits at the origin and sources a radial field whose strength decays as 1/r — the natural 2D analogue of Newtonian gravity (logarithmic potential, like the field around an infinite line charge in 3D). The probe particle 2 experiences an attractive acceleration directed toward the origin with magnitude k·p1/(p2·r), where p1 acts like a source charge/mass, p2 acts like inertia (so the force has p1·p2 form but acceleration scales as p1/p2), and the coupling constant k ≈ 0.159 ≈ 1/(2π). The force is purely central and independent of the probe's velocity.

The system behaves as a 2D electrostatics analogue. Particle 1 sources a logarithmic scalar potential φ(r) = (p1/2π)·ln(r) — the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian — and particle 2 moves under the gradient force a = -∇φ = -p1·r̂/(2πr). The probe's own property p2 does not couple to the field, so the acceleration is independent of p2 and exactly linear in p1 with magnitude 1/(2πr).

Particle 1 (fixed at the origin) sources a 2D logarithmic potential φ(r) = k·(p1/p2)·ln(r). The probe particle 2 experiences the corresponding central force, giving radial acceleration a_r = -k·(p1/p2)/r — i.e. force falls off as 1/r rather than 1/r² as in 3D Newtonian gravity, which is the natural Green's-function behaviour of the Laplacian in two dimensions. The coupling depends linearly on the source strength p1 and inversely on the probe property p2 (so p2 plays the role of an inertial-like mass), with the coefficient k ≈ 1/(2π).

The probe particle moves under a 2D logarithmic potential sourced by the fixed particle: phi(r) = (p1/(2*pi)) * ln(r). Particle 2 has inertia given by its scalar property p2, so its acceleration is a = -(p1/(2*pi*p2)) * r_hat / r — a central, attractive force whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (the natural Green's function of the 2D Laplacian), with the source strength scaling linearly with p1 and the response scaling inversely with p2.

The universe contains 10 point particles in a 2D plane that interact through a pairwise attractive logarithmic potential — the 2D analogue of the Coulomb force. Each particle accelerates toward every other particle with magnitude C/r (where r is the inter-particle separation), giving the equation of motion a_i = C · Σ_{j≠i} (r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|². This is precisely the gradient of a logarithmic potential, the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian, so each particle acts as a source of a scalar field obeying a 2D Poisson equation. The coupling constant C ≈ 0.16 (close to 1/(2π)).

The 10 particles interact through a 2D Coulomb / logarithmic potential: the field obeys ∇²φ = -ρ in two dimensions, whose Green's function is a logarithm, so each particle pulls every other with an attractive force F = G·(r_j-r_i)/|r_j-r_i|² (magnitude G/r). The coupling constant G is approximately 1/(2π) ≈ 0.16, consistent with the standard 2D Poisson Green's function. Crucially, each particle carries an index-dependent inertial mass that doubles in blocks of roughly three: particles 0–3 have mass 1, particles 4–6 have mass 2, and particles 7–9 have mass 4, so the same pairwise force produces visibly smaller accelerations on the higher-index particles while still conserving total momentum.

The universe implements two-dimensional electrostatics with universal attractive "charges": every particle sources a logarithmic potential φ(r) = -(1/2π) ln(r), the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian. Each particle is then accelerated by the gradient of the total field summed over all other particles, giving a pairwise attractive force of magnitude 1/(2π r) along the connecting line. No velocity-dependent or non-pairwise terms were observed — the dynamics are simply Newtonian motion under this 2D Coulomb-like attraction.

The 10 particles in this 2D universe attract each other pairwise with a force of magnitude F = k/r (with k ≈ 1/(2π)), directed along the line connecting them — this is the 2D analogue of gravity/Coulomb interaction, derivable from a logarithmic potential φ ∝ ln(r). Particles have quantised inertial masses in three tiers (m=1 for indices 0–3, m=2 for indices 4–6, m=4 for indices 7–9), so accelerations differ for otherwise-identical pair interactions: a_i = F_i/m_i via Newton's second law. There is no background field — distant particles influence a target purely through summed 1/r pair forces, which I verified by reproducing both isolated-pair accelerations and the small drifts induced by particles placed hundreds of units away.

The 10 particles interact through a 2D logarithmic (Coulomb-in-2D) potential: each particle sources a scalar field obeying the 2D Laplace equation, so the pairwise force on particle i from particle j points toward j with magnitude c/|r_i-r_j|, where c ≈ 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159. Different particles carry different inertial masses, organised into three index-based groups: particles 0-3 have mass 1, particles 4-6 have mass 2, and particles 7-9 have mass 4, so each particle's acceleration is the total field force divided by its mass. All charges are equal and attractive (no repulsion observed in any pairing).

The universe has a fixed radial field centered on the immobile anchor at the origin. Every particle's acceleration is purely radial with magnitude g(r) = alpha*r - beta/r, where r is the distance from the anchor. This combines a linear "anti-Hooke" outward push (alpha*r) with an attractive 1/r pull (-beta/r), producing a stable circular orbit radius at r = sqrt(beta/alpha) ≈ 12.6 (where the field vanishes); inside this radius particles are pulled inward, outside they are flung outward. Crucially, the acceleration is independent of the particle's own mass and of every other particle's mass — only the anchor's location matters — so the 20 ring orbiters and the probes all decouple and move in this single static central field. Fitted constants: alpha ≈ 0.05, beta ≈ 7.96; the ring orbiters at r=5 with v≈2.59 satisfy v² = beta - alpha*r² = 6.71 exactly.

This 2D universe uses a logarithmic gravitational potential, so the force between any two massive particles is attractive and falls off as 1/r (rather than 1/r² as in 3D Newtonian gravity); accelerations from neighbours sum vectorially with strength G·m_j·(r_j−r_i)/|r_j−r_i|². Superposed on this is a position-dependent "cosmological repulsion" Λ·r pointing away from the origin — a Hooke-like anti-restoring acceleration that dominates at large distance and would drive runaway exponential expansion in the absence of gravity. Test particles couple only through their position (acceleration is independent of their own mass), and the very massive central anchor (M ≈ 10¹⁵, with G·M ≈ 8) sets a near-Keplerian inner region where 1/r attraction balances Λ·r repulsion at r ≈ 12–13, giving the observed circular orbits at r=5 with v ≈ 2.59.

The 2D universe has a single fixed central potential pinned at the anchor: every particle (anchor, ring orbiters, probes) experiences a purely radial acceleration toward the origin equal to A/r − B·r, with A≈8 and B≈0.05. The 1/r piece behaves like 2D logarithmic gravity from the very massive anchor, while the linear repulsive piece acts like an anti-Hooke "cosmological" outward push from a uniform negative-density background. The acceleration is independent of probe mass and of the masses/positions of other particles (probes don't gravitate on each other), and the ring orbiters maintain rigid circular rotation at radius 5 because at that radius A/r − B·r exactly supplies the required centripetal acceleration ω²r ≈ 1.34.

The anchor at the origin sources a static, radial scalar field with potential V(r) = A·ln(r) − (B/2)·r², producing acceleration a⃗ = (−A/r + B·r)·r̂ on every other particle. Short-range behavior is logarithmic attraction (∝ 1/r toward the anchor), while long-range behavior is harmonic repulsion (∝ r outward), with an unstable equilibrium at r = √(A/B). All non-anchor particles (background orbiters and probes) are passive test particles in this field — they do not source forces on one another, and their inertial mass cancels out so that acceleration depends only on position relative to the anchor (best-fit constants A ≈ 8, B ≈ 0.05).

The universe is 2D with a modified gravitational field: each particle sources a logarithmic gravitational potential (so the gravitational acceleration toward a source of mass M at distance r is G·M/r, not G·M/r²), and there is a universal cosmological-constant-like outward acceleration B·r proportional to position from the origin. The total acceleration on any particle is the sum of pairwise log-gravity attractions plus this de Sitter–style repulsion, giving an effective radial acceleration around the anchor of a(r) = -A/r + B·r with A = G·M_anchor ≈ 7.95 and B ≈ 0.05, producing a stable orbital radius near r ≈ √(A/B) ≈ 12.6 beyond which particles are flung outward. Probe acceleration is independent of probe inertial mass (equivalence principle holds).

The universe has a single static central field centered at the anchor (origin) whose potential is U(r) = -A r²/2 + B ln(r), producing a purely radial acceleration a_r = A·r - B/r on every particle, with A ≈ 0.0493 and B ≈ 7.95. The force is independent of both the source masses and the test particle's mass — particles are massless test charges in a fixed external field. This gives attraction for r < √(B/A) ≈ 12.7 (1/r term dominates, like 2D gravity/logarithmic confinement) and repulsion for larger r (harmonic anti-spring), so the 20 ring orbiters at r=5 sit deep in the attractive region and orbit circularly with speed √(B - A r²) ≈ 2.59, while probes far away are flung outward.

The simulated universe behaves like a 2D world with two ingredients: (1) a uniform "dark-energy"/cosmological background that gives every particle a mass-independent outward acceleration H²·r from the origin (with H² ≈ 0.05), explaining the observed asymptote a/r → 0.05 at large distances; and (2) ordinary attractive gravity between matter particles using a 2D logarithmic potential, i.e. force per mass F = G·mⱼ/|r_ij| (inverse-first-power, α = 1), with G ≈ 0.178. The single ultra-massive anchor at the origin is a pure cosmological landmark — it does not participate in gravitational attraction, otherwise its 10¹⁵ mass would dwarf everything; the ring of 20 orbiters (total mass 45) supplies an inward 2D-Gauss-law contribution G·M_ring/r on probes outside the ring, matching the measured a ≈ 0.05·r − 8/r law.

The universe has a two-component central field sourced linearly by each particle's mass: a Newtonian-2D-like attractive piece producing acceleration -G·m/r along the line of sight (corresponding to a logarithmic gravitational potential, ∇²φ ∝ ρ in 2D), plus a repulsive cosmological-constant-like linear piece +Λ·m·r outward (a de-Sitter-style background that becomes dominant at large distances). Test particles' accelerations are independent of their own inertial mass and are the vector sum of contributions from all sources; because the central anchor has mass ~10¹⁵, it overwhelmingly determines probe trajectories, yielding the radial law a_r ≈ -G·M_anchor/r + Λ·M_anchor·r with a force-balance radius near r ≈ √(G/Λ) ≈ 12.5 where attraction and cosmic repulsion cancel.

The universe contains a fixed, static central force field anchored at the origin. Every particle (anchor, orbiters, probes) experiences the same purely radial acceleration a_r = −A/r + B·r with A≈8 and B≈0.05 — attractive (∝1/r) inside the equilibrium radius r_eq = √(A/B) ≈ 12.65 and repulsive (∝r) outside it. The field does not depend on the particle's inertial mass, on the masses of other particles, or on inter-particle interactions; the anchor's huge mass and the ring of orbiters are only props moving in the same field, and the ring's circular speed v² = A − B·r² confirms the law at r = 5.

Every particle in this universe feels a purely central, radial acceleration produced by a fixed field anchored at the origin: a(r) = α·r − β/r, pointing radially outward when positive. The −β/r piece is the 2D analogue of Newtonian gravity (i.e. a logarithmic potential −β·ln r), while the +α·r piece is a uniform outward "anti-gravity" term akin to a positive cosmological constant in a de-Sitter universe. Crucially, the acceleration depends only on the particle's distance from the origin — not on its inertial mass, not on the masses or positions of other particles — which is why the 20 ring orbiters all share the same angular frequency ω² = β/R² − α at the ring radius R = 5, and why probes follow identical trajectories regardless of mass.

The universe is governed by a pair-wise central force in which every particle attracts every other particle along the line joining them, with force magnitude F = G/d^p where d is the inter-particle separation and p ≈ 1.6 (i.e. a non-standard "fractional" gravity weaker than 1/r² at short distances but still divergent at d→0). Starting from the symmetric ring configuration, the centre particle is held at the origin by perfect symmetry of forces from the ten ring particles, while each ring particle experiences a net inward force that grows as it approaches the centre, causing it to plunge through and emerge with very large radial velocity on the opposite side — the "explosion" observed in the trajectories. The functional form was identified by reading initial accelerations a(r) for various ring radii (which scaled approximately as r^(−1.6)) and confirmed by the constant terminal velocities seen at large separations, indicating the force falls off (but not as 1/r²) with distance.

The 11 particles interact through a pairwise central attractive force per pair that combines a standard Newtonian inverse-square term with a slower-decaying inverse-distance term: F_ij = G/r² + H/r along the line between particles, corresponding to potential V(r) = -G/r - H·ln(r). All particles (centre included) are dynamically equivalent unit masses. Evidence: with the symmetric ring + centre configuration, the initial radial acceleration on a ring particle satisfies a·R² ≈ 4.86·G + 5.5·H·R (matching geometric sums over the 10-particle ring), which fits the measured a(R) data for R=4–15 with G≈7.4, H≈1.31; the dynamics collapse-then-explode through the origin with terminal radial speed governed by the energy released, consistent with this two-term force law.

The universe contains particles that all attract each other through a pairwise central force whose magnitude is the sum of an inverse-square Newtonian term (A/d²) and an inverse-distance term (B/d, which corresponds to a 2D logarithmic potential). The acceleration on each particle is therefore the vector sum over every other particle of (A/d³ + B/d²)·r̂. In the symmetric ring initial condition, by symmetry the central particle stays at rest while the ring contracts radially under this dual force, passes very close to the center where the inverse-square term dominates and ejects the particles outward with very large velocities, leading to the apparent runaway expansion observed at late times.

The 11 particles in this universe interact through pairwise attractive forces with magnitude proportional to 1/d^1.5 (weaker fall-off than Newtonian gravity). Each particle's acceleration is the sum over all other particles of K*(r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|^2.5. Because of this softer power, ring particles starting from rest fall inward, slingshot through their closest approach, then fly outward with nearly constant terminal velocity. The fitted coupling constant K≈6.86 produces an effective central-restoring acceleration of approximately 34.7/r^1.5 on each ring particle in the symmetric ring configuration.

The universe contains 11 identical particles that interact pairwise through an attractive central force with two contributions: an inverse-square term α/d² (analogous to 3D gravity acting in this 2D plane) plus an inverse-distance term β/d (analogous to a 2D Coulomb/log-potential force). By analyzing initial radial accelerations of the symmetric ring at five different radii, I established that the inward acceleration on a ring particle scales as a(r) = A/r² + B/r with A≈34.65, B≈7.29, which decomposes (using the geometric ring sums Σ1/sin(πk/10)=15.45 and Σ1=9 for k=1..9) into a universal pair force F(d) = α/d² + β/d with α≈7.13, β≈1.33. The force acts along the line of centers, identically on every particle (center and ring alike), with no other dynamics.

The 30 particles interact through a 2D Coulomb-like attractive field: each pair contributes a force along the unit separation vector with magnitude proportional to 1/r (corresponding to the gradient of a logarithmic potential, which is the natural "gravity" in 2D). The coupling constant c is roughly 0.165 in the universe's units. The dynamics are second-order Newtonian (acceleration = force), and the field appears scale-invariant in the sense that pairwise forces depend only on relative positions of pairs; however, longer integrations exhibit a non-conservative-looking drift at the chain ends which my model does not fully capture and which may indicate an additional non-pairwise gauge or self-field term beyond the leading attractive 1/r law.

The universe contains 30 particles in 2D coupled to a scalar field obeying the 2D Poisson equation, so a point source produces a logarithmic potential and a 1/r radial force. Every particle acts as an equal-strength source, but each particle carries an intrinsic test-charge q_i that determines how it RESPONDS to the collective field: q_i = +1 for indices 0-14 and q_i = -1 for indices 15-29. The equation of motion is d²r_i/dt² = G·q_i·Σ_{j≠i} (r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|² with G ≈ 0.16; because only the receiver's charge appears (not q_i·q_j), Newton's third law is broken — group A particles attract each other while group B particles repel each other, even though the field they generate is identical.

In this universe particles experience no direct position-based force; instead, each particle's acceleration is a weighted sum of the velocities of all other particles, with weights decaying with spatial separation (approximately Gaussian). Because the coupling matrix acts directly on v (not on v_k - v_j), the system has positive eigenmodes that grow exponentially in time, producing the characteristic index-correlated velocity patterns I observed: particles near (in index/space) a moving particle quickly acquire similar velocity, and collective modes amplify rather than dissipate. Stationary configurations are stable (no force on resting particles), but any seed velocity propagates and grows through the dense particle medium.

The simulated universe appears to have particles interacting through a pairwise attractive central force, similar to gravity but with the distance-dependence exponent (α) treated as unknown. Each particle experiences an acceleration that is the sum over all other particles of G*(r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|^α, with a small softening parameter ε to prevent singularities at close approach. While I observed unusual behaviors such as symmetric outward forces in linearly-arranged clusters that don't perfectly match standard Newtonian gravity, the inverse-power attractive law captures the basic two-body and short-range collective dynamics I observed (e.g., particles pulling toward neighbors with a strength that decays with distance).

The universe contains two species of particles distinguished by index: indices 0–14 are "attractive" (sign +1) and indices 15–29 are "repulsive" (sign −1). Each particle i feels a total force equal to sign_i · k · Σ_{j≠i} r̂_{i→j} / r_ij — that is, a 1/r central interaction where the force direction depends only on the receiver's species, not on the source's. This breaks Newton's third law: an A-B pair both accelerate in the same direction (A pulled toward B while B is pushed away from A), producing the characteristic "V-shaped" velocity profile observed in mixed chains, while pure-A chains collapse and pure-B chains explode symmetrically. The coupling constant k ≈ 0.16.

Particle 1 acts as a stationary source generating a 2D logarithmic potential V(r) = C·p1·ln(r), which is the natural Green's function of the 2D Laplacian. The probe particle 2 experiences an attractive radial force F = -C·p1/r in the r̂ direction, and its acceleration scales inversely with its own scalar property p2 (so p2 plays the role of inertial mass while p1 plays the role of source charge). Numerically the coupling constant C is approximately 0.8, and the resulting motion (verified across many initial conditions) is exactly that of a particle in a logarithmic central potential — bound orbits decay inward and unbound trajectories curve gently — independent of distance through a strict 1/r force law.

Particle 1 acts as a static source generating a 2D logarithmic potential φ(r) = G·p1·ln(r), and particle 2 experiences an acceleration a = -(G·p1/p2)·r̂/r — i.e. a radial 1/r attractive force whose strength scales linearly with the source charge p1 and inversely with the probe's "inertia" p2. The prefactor G fits to approximately π/4, consistent with a 2D Coulomb/gravitational analogue where field lines spread in a plane rather than 3D space.

Particle 1 acts as a stationary monopole source of a scalar field that pulses synchronously everywhere with angular frequency ω ≈ π/2 (period 4). The field's spatial profile decays as 1/r in this 2D world, and the probe particle 2 experiences a radial acceleration directed toward the source equal to (A·p1 / (p2·r))·cos(ω t), with coupling A ≈ π/4. Notably there is no retardation/phase delay with distance — the oscillation is globally synchronous — and the probe's property p2 acts like an inertial mass that suppresses its response.

The universe contains a time-oscillating radial scalar field sourced by particle 1. The probe particle 2 experiences a central acceleration whose magnitude scales as p1/(p2·r) (linear in the source charge p1, inverse in the probe's "inertia-like" property p2, and falling as 1/r), modulated in time by cos(ωt) with a universal angular frequency ω ≈ π/2. The associated velocity therefore oscillates as sin(ωt) with amplitude p1/(2·p2·r), so for stationary initial conditions far from the source the particle merely jitters back and forth, while close to the source it can be flung outward when the field reverses sign.

Particle 1 sits at the origin and sources a radial scalar field whose strength oscillates sinusoidally in time, F_radial(r,t) = -K·p1·cos(ω·t)/r pointing along r̂. Particle 2 couples to this field with inertial mass equal to its property p2, so its acceleration is a = -(K·p1/(p2·r²))·cos(ω·t)·r_vec. Empirically K ≈ π/4 and ω ≈ π/2, giving a universal field oscillation period of 4 time-units regardless of separation, masses, or initial conditions; this produces small-amplitude radial breathing at large r and dramatic time-modulated escapes/captures at small r.

The probe particle (p2) feels an attractive radial acceleration from the fixed source (p1) that scales linearly with p1 and inversely with p2. The radial force magnitude has two components: a screened core where g(r) = r·F saturates at a constant ≈ 6 inside a characteristic length R ≈ 0.75 (Coulomb-like 1/r behaviour at small separations, as in 2D point sources), and a slowly decaying long-range tail B/r^β with β ≈ 1.7 that dominates beyond a few R.
