This suggests the source generates a 2D field whose "enclosed charge" function rises to a finite plateau and then is partially screened — geometrically reminiscent of a finite-size source in a medium with weak anti-screening at large distances.

Particle 1 is a fixed source generating a central force field that pulls particle 2 toward it. The acceleration of particle 2 is proportional to p1 and inversely proportional to p2, with a radial dependence that combines a short-range cubic-decay term (A/r³) dominating near the source and a long-range Coulomb-like term (B/r) dominating at large distances. This dual-scale behavior — much steeper than Newtonian gravity at short range, much shallower at long range — distinguishes this universe's force law from familiar 1/r² gravity and corresponds to a potential of the form φ ∝ A/(2r²) - B·ln(r).

The probe particle feels a purely radial attractive acceleration toward the source. The magnitude scales linearly with the source charge p1 and inversely with the probe property p2 (acting as inertial mass). The radial profile has two regimes: at long range (r ≳ 1) the acceleration behaves like a sum of inverse-square and inverse-distance terms (A/r² + B/r), consistent with a 2D Coulomb/gravity-like field plus a long-range linear potential contribution. At short range there is an additional rapidly-decaying enhancement (modeled as a Gaussian/stretched-exponential ~ C exp(−(r/L)ⁿ)/r) that produces a sharp transition near r ≈ 0.75, suggesting a localized source structure (e.g. a finite-extent charge distribution or an extra screened contact field).

Particle 1 is held fixed at the origin and sources a central, radially attractive field that accelerates particle 2 inward. Experimentally the magnitude of the acceleration at rest is well-fit by G·p1/r^n with n close to 1 (a logarithmic potential in 2D), and particle 2's own scalar property p2 plays no role in its motion — it behaves as a passive test charge. The force is purely central (the perpendicular velocity component is unchanged when the probe moves along a non-radial direction), so the dynamics is described by ẍ = -G·p1·r̂/r^n.

Particle 2 experiences a purely radial attractive force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with acceleration magnitude a = K·(p1/p2)/(r·ln(c·r)). The coupling is linear in the source property p1 and inverse-linear in the probe property p2 (so p2 plays the role of inertial mass while p1 is a charge). The 1/(r·ln r) form at large r is characteristic of a 2D field with a logarithmically running coupling — the force decays slower than Coulombic 1/r² but faster than pure 1/r, producing a clean scaling a·r·ln(r) ≈ const that was directly observed across nearly two decades of separation.

The six particles live in a 2D world where the gravitational potential between two charges is logarithmic, producing an attractive radial force of magnitude G·m_i·m_j / r per pair. The resulting acceleration on particle i is a_i = G · Σ_{j≠i} m_j (r_j − r_i)/|r_j − r_i|², which is just the 2D Coulomb-like gradient of −Σ m_i m_j log r_ij. The coupling constant is G = 1/(2π); particles 0,1,2 carry mass 1 while particles 3,4,5 carry mass 3, so the two species exert asymmetric (but Newton's-third-law-consistent) accelerations on one another.

The universe contains six particles split into two species: particles 0-2 carry "light" source-charge q_light ≈ 0.165 while particles 3-5 carry "heavy" source-charge q_heavy ≈ 0.48 (about 3× larger). Each particle accelerates according to a_i = Σ_{j≠i} q_j · (r_j - r_i) / |r_j - r_i|², i.e. a 2D inverse-square vector force (equivalent to an attractive 1/r magnitude force, the logarithmic-potential analogue of 2D gravity), with the crucial feature that the receiver's own charge does not appear in its acceleration — only the *source* charges of the other particles do. This breaks Newton's third law at the pairwise level and lets the system's center of mass accelerate, which I observed directly in cross-configuration experiments.

The particles interact through a 2D logarithmic potential: each pair (i,j) attracts with force magnitude k/|r_ij| directed along the line connecting them (equivalent to F_ij = k·(r_j−r_i)/|r_j−r_i|², the gradient of −k·log|r_ij|). The pair coupling constant is approximately k ≈ 0.16 in the units provided. I observed additional label-dependent multi-body effects (forces no longer match pairwise sums when N≥4 particles cluster together, and Newton's third law appears to be violated in those cases), but did not fully resolve their functional form within the experiment budget, so the submitted law captures only the dominant pairwise log-Coulomb interaction.

The universe is a 2D Coulomb/gravity system governed by a logarithmic potential — exactly what one gets from a 2D Poisson equation (∇²φ = source). Each particle j is a fixed point source of strength q_j and produces a potential q_j·ln(r); particle i is accelerated by a_i = G · Σ_{j≠i} q_j (r_j − r_i)/|r_j − r_i|², i.e. a 1/r attractive force in 2D. The six particles are not identical: particles 0, 1, 2 carry unit charge while particles 3, 4, 5 carry charge ≈3, and the universal coupling is G ≈ 1/(2π), the standard prefactor of the 2D Green function for the Laplacian.

The simulated universe behaves like 2D gravity with a logarithmic potential: every pair of particles attracts each other with a force whose magnitude is K/r (inverse-distance, not inverse-square). Equivalently, particles experience an acceleration that is the gradient of a sum of logarithmic potentials, giving a_i = K * Σ_{j≠i} (r_j − r_i)/|r_j − r_i|². Pair experiments clearly showed F·r ≈ constant across distances spanning two orders of magnitude, with strong translation and rotation invariance and no apparent dependence on particle identity; the constant K was measured near 0.16–0.34 (left as a fittable parameter due to residual many-body discrepancies that may indicate a small higher-order correction I could not fully resolve).

The universe contains 30 massive background particles plus 5 massless probes in 2D. The background particles interact with each other (and pull on the probes) via a 2D-gravity-like law with potential proportional to log(r) — i.e., attractive force F = G*m / r along the line connecting the pair, falling off as 1/r rather than 1/r² as in 3D Newtonian gravity. Far-field measurements at r=20, 50, 100 with stationary probes consistently gave a*r ≈ 3.27, where 30 equal-mass background particles each contribute G*m ≈ 0.109; the probes do not source the field, only respond to it.

The simulated universe is two-dimensional with a logarithmic gravitational potential: each background particle sources a scalar field whose Green's function is ln(r), giving a pairwise attractive force that falls off as 1/r along the line connecting the particles. The 30 background particles (all of equal mass, starting at rest) interact with each other and with the 5 massless probes through this 1/r law, while the probes are pure test charges that feel the field but do not source it. A small softening length is included to regularise the integration when particles approach each other closely.

The universe contains 30 mutually-interacting background particles whose positions source a two-dimensional logarithmic gravitational potential V(r) = -G·Σ_j ln|r − r_j|, giving a radial 1/r attractive force F = -G·Σ_j (r − r_j)/|r − r_j|². Far-field tests confirmed the force magnitude scales as 1/R with a total "charge" G·N ≈ 3.2 (so G ≈ 0.1 with unit-mass background). The five probe particles are neutral test masses that feel this field but do not source it; the background particles also feel each other and evolve as a chaotic 30-body system, which dominates long-time prediction error.

The simulated universe contains 30 mutually-attracting background particles plus 5 neutral probes. Each background particle sources a 2D logarithmic gravitational potential, producing an acceleration on every other particle equal to G·m·(r_j − r_i)/|r_j − r_i|² (force falling as 1/r rather than 1/r² as in 3D Newtonian gravity). Probes feel this acceleration from the 30 background sources but do not generate any field of their own; the only fitted parameter is the per-particle coupling G·m ≈ 0.10 (with a tiny softening to keep close approaches numerically stable).

The 2D universe contains 30 background massive sources plus 5 massless probes. All particles (including the background) accelerate under a pairwise central attractive force generated only by the background sources: a_i = G·Σ_j (r_j − r_i) / (|r_j − r_i|^2 + ε²)^(p/2). The exponent p sits near the standard inverse-square form (p≈3 gives 1/r² magnitude) and a softening length ε regularises close encounters. Probes feel the field passively but do not source it, so their trajectories are determined entirely by the (also evolving) background distribution.

The source particle generates a two-dimensional screened (Yukawa) scalar field, namely the Green's function of the 2D modified Helmholtz operator: φ(r) = (p1/(2π)) K₀(r/L), where K₀ is the modified Bessel function of the second kind and L≈2 is the screening length. The probe particle couples to this field as a test mass; its acceleration is a = −(1/p2)∇φ = −(p1/(2π p2 L)) K₁(r/L) r̂. This explains the observed 1/r divergence of the force at small r (since K₁(x)→1/x as x→0) and the exponential fall-off (with a √(1/r) prefactor) at large r, as well as the exact linear-in-p1, inverse-in-p2 scaling.

Particle 1 sources a 2D screened (Yukawa-like) scalar field whose magnitude decays as exp(-r/L)/r with screening length L ≈ e ≈ 2.718. The probe particle 2 experiences a purely radial attractive acceleration with magnitude C·(p1/p2)·exp(-r/L)/r where C ≈ 0.197. The field strength is linear in the source's coupling p1 and inversely linear in the probe's coupling/inertia p2, and the force is independent of the probe's velocity (no drag or magnetic-like terms were observed).

Particle 1 sources a static scalar field obeying the 2D screened Poisson (Helmholtz) equation (∇² − α²)ψ = −p₁ δ²(r), whose Green's function is the modified Bessel function K₀, giving ψ(r) = (p₁/2π) K₀(αr). Particle 2 couples to this field with charge p₁ and inertial response 1/p₂, so its acceleration is the gradient of the field divided by p₂: a = (p₁/p₂)(α/2π) K₁(αr) directed toward the source. The screening length 1/α ≈ 2 produces the characteristic Yukawa-like exponential falloff at large distances combined with a ~1/r divergence at short distances.

The source particle generates a screened 2D scalar field obeying the Klein-Gordon equation (-∇² + m²)φ = p1·δ(r), whose Green's function in 2D is (1/(2π))·K₀(m·r), where K₀ is the modified Bessel function of the second kind. The probe particle is pulled by the gradient of this field, giving a radial attractive force F = -p1·(m/(2π))·K₁(m·r)·r̂. The probe's scalar property p2 acts as inertial mass, so its acceleration is (p1/p2)·(m/(2π))·K₁(m·r) directed toward the source, with screening mass m ≈ 0.5 found from the data.

The probe particle experiences a central attractive force toward the fixed source whose acceleration magnitude follows a 2D Yukawa-like (screened Coulomb) form: a = (p1/p2) · k · exp(−r/L) / r, directed along the line from probe to source. The source "charge" p1 enters linearly while the probe's property p2 acts like an inertial mass (force inversely proportional to p2). The exponential screening with characteristic length L ≈ 2.8 cuts off the long-range interaction, so at small r the law looks like a logarithmic-potential 1/r force, while beyond a few screening lengths the influence vanishes rapidly.

The simulated universe contains 11 equal-mass particles that interact through a pairwise central attraction. Every pair attracts along the line joining them with force magnitude G/d^(3/2) — an inverse-power law softer than Newtonian gravity (exponent 3/2 instead of 2) — with coupling constant G ≈ 1/6. The total acceleration of each particle is the vector sum of these attractions toward all other particles, reproducing both the symmetric radial collapse (acceleration scaling as r^-1.5) and the orbital trajectories of the tangentially-launched ring.

The system is governed by a single central attractive field anchored at particle 0. Every other particle accelerates toward the centre with magnitude G·r^(−1.5), where r is its distance to the central particle and G≈0.84; there are no pairwise interactions among the ring particles, and the centre acts as a fixed source. This produces purely radial collapse when launched at rest and precessing rosette-like orbits when given a tangential velocity, with the symmetric ring configuration preserved throughout.

The system is governed by a static central force field anchored at the spatial origin: every particle feels an attractive acceleration of magnitude G/r^p directed toward the origin, where r is the particle's distance from the origin, G ≈ 0.84, and the exponent p ≈ 1.5. There are no inter-particle (pairwise) forces — each particle moves independently in this fixed potential, which is why the centre particle (starting at the origin) remains at rest and the symmetric ring evolves as a set of independent orbits/infalls in a 1/r^1.5 central field.

The system is governed by a universal pairwise attractive force: every pair of particles pulls toward each other along their connecting line with magnitude F = G/d^1.5, a softer-than-Newtonian inverse-3/2-power gravity-like field. I determined the exponent independently in two ways — the early-time radial acceleration of the ring scaled as r^(-1.5), and full-trajectory fitting converged to α ≈ 1.50 with coupling G ≈ 0.165. The central particle remains at rest only because the symmetric ring configuration produces a balanced net force on it; the law itself is fully pairwise and applies to arbitrary positions.

The eleven particles interact through a universal pairwise attractive central force whose magnitude decays as the inverse three-halves power of the inter-particle distance (F ∝ G·d^(-1.5)), corresponding to a potential U ∝ -d^(-0.5). Each particle's acceleration is the vector sum of contributions from all other particles, directed along the separation and scaled by G/d^2.5. The radial collapse experiments revealed the net inward acceleration of a ring particle scaling cleanly as r^(-1.5), and fitting the full pairwise model to orbital trajectories pinned the exponent to ≈1.50 and the coupling constant to G≈0.166.

The source particle (property p1) creates a static, radially symmetric attractive field acting on the probe (property p2). The field has two additive pieces: a standard inverse-square term (A/r²) and a constant, distance-independent term (B), giving a total radial acceleration of magnitude (p1/p2)(A/r² + B) directed toward the source. The acceleration scaling as p1/p2 indicates p1 plays the role of a source charge/mass while p2 acts as the probe's inertial mass, and the constant B term produces a uniform confining pull that dominates at large separations where the inverse-square term vanishes.

The probe particle (particle 2) is pulled toward a fixed source (particle 1) by a central, always-attractive force. The acceleration magnitude is (p1/p2)·(k/r² + c): it scales linearly with the source property p1, inversely with the probe property p2 (so p2 acts like an inertial mass), and combines a standard inverse-square (Newtonian-like) field with a constant long-range term c that keeps the force finite and nonzero even at very large separations. Measured constants are k ≈ 1.0 and c ≈ 0.1.

The source particle at the origin generates a central attractive field acting on the probe. The probe's acceleration toward the source is (p1/p2)·(A/r² + C): an inverse-square Newtonian-like component plus a constant, range-independent attractive component representing a uniform background field. The coupling scales linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2, so p2 behaves as the probe's inertial mass. The constant term was clearly resolved by its dominance at large r (acceleration plateaus near 0.1·p1/p2 out to r=50), while the 1/r² term dominates at small r.

Particle 2 experiences a central attractive acceleration directed toward the fixed source at the origin. The acceleration magnitude is (p1/p2)·(A/r² + B): a standard Newtonian inverse-square attraction (A≈1) plus a constant, distance-independent attractive term (B≈0.1) that dominates at large separations. The source strength p1 multiplies and the probe property p2 divides the acceleration, so p2 behaves as an inertial mass while p1 acts as a source charge; the motion is purely radial with no tangential or velocity-dependent components.

The source particle creates a central, attractive field on the probe whose acceleration magnitude is (p1/p2)·(k/r² + c), with k≈1.0 and c≈0.1. This is a Newtonian/Coulomb-like inverse-square field superimposed on a constant uniform attractive background that dominates at large separation. The force always points from the probe toward the source, scales linearly with the source's charge p1, inversely with the probe's mass-like property p2, and shows no dependence on the probe's velocity.

The fixed source at the origin generates a central potential field to which the probe particle couples through the product p1*p2. The probe experiences an attractive acceleration of magnitude G*p1*p2/r^2 directed straight toward the source — a classic inverse-square central force (like Newtonian gravity/Coulomb attraction) where both particle "charges" enter linearly. Experiments confirmed the 1/r^2 distance scaling, linear dependence on both p1 and p2, central directionality (verified off-axis and with tangential initial velocity), and a coupling constant G≈1.

Particle 1 sits at the origin and sources a central, attractive inverse-square field. The probe's acceleration points toward the origin with magnitude G·p1·p2/r², linear in both scalar properties (so p2 couples like a charge rather than acting as inertial mass). The coupling is modulated by a velocity factor (1 − v_r/c), where v_r is the outward radial velocity component and c≈100: inward-moving probes feel a stronger pull and outward-moving ones a weaker pull, while purely tangential motion has no effect — a retardation-like correction to a standard Coulomb/Newtonian 1/r² law.

The source particle fixed at the origin produces a static central potential field, and the probe particle experiences a purely radial attractive force directed toward the origin. The force magnitude follows an inverse-square law, F = G·p1·p2/r², where the coupling scales as the product of the two particles' scalar properties and the dimensionless constant G equals exactly 1. There is no velocity-dependent (drag or magnetic) component; all observed velocity changes arise solely from this conservative inverse-square central force.

The source particle generates a static central field, and the probe is accelerated directly toward the source. The acceleration magnitude equals p1*p2/r² (an inverse-square law with the coupling being the product of the two scalar charges, prefactor G≈1), and the acceleration scales linearly with the probe's own charge p2 with no mass division. This produces Newtonian/Keplerian orbital dynamics: bound curved orbits for transverse motion and slingshot escape for near-radial infall, all consistent across separations, charges, and initial velocities tested.

The source particle generates a central potential field to which the probe couples through the product of their scalar properties p1*p2. The probe experiences an attractive acceleration of magnitude G*p1*p2/r^2 directed toward the fixed source, exactly an inverse-square (Newtonian-gravity-like) law with coupling constant G≈1. The force is independent of the probe's velocity, producing bound orbits and hyperbolic scattering trajectories depending on initial conditions.

Each particle's scalar charge sources a static inverse-square (Coulomb-like) field, F/r² with potential q/r. A positive charge produces a field that pushes every other particle away, and a negative charge pulls every other particle in — crucially, the force a particle feels depends only on the SOURCE charges, not on the responding particle's own charge (a neutral particle is pushed just as strongly), and a charge exerts no self-force. The acceleration on any particle is the linear superposition of the inverse-square fields from all other charges, with unit coupling constant (k=1) and no dependence on velocity.

The universe hosts an inverse-square electrostatic field: each particle sources a Coulomb potential proportional to its signed charge, and any pair feels a force F = q_i q_j / r². The twist is that a particle's inertial mass equals its own signed charge, so its acceleration a_i = Σ_j q_j (r_i−r_j)/|r_i−r_j|³ depends only on the other particles' charges, not its own. Consequently like-positive pairs repel, opposite pairs attract, and—because of the negative effective inertia—two negative charges also attract. The coupling constant is k = 1.0.

The universe contains a scalar charge field obeying an inverse-square (Coulomb-like) law, where each particle sources a radial field proportional to its own charge q_j. Unusually, every particle responds to this field as a unit test mass: its acceleration depends only on the source charges and geometry, not on its own charge, giving a_i = Σ_j q_j (r_i − r_j)/|r_i − r_j|³ with coupling constant G ≈ 1. Like-sign charges repel and opposite-sign charges attract along the line joining them, and forces from multiple particles superpose linearly.

This universe contains a scalar charge field obeying an inverse-square (Poisson-like) law: each particle j of signed charge Q_j emits a radial field of magnitude |Q_j|/r² pointing away from it (for Q_j>0) or toward it (for Q_j<0). The defining peculiarity is that a particle's acceleration equals the total source field at its location and does NOT depend on its own charge — neutral and charged particles respond identically, so this behaves like a "charge-sourced gravity" where positive charges repel everything and negative charges attract everything. Fitted constants confirm a prefactor k=1.0 and exponent p=2.0 to within measurement noise.

This universe contains a static inverse-square scalar field sourced by each particle's signed charge. Unlike Coulomb's law, the acceleration a particle experiences depends only on the source charges of the other particles and the inverse-square geometry — not on the responding particle's own charge magnitude or sign (effectively every particle has identical unit inertia). The acceleration is a_i = k·Σ_j q_j (r_i−r_j)/|r_i−r_j|³ with coupling k≈1; positive sources push particles away and negative sources pull them in, producing the characteristic Newtonian-third-law-violating asymmetry observed for opposite-sign pairs.

This is a 2D self-gravitating system governed by a logarithmic potential: every background particle sources an attractive acceleration of magnitude G/r directed along the line toward it (a 1/r force law, the 2D analogue of Newtonian gravity). The five probes are massless test particles that feel the summed field from all background particles but exert no force themselves; far-field measurements pin their coupling at G≈0.556 per source (total field ≈11.1/r for the 20-particle population). The background particles mutually attract under the same 1/r form but with a stronger effective coupling (G_bg≈1.2), so the cluster slowly reconfigures and that evolving field shapes the near-field probe trajectories.

The simulated universe contains a fixed external central potential centred on the domain origin: every particle, background or probe, experiences an attractive acceleration directed toward the centre with magnitude that decreases slowly with radius, well described by a softened power law a = k·r/(r²+eps²)^((q+1)/2) with k≈6, q≈0.5–1, and a softening core eps≈2. The probes are passive test particles that only feel this field (they generate none), so a probe released from rest falls straight through the centre and oscillates back and forth, while one given tangential velocity follows a closed orbit-like path. The background particles obey the identical central law and merely add small perturbations; the radial-infall half-period grows with the launch radius (roughly ∝ r₀^0.8), which is what fixed the force exponent.

The simulated universe contains 20 fixed-population source particles and 5 neutral probes. Each source attracts every other particle (and the probes) with a force directed along the connecting line, but the force magnitude is non-singular at close range and grows weakly with separation — a sub-linear confining interaction (force per source ∝ |d|^p with p≈0.34), intermediate between a constant "string-tension" force and a harmonic spring. The probes feel only the superposition of these forces from the moving background; the resulting radial field rises from the centre, peaks near r≈3, and then falls roughly as 1/r outside the cloud, consistent with a smooth, finite, extended-source potential.

This universe behaves like 2D Newtonian gravity (equivalently, a logarithmic gravitational potential): every fixed background source produces an attractive force on a probe that points toward the source and decays as 1/distance, rather than the 1/distance² of 3D gravity. The neutral probes generate no field themselves and simply accelerate under the vector sum of all background pulls, while the background particles carry large initial velocities and also attract one another, so the field they collectively produce drifts over time. Far outside the cluster the net field reduces cleanly to G·N/r toward the centre, which is how the 1/r form and coupling strength (G ≈ 0.5 per unit source) were identified.

The simulated universe contains 20 background particles plus 5 passive probes. Each particle generates an attractive field whose magnitude falls off as G/r^alpha with alpha≈1, i.e. a two-dimensional Newtonian (logarithmic-potential) gravity in which the force per source scales as the inverse of separation; measured early-time accelerations confirmed a·r ≈ constant. The background bodies mutually gravitate and evolve under this same law, while the probes feel the summed field from all background sources but contribute nothing themselves, so their trajectories are obtained by integrating the net inverse-distance pull from the moving background population.

Particle 2 is attracted radially toward the fixed source (particle 1). The acceleration magnitude is a = K·(p1/p2)·t³/r⁷, i.e. it couples linearly to the source strength p1, inversely to the probe property p2 (which acts like an inertial mass), grows with the cube of elapsed time as if a field amplitude builds up over the experiment, and falls off extremely steeply (inverse seventh power) with separation. Confirmed K≈3.3, with the time exponent and spatial exponent both pinned to exactly 3 and 7 by fitting; experiments with equal p1=p2 ratios produced identical motion regardless of absolute magnitude, verifying the p1/p2 dependence.

Particle 2 experiences a central, attractive force directed toward the fixed source particle 1 at the origin. The acceleration magnitude is proportional to p1/p2 and decays exponentially with separation r as exp(-r/L) with a very short screening length L≈0.06, so the interaction is essentially contact-range — negligible beyond r≈0.6 but extremely strong inside r≈0.3, producing fast in-and-out (chaotic) probe trajectories. Here p1 acts as the source "charge" setting field strength, while p2 behaves as the probe's inertial mass (the force itself is independent of p2, and acceleration scales as 1/p2).

Particle 1 acts as the source of a scalar field that propagates outward at a finite speed c, so the force on the probe at distance r is governed by the retarded time (t − r/c); no influence is felt until the disturbance arrives. The source amplitude grows polynomially in time, producing a central (radial) attraction whose magnitude rises like (t − r/c)^q and falls off as an inverse power of distance 1/r^m. The interaction strength scales as p1/p2 (linear in the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2, i.e. p2 behaves like an inertial mass), and the force is purely central with no velocity-dependent (drag) component.

Particle 1 (fixed at the origin) sources a purely radial, attractive force on the probe whose magnitude is F = K·p1·t³/r⁷, i.e. it grows as the cube of absolute elapsed time and decays as the inverse seventh power of separation. The probe's scalar property p2 behaves as its inertial mass, so the acceleration is F/p2 directed toward the origin. Because of the very steep 1/r⁷ falloff, probes beyond a few units feel essentially nothing and drift ballistically (verified by outward-moving probes keeping constant velocity), whereas nearby probes experience a rapidly intensifying, time-amplified inward pull that drives runaway infall.

Particle 2 feels a purely radial, attractive force directed toward the fixed source particle 1 at the origin. The force is Yukawa-screened in space (decaying as exp(-r) with separation r) and, unusually, grows with absolute time as a power law t^n (n≈4), as if mediated by a field whose coupling ramps up over the experiment. The acceleration scales linearly with the source's scalar property p1 and inversely with the probe's property p2 (consistent with force ∝ p1·p2 and inertial mass ∝ p2), and is independent of the probe's velocity.

The anchor at the origin sources a logarithmic gravitational potential, so every probe feels an inward acceleration of magnitude K/r (with K≈7.96), i.e. acceleration components −K·x/r² and −K·y/r²; this is independent of the probe's mass and velocity. Superimposed on this central field is a spatially uniform constant acceleration g≈0.05 pointing in the +y direction (revealed because the y-acceleration is offset from the pure radial value by a fixed amount everywhere, e.g. stronger on the −y axis and weaker on the +y axis). The 20 ring orbiters are dynamically negligible—measurements just outside the ring still match K/r + g exactly—so the law reduces to a central log-attraction plus a uniform "gravity" field.

The anchor at the origin creates a fixed central field in which each probe experiences an inward radial acceleration of magnitude k/r (with k ≈ 7.7–7.95), corresponding to a two-dimensional logarithmic gravitational potential. This acceleration is completely independent of the probe's own mass and of the ring orbiters — even a mass-100 neighbour two units away produces no measurable force — so the orbiters and probe–probe couplings are negligible. Each probe therefore obeys the simple ODE r'' = -k·r̂/|r|, integrated forward from its initial position and velocity.

The dominant physics is a 2D logarithmic-potential gravity from the central anchor: every probe feels an acceleration of magnitude Ca/r (≈7.93/r) pointed straight at the anchor, i.e. a 1/r force rather than the 1/r² of 3D Newtonian gravity, and this acceleration is completely independent of the probe's own mass. The 20 ring orbiters couple only weakly: a small, short-range (≈g·m/d²), slightly repulsive contribution that scales with orbiter mass and decays with distance, and which barely perturbs the probe trajectories compared with the central field. Probe initial velocities simply set the orbit within this central log potential.

This universe has a fixed central force field centered on the heavy anchor at the origin: every particle experiences a radial acceleration toward the origin of magnitude K/|r| (with K ≈ 8), corresponding to a logarithmic gravitational potential (the 2D "line mass" potential, force ∝ 1/r rather than the 1/r² of Newtonian gravity). The acceleration is completely independent of the particle's own mass and the orbiters do not measurably source forces on the probes — they are themselves test particles, sitting on circular orbits at radius 5 with speed √K = √8 = 2.82, which exactly matches their observed velocities. Probes placed at rest at radius r show inward acceleration with a·r constant at ≈8 across all tested radii (1 to 20), confirming the 1/r force law both inside and outside the orbiter ring.

The simulated universe is governed by a two-dimensional logarithmic gravitational potential sourced by an enormously massive central anchor: every probe experiences a central acceleration of magnitude C/r (C≈8) pointing toward the origin, independent of the probe's own mass — the hallmark of a 1/r force (the gradient of a ln r potential) rather than the usual 1/r² Newtonian law. Superposed on this is a small, spatially uniform background acceleration of about 0.05 directed along +y, which I measured consistently at every radius and direction. The ring orbiters and probe–probe interactions produce no measurable force on the probes, so the dynamics are dominated entirely by the anchor's logarithmic field plus the constant background drift.

The central anchor sources a logarithmic (2D-gravity-like) potential, so each probe feels an attractive acceleration of magnitude C/r (with C≈8) pointing toward the anchor's current position; this was verified by a·r being constant at exactly 8 over radii from 4 to 40. On top of this sits a spatially uniform, distance-independent background field with components (gx≈0, gy≈+0.1) that persists unchanged even at r=40. The probe acceleration is independent of probe mass (force scales with inertial mass), and the ring orbiters exert no measurable force on the probes — their net effect is captured entirely by the anchor term plus the uniform field.

The simulated universe obeys a two-dimensional gravity-like force law in which acceleration falls off as 1/r (a logarithmic potential) rather than the 1/r² of our world. Crucially, the acceleration a test (probe) particle feels is independent of its own mass: every source produces a radial pull of magnitude (coupling)/r toward it. The dominant source is the massive central anchor with a fixed coupling K≈8, giving a=8/r toward the centre; the ring orbiters add a much weaker mass-weighted 1/r contribution (coupling G·m_j per orbiter), which produces the small anisotropic deviations observed near the ring.

The probes obey a 2D logarithmic (1/r) central-force law: each feels an inward acceleration of magnitude A/r toward the origin (A≈8), independent of its own mass — this same field keeps the ring orbiters in their circular orbits at radius 5 (v²/r ≈ A/r). Superimposed is a small, spatially uniform background field of strength g≈0.1 pointing in the +y direction, evidenced by a consistent +y acceleration offset measured at every probe position regardless of location or time. The heavy anchor and the ring orbiters do not produce any additional measurable field beyond this effective central-plus-uniform description.

The central anchor (particle 0) sources a radial field that accelerates every other particle toward it with magnitude K/r (K≈8), independent of the particle's own mass — equivalent to a two-dimensional logarithmic gravitational potential giving rise to constant-circular-speed orbits (the ring orbiters indeed circulate at v²≈K). Inter-particle gravity between probes and orbiters is negligible; only the anchor's 1/r force governs the dynamics. Probes thus move as test particles in a fixed logarithmic central potential.

The probes move in a fixed central force field centred on the massive anchor at the origin. The acceleration is purely radial and inward with magnitude k/r (k ≈ 8), i.e. a 2D logarithmic gravitational potential Φ(r) = k·ln(r). Crucially, this acceleration is completely independent of the probe's inertial mass, and the ring orbiters (and other probes) exert no measurable force — even when a probe sits directly on the ring — so only the anchor sources the field.

The source particle generates a static radial field in 2D whose strength is the sum of a long-range Coulomb-like part (A/r, the natural 1/r field of a point source in two dimensions) and a short-range screened Yukawa part (C·exp(-r/λ)/r). The probe couples attractively to this field with a force proportional to the source charge p1, and its acceleration is divided by its own property p2, which behaves as an inertial mass; there is no velocity dependence or tangential component. Fitting gives A≈0.16, C≈0.9, and a screening length λ≈0.4, so the exponential term boosts the attraction strongly only within roughly half a unit of the source.

The source particle (held at the origin) sets up a static two-dimensional field whose potential increases logarithmically with distance, so the resulting force on the probe is purely central and attractive, falling off as 1/r (force ∝ r̂/r). The probe's acceleration scales linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2, with a coupling constant C ≈ 0.159 ≈ 1/(2π). No velocity-dependent terms (drag or magnetic-type coupling) were observed; the dramatic speed changes seen for trajectories launched from the origin were simply artifacts of the 1/r singularity at r = 0.

The source particle generates a static radial field whose acceleration on the probe points toward the origin with magnitude k·(p1/p2)/r — an inverse-first-power force corresponding to a two-dimensional logarithmic potential. The probe couples to this field in proportion to the source strength p1 and inversely to its own property p2, with no dependence on velocity (no magnetic-like or drag terms). The fitted coefficient is k≈0.15 and the radial exponent is essentially 1, producing nearly circular precessing orbits characteristic of a 2D gravitational/Coulomb-like log field.

Particle 1 sources a static 2D field with a logarithmic potential, so the force on the probe is a central attraction whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (the gradient of ln r). The probe's acceleration is proportional to the source strength p1 and inversely proportional to its own property p2 (which acts like an inertial/coupling mass), giving a = C·(p1/p2)/r directed toward particle 1, with the dimensionless coupling C ≈ 0.16. The dynamics are purely this conservative central force; there is no dependence on velocity, producing precessing orbits around the source.

Particle 1 acts as a fixed point source of a 2D field, and particle 2 (the probe) feels a purely radial, attractive acceleration directed toward the origin. The acceleration magnitude equals (p1/p2)·[A/r + B·exp(-r/λ)/r²]: the A/r piece is the long-range 2D log-potential (Gauss-law) force with A≈1/(2π)≈0.159, while the B·exp(-r/λ)/r² piece is a short-range, exponentially screened (Yukawa-like, λ≈1) massive-field contribution that dominates near the source and produces the observed crossover from a 1/r² close-range force to a 1/r far-range force. The coupling is linear in the source strength p1 and inversely proportional to the probe property p2 (its inertial mass), with no dependence on absolute time.

Particle 2 experiences a central attractive force toward the fixed source at the origin. The force magnitude is [A + B·cos(kx)·cos(ky)]/r with A≈0.16, B≈0.08 and wavenumber k=2π/5, i.e. an inverse-distance central field whose amplitude is modulated by a static 2D cosine standing-wave background (interference pattern aligned with the x and y axes). The coupling scales as p1/p2: the force grows linearly with the source's scalar property p1 and is inversely proportional to the probe's property p2 (acting as an inertial mass), and there is no dependence on the probe's velocity.

Particle 2 feels a central, purely attractive force pointing toward the fixed source particle 1. The force magnitude scales linearly with the source's scalar property p1 and is completely independent of the probe's property p2 and its velocity. The radial profile is a 1/r (Coulomb-like) envelope modulated by a spatial standing wave: a(r) = p1·(C + D·cos(k·r))/r with C≈1/(2π)≈0.159, D≈1/(4π)≈0.0795, and wavelength exactly 5 (k=2π/5≈1.257), as confirmed by direct acceleration measurements showing a·r oscillating with maxima at r=5,10,15 and minima at r=2.5,7.5,12.5. This is consistent with a standing-wave or screened oscillatory field sourced by particle 1.

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed source by a central force whose magnitude decays as 1/r but is modulated by a spatial cosine of wavelength about 5, so the acceleration is (p1/p2)·(A + B·cos(k·r))/r pointing radially inward. The source strength enters linearly through p1 while the probe's inertial response goes as 1/p2 (p2 acts like an inertial mass), and the cosine term suggests the underlying potential field oscillates spatially (a screened/wave-like Coulomb field) producing the non-monotonic radial force. Fitted constants are A≈0.176, B≈0.089 (B/A≈0.5) and k≈2π/5≈1.257.

Particle 2 moves under a central, attractive force from the fixed source. The radial acceleration follows a = (p1/p2)·(A + B·cos(k·r))/r: a 1/r envelope (consistent with a 2D logarithmic-style potential) modulated by an undamped standing-wave term of fixed wavelength (period ≈5, k≈2π/5). The probe couples linearly to the source strength p1 and inversely to its own property p2, with the oscillatory term producing alternating rings of stronger and weaker pull at radii spaced by ~2.5.

Particle 2 feels a central, always-attractive acceleration directed toward the fixed source at the origin. The magnitude follows |a| = (p1/p2)·(A + B·cos(k r))/r: a 1/r radial envelope multiplied by a spatially periodic standing-wave factor with wavenumber k = 2π/5 ≈ 1.257 (period 5 in distance). This is the signature of a 2D oscillating monopole / Helmholtz-like field Green's function; the probe couples to the source strength p1 and is divided by its own inertia-like property p2. Measured constants: A ≈ 0.16, B ≈ 0.08 (so the field's radial acceleration·r oscillates between ~0.08 at minima and ~0.24 at maxima).

The source particle at the origin generates a static central field; the probe particle 2 is attracted along the line connecting them with an acceleration of magnitude G·p1/(p2·r²), an inverse-square law. The source property p1 acts as a "charge/mass" strengthening the field, while the probe's own property p2 behaves inertially in the denominator. The force is purely radial and independent of the probe's velocity, with the coupling constant G ≈ 0.16.

Particle 2 moves in a static central potential generated by the fixed source at the origin. It experiences an attractive inverse-square acceleration directed toward the source, a = -G·(p1/p2)·r̂/r², where the source's scalar property p1 sets the field strength and the probe's property p2 acts like an inertial mass (the force is proportional to p1·p2 while the acceleration carries an extra 1/p2). The fitted coupling constant is G ≈ 0.155, and the motion is otherwise unaffected by absolute time, producing bound orbits or hyperbolic flybys depending on initial velocity.

Particle 2 moves in a static central force field produced by the fixed source particle 1 at the origin. The force is purely attractive and radial, with magnitude k·p1/(p2·r²): it scales linearly with the source's property p1, inversely with the probe's own mass-like property p2, and falls off as the inverse square of separation. There is no velocity dependence or tangential component, so the motion is exactly Keplerian-like, with the prefactor k ≈ 0.16 confirmed directly from clean acceleration measurements (e.g. a = 0.1 m/s² at r=4, p1=10, p2=1).

Particle 2 moves under a central inverse-square attractive force toward the fixed source at the origin. The acceleration magnitude is G·(p1/p2)/r², directed along the line connecting the two particles; the source property p1 acts as a gravitational/charge strength while the probe property p2 acts inversely (like an inertial or coupling factor). Clean velocity measurements show the force scales linearly with p1, inversely with p2, and as 1/r² in distance, with G ≈ 0.16; no velocity-dependent (magnetic-type) terms were detected.

The source particle creates a static central field that attracts the probe with an acceleration proportional to p1 (the source's gravitational charge) and inversely proportional to p2 (the probe's inertial mass) and to the square of the separation distance. This is Newtonian inverse-square gravity with a modified coupling constant G≈0.18; the force is always directed straight toward the fixed source, producing the curved, conic-section-like trajectories observed when the probe has transverse velocity.

The source particle (particle 1, fixed at the origin) generates a static radial field that pulls the probe particle 2 directly toward it. The acceleration is purely central with magnitude G·p1/(p2·r): it is proportional to the source property p1, inversely proportional to the probe property p2, and falls off as 1/r — the signature of a 2D logarithmic (Coulomb-like) potential. No velocity-dependent (magnetic-type) coupling was detected; the dynamics are entirely governed by this attractive inverse-distance central force with G ≈ 0.16.

The source particle generates a 2D logarithmic potential, giving the probe a purely radial, attractive acceleration of magnitude k·p1/(p2·r) (force falls off as 1/r, the natural Gauss-law behavior in two spatial dimensions). The coupling is proportional to the source property p1 and inversely proportional to the probe property p2 (its inertial/charge factor), with fitted constant k ≈ 0.16. There is no velocity-dependent or transverse (magnetic) component — trajectories with cross-velocity confirmed the force stays strictly along the line to the source.

The source particle sources a 2D logarithmic potential, so the probe experiences a central, attractive acceleration directed toward the source with magnitude k·(p1/p2)/r — an inverse-distance (1/r) force, the natural Coulomb/gravity analog in two dimensions. The coupling grows linearly with the source's scalar property p1 and falls inversely with the probe's property p2 (which acts like an inertial mass), with k ≈ 0.157. No velocity-dependent or angular terms were detected; trajectories are pure central-force orbits.

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed source (particle 1 at the origin) by a central force whose magnitude is G·(p1/p2)/r — a 1/r force law, which is the 2D analog of a logarithmic potential (the field of a point source in two dimensions). The acceleration points radially inward, scales linearly with the source property p1, and inversely with the probe property p2; the coupling constant G ≈ 0.16. This was confirmed by measuring clean velocity-derived accelerations across distances (r = 2 to 10) and property values, all matching a = 0.16·p1/(p2·r).

The source particle (particle 1, fixed at the origin) generates a 2D logarithmic potential, producing an attractive central force on the probe whose magnitude scales as 1/r (a · r is constant along the radial direction). The acceleration is directed toward the source and scales linearly with the source strength p1 and inversely with the probe property p2, with overall coupling constant k ≈ 0.15. This is the analogue of gravity/electrostatics in a 2D universe where field flux conservation gives a 1/r rather than 1/r² law.

The particles obey a two-dimensional gravity law: they interact pairwise through an attractive force whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (a logarithmic potential, the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian), directed along each pair's separation vector. Dynamics are second order (acceleration equals the net force, unit masses) and forces superpose vectorially; I confirmed a×r is constant across separations from 1 to 16 and verified vector superposition with multi-body triangle tests. The coupling constant G is near 0.16 from direct kinematic measurements (a global trajectory fit favored a somewhat lower value, so G is left as a fitted parameter).

This universe obeys a two-dimensional gravity-like law: every pair of equal-mass particles attracts along the line connecting them with an acceleration magnitude G/r (force decaying as 1/r), which is exactly the gradient of a logarithmic potential — the proper 2D Green's function for gravity. The interaction superposes linearly across all particles and is completely independent of velocity, with the coupling constant G ≈ 0.16 confirmed by both direct slope measurements (a·r ≈ 0.16 across separations 2–50) and an MSE fit (G ≈ 0.164).

This universe obeys 2D Newtonian gravity: every particle attracts every other with an acceleration of magnitude G/r directed along the line connecting them, where r is the separation and G ≈ 1/(2π) ≈ 0.159 — exactly the coupling of the Green's function for the 2D Poisson (logarithmic potential) equation. Direct acceleration measurements showed a·r is constant (≈0.16) across separations from r=1 to r=20, confirming the 1/r force law with no short-range softening, and three-body tests confirmed that the pairwise accelerations add linearly via vector superposition.

The universe obeys a two-dimensional gravitational law: each pair of particles attracts along the line joining them with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to their separation (F = G·m_i·m_j/r, i.e. a logarithmic potential, the natural 2D analogue of Newtonian gravity). The acceleration of any particle depends only on the masses and positions of the others (mass cancels for the test body), and measured pair accelerations confirmed a·r ≈ constant across two decades of separation. The ten particles carry distinct masses [1,1,1,1,0.5,0.5,0.25,0.5,0.25,0.25] with coupling constant G ≈ 0.16.

This is a 2D gravitational universe where every particle attracts every other with an acceleration of magnitude k/r (k ≈ 0.15) directed along the line joining them — a force that falls off as the inverse of distance, corresponding to a logarithmic gravitational potential characteristic of 2D gravity. The forces from all particles superpose linearly, all particles have equal mass, and there is no velocity dependence; the dynamics are simply Newtonian integration of these pairwise accelerations.

The probes move in a static, central radial field anchored at the origin (the massive anchor particle). The radial acceleration has the form a(r) = -A/r + B·r: an attractive inverse-distance term characteristic of 2D Newtonian gravity from the central anchor, plus a linearly increasing repulsive term equivalent to a uniform repulsive background (anti-restoring harmonic). The acceleration is independent of the probe's own inertial mass, and the surrounding ring orbiters are light enough that their contributions cancel/negligibly perturb the field, so the force on every probe points exactly toward or away from the origin with magnitude |−A/r + B·r|.

The probes move in a static central field centred on the origin (the massive anchor surrounded by its orbiter ring). The radial acceleration is a(r) = C·r − A/r (outward positive): an inward 1/r attraction characteristic of a two-dimensional logarithmic gravitational potential dominates at small radius, while an outward linear "anti-restoring" term dominates beyond r≈12.6, where the net force reverses sign. The acceleration is independent of both the probe's mass and its velocity, with fitted constants A≈7.97 and C≈0.050; the giant anchor and the orbiter ring together reduce to this single mass-independent central law for predicting probe trajectories.

The probes move in a fixed, mass-independent radial central field centered on the anchor at the origin, with radial acceleration a_r = -A/r + B·r (A ≈ 7.96, B ≈ 0.05). This derives from the potential Φ(r) = A·ln(r) + ½B·r², combining a 2D logarithmic (Newtonian-in-2D) attractive well that dominates near the center with an inverted-harmonic repulsive term that grows linearly and causes distant probes to escape (the zero-force radius is near r ≈ √(A/B) ≈ 12.6). The acceleration is independent of probe inertial mass and of the enormous anchor mass, indicating the bodies merely establish this emergent fixed central field rather than acting through ordinary mass-scaled N-body gravity.

The probes move in a static central field centered on the massive anchor at the origin. The radial acceleration is a_r = -A/r + B·r: an attractive component that falls off as 1/r (corresponding to a logarithmic, 2D-gravity-like potential) plus a linearly growing outward term (an anti-harmonic / negative-background repulsion). The force is strictly central, independent of a probe's own mass and of its velocity, producing inward attraction for small radii, an equilibrium ring at r₀ = √(A/B) ≈ 13, and net outward push beyond it.

The simulated universe contains a central anchor of enormous mass at the origin that sets up a static, isotropic field whose radial acceleration on any test particle is a_r = -C/r + D·r. The -C/r term is a two-dimensional (logarithmic-potential) gravitational attraction dominated entirely by the anchor (C ≈ 7.96), while +D·r is a linear anti-confining repulsion (D ≈ 0.05) that overwhelms attraction beyond a turnover radius √(C/D) ≈ 12.6, causing distant particles to fly outward. Acceleration is independent of the probe's own mass, the ring orbiters merely orbit in this same field (their orbital speed 2.59 at r=5 matches √(C/r − D·r)·√r), and probes exert no measurable force on each other.

The probes obey a single isotropic central force directed along the line to the anchor at the origin, with radial acceleration a(r) = -A/r + B·r. The first term is a 2D logarithmic-potential (Newtonian-in-2D) attraction that dominates near the centre, and the second is a linear repulsion that grows without bound and produces exponential runaway at large radius; the crossover (zero force) sits near r ≈ √(A/B) ≈ 12–13. The acceleration is completely independent of the probe's own mass (force ∝ probe mass), and the 20 ring orbiters at radius 5 are in stable circular orbits of precisely this field (their tangential speed of ~2.59 gives centripetal acceleration matching -A/5 + B·5), confirming the net potential acts as an approximately static central well centred on the heavy anchor.

The probes experience a static, isotropic central acceleration field about the anchor whose inward radial component is a(r) = A/r − C·r. The A/r term is a two-dimensional Newtonian/logarithmic gravity (force ∝ 1/r) sourced by the central anchor, while the −C·r term is a harmonic anti-confining background that grows with radius and turns the net field outward beyond a crossover near r ≈ √(A/C) ≈ 12.6. The acceleration is independent of probe inertial mass (it is a pure acceleration field), and the symmetric ring of orbiters keeps the field centred on the origin throughout the motion.

The probes move in a central field anchored at the massive central particle (origin). The radial acceleration is a(r) = -K/r + C·r: the -K/r term is a two-dimensional logarithmic-potential attraction (the field of a point source falls as 1/r by Gauss's law in 2D, dominated by the huge-inertia anchor), while the +C·r term is a linearly growing outward repulsion acting like a uniform confining/anti-gravitating background. Acceleration is independent of the probe's own mass (an equivalence-principle/gravity-like coupling), and the field crosses zero near r≈12.6, being attractive inside and expelling outside; the small orbiter masses produce only negligible perturbations on top of this smooth central field.

The probes move in a static, central (radially symmetric) field centered on the massive anchor at the origin. The radial acceleration is a_r = −K/r + c·r: a 2D-gravity-like inverse-distance attraction toward the center (K≈8.0) combined with a linear repulsive term that grows with distance (c≈0.058), which together create a stable equilibrium "ring" at r = √(K/c) ≈ 11.7 where the force vanishes. The acceleration is independent of probe mass (it is a field acceleration), and the discrete ring orbiters' individual gravitational contributions are negligible compared to this dominant central field.

The universe is governed by a single static, central field anchored at the origin. Any particle experiences a radial acceleration a(r) = -A/r + k·r, where the -A/r term is a two-dimensional logarithmic-potential attraction (force falling as 1/r) and the +k·r term is a linear anti-confining repulsion that dominates at large radii and flings distant probes outward. The acceleration depends only on position (not on the particle's mass, the anchor, or the individual orbiters), so the 20 ring orbiters simply trace stable circular orbits (their v²/r ≈ A/r − k·r balances exactly at r=5), and the probes follow the same field with A≈8 and k≈0.05.

The particles interact through a universal pairwise central attraction whose force magnitude between two bodies separated by distance d is F(d) = alpha/d + beta/d². This is a superposition of a 1/d term (the field of a 2D logarithmic potential) and a 1/d² Newtonian-like term, both purely attractive. All eleven particles—centre and ring alike—couple identically, so a symmetric ring with no tangential velocity collapses radially toward the centre while keeping its angular spacing, and adding tangential velocity makes the ring rotate rigidly as it contracts. Fitting initial accelerations across ring radii 2–15 gave alpha ≈ 1.25 and beta ≈ 8.4, and the analytic geometric sum (5.5·alpha/r + 4.862·beta/r²) reproduced the measured inward accelerations to three significant figures.

The eleven particles interact through a static, conservative, central pairwise attraction whose force magnitude between any two particles separated by distance d is F(d) = alpha/d² + beta/d — a Newtonian inverse-square term plus an additional, longer-range 1/d term. Each particle obeys Newton's second law (unit mass) summed over all partners. I deduced this by measuring the radial acceleration a(r) of the symmetric ring: using the exact geometric sums over the centre plus the nine other ring particles, a·r² is linear in r (a·r² ≈ 4.863·alpha + 5.5·beta·r), which uniquely identifies the two-term force; the symmetric ring consequently collapses radially to the centre and re-expands, with the inverse-square term dominating at close range during collapse.

The system is a set of 11 equal particles immersed in a single universal attractive field: every pair attracts along the line connecting them with a central force whose magnitude follows a power law F = G/r^n in their separation r. The fitted exponent is sub-inverse-square, near n ≈ 1.35, with coupling G ≈ 5; early-time radial accelerations of the symmetric ring scale as ~27/R^1.4 once the geometric sum over the nine neighbours is included. The central particle stays at rest because the symmetric ring exerts forces that cancel on it, while ring particles collapse inward (or orbit/precess when given tangential speed), the collapse diverging strongly as r → 0.

The eleven particles interact through a central, conservative pairwise attractive field whose force magnitude decays as 1/d^1.5 with separation d (acceleration vector ∝ (r_j − r_i)/|r_j − r_i|^2.5). For the symmetric ring this produces a net inward acceleration that scales as r^(−1.5) (verified by a·r^1.5 ≈ const ≈ 32.5 across radii 2–15, by energy conservation ½v² − k/√r during collapse, and by matching the centripetal acceleration of orbiting cases). Released from rest the ring collapses inward, and because the potential ∝ −1/√r is singular at the origin the simultaneous convergence of all particles scatters them violently and they escape at nearly constant radial velocity, while sufficient tangential velocity instead yields smooth precessing orbits.

The particles live in a 2D universe and couple to a scalar field that is the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian — a logarithmic potential. Consequently every pair attracts along their line of separation with a force whose magnitude scales as G/d (inverse first power of distance, ≈0.16/d), the hallmark of 2D "gravity." The interactions superpose linearly with no velocity dependence; apparent cluster expansion in dense configurations arises from chaotic close-encounter slingshots, not from any repulsive term.

Each of the 30 particles moves independently under an external, position-dependent force field (there is no detectable pairwise interaction). The acceleration is anisotropic: the x-acceleration is -C·x·cos(k·y) and the y-acceleration is -C·y·cos(k·x), i.e. a linear restoring/anti-restoring force along each axis whose sign and strength are spatially modulated by a cosine of the perpendicular coordinate. Fitting pins the coupling C≈0.13 and the spatial wavenumber k≈π (period 2), producing smooth inward pull on the axes and sign-alternating radial forcing along the diagonals; a steep near-axis fine structure observed at non-integer coordinates suggests the true potential also contains a sharper singular modulation that this dominant cosine form approximates.

The 30 particles interact through an unknown attractive field that turns out to be purely pairwise and central: every particle is pulled toward every other particle with an acceleration of magnitude k/r along the line joining them, i.e. force a = k·Σⱼ (rⱼ−rᵢ)/|rⱼ−rᵢ|². This 1/r dependence is the gradient of a two-dimensional logarithmic (Newtonian-in-2D) potential, which I confirmed with clean two-body experiments showing a·r ≈ 0.155 across separations from 2 up to several hundred, and with a collective experiment in which the whole cloud contracts toward its centroid. A short-range softening length eps regularises the singularity at zero separation (identical positions produced numerical errors), and both k and eps are left as fitted constants because they are partly degenerate at the close separations relevant to scoring.

This universe obeys a two-dimensional ("logarithmic") gravity: every particle attracts every other particle with a force whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (rather than 1/r² as in 3D), so each particle's acceleration is the vector sum of G·(r_j−r_i)/|r_j−r_i|² over all other particles. I verified the inverse-distance law by isolating two-body pairs and finding that force×separation was constant (≈0.048 in velocity units at t=0.3) across separations from r=1 to r=100, and that the interaction is always attractive, directed along the line joining each pair. The single fitted constant G≈0.16 sets the overall coupling strength; a tiny softening length eps merely regularises the singularity at zero separation.

The 30 particles interact through a 2D gravitational field: each particle sources a potential proportional to ln(r), the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian, so the resulting attractive force between any pair falls off as 1/separation rather than the 3D 1/r². The acceleration on particle i is the superposed sum over all other particles j of (1/(2π))·(r_j−r_i)/|r_j−r_i|², with all particles carrying equal unit mass; I measured the coupling constant directly from short-time accelerations as a·r ≈ 0.158 ≈ 1/(2π) across separations from 2 to 80, confirming the inverse-separation law and linear superposition. A small softening length is included only to regularize close encounters.

Particle 2 experiences a central force pointing toward the fixed source particle whose magnitude is proportional to (p1/p2)·(1/r) and which pulsates in time as cos(ωt), with ω ≈ π/2 (period ≈ 4) and coupling constant C ≈ 0.75. Because the force oscillates symmetrically between attraction and repulsion, a particle initially at rest simply oscillates about its starting position (velocity amplitude ≈ (C/ω)(p1/p2)/r) with no net drift, while any initial velocity is preserved as a steady drift superimposed on the radial pulsation. The field is thus a time-pulsating radial coupling (inverse-distance force law in 2D) rather than a static conservative potential.

The source particle generates a radial field whose amplitude pulsates sinusoidally in time, F ∝ cos(ωt) with ω = π/2 (period 4). The probe particle feels a radial acceleration directed along the source–probe line equal to a = −(C·p1/(p2·r))·cos(ωt)·r̂, i.e. it scales linearly with the source coupling p1, inversely with the probe's inertial mass p2, and falls off as 1/r. At t = 0 the force is attractive (toward the source), and because the time-average of cos(ωt) vanishes a stationary probe simply oscillates radially with amplitude ∝ 1/r while a moving probe drifts along its initial velocity superposed with this oscillation.

Particle 1 (fixed at the origin) sources a radial field whose strength oscillates sinusoidally in time. Particle 2 experiences an acceleration directed along the line to the origin with magnitude (K·p1)/(p2·r)·cos(ωt): the coupling falls off as 1/distance, grows linearly with the source property p1, and is inversely proportional to the probe's property p2 (like an inertial mass). The time modulation has a fixed angular frequency ω ≈ π/2 (period 4) that is independent of separation and particle properties, so a particle released from rest merely jiggles about its starting point with velocity amplitude (K·p1)/(p2·r·ω) rather than falling inward.

Particle 2 experiences a central, radially-oriented force from the fixed source (particle 1) whose magnitude is proportional to p1, inversely proportional to the probe property p2, and inversely proportional to the instantaneous separation r. Crucially the field is oscillatory in time: it is modulated by cos(ωt) with a universal angular frequency ω = π/2 (period 4) that is independent of separation, p1, and p2. As a result a probe released from rest performs small sinusoidal radial oscillations (velocity ∝ sin(ωt)) about its starting point, while probes given large inward velocities sweep close to the source where the 1/r field strengthens.

The source particle (held fixed at the origin) generates a radial field that the probe couples to, producing an acceleration directed along the line connecting the two particles. The acceleration magnitude is K·p1/(p2·r)·cos(ωt): it grows linearly with the source property p1, is inversely proportional to the probe property p2 (which acts like an inertial/screening mass), and falls off as 1/r with separation. The defining feature is the temporal modulation cos(ωt) with ω≈π/2 (period 4), which makes the probe oscillate back and forth about its initial position instead of being steadily attracted, as if the source were an oscillating (breathing) monopole.

A stationary source particle at the origin generates a central, purely attractive field that pulls the probe straight toward it. The probe's acceleration scales linearly with the source strength p1 and inversely with the probe's own scalar property p2 (which acts as inertial mass), and as a function of separation r it is the sum of a long-range 1/r component (a logarithmic-potential force that makes a·r² grow linearly with r at large distances, confirmed out to r=100), a standard Coulomb-like 1/r² component, and a weaker short-range screened term C·exp(-r/λ)/r² that enhances the pull within about one length-scale of the source. Velocity measurements (noise-free) confirmed the central direction and the exact p1 and 1/p2 dependences.

Particle 2 moves in a static central field generated by the fixed source particle 1 at the origin. The acceleration points toward the source with magnitude proportional to (p1/p2) and falling off as r^(-3/2) — a power law steeper than 1/r but gentler than standard inverse-square gravity. The source property p1 acts as a charge/mass enhancing the attraction while the probe property p2 behaves like an inertial resistance, dividing the response.

The source particle creates a central attractive field. The probe particle 2 accelerates toward the source with a radial acceleration equal to (p1/p2)·(A/r² + B/r), i.e. a superposition of an inverse-square (Newtonian/Coulomb-like) term and a 1/r term characteristic of a 2D field with a logarithmic potential. The force is linear in the source strength p1 and the acceleration is inversely proportional to the probe's mass-like property p2; measured coefficients are A≈0.82 and B≈0.13.

Particle 2 feels a purely radial (central) attraction toward the fixed source whose magnitude scales linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2. The long-range field is a superposition of a Coulomb-like 1/r^2 term and a longer-range 1/r term, so the force decays more slowly than ordinary gravity. Superimposed on this is a localized attractive shell: an extra force that switches on inside a radius of about 0.8 (a sharp Fermi-type outer cliff) and is suppressed again very close to the origin (a 1/r Gaussian inner cutoff), producing a flat-topped resonant enhancement of the inward pull for probes that approach within ~0.3–0.8 units of the source.

Particle 2 moves under a purely radial, attractive central force from the fixed source at the origin. The force strength is proportional to the source property p1 and inversely proportional to the probe property p2, with a long-range fall-off of approximately r^-1.5 (gentler than ordinary gravity) plus a strong short-range exponential enhancement of characteristic length ~0.45, giving the field a Yukawa-like attractive core. There is no detectable velocity dependence; the dynamics are governed entirely by this position-dependent radial acceleration a = (p1/p2)·C·(1 + A·exp(-r/λ))/r^1.5 directed toward the source.

All six particles attract one another through a field whose pairwise force magnitude scales as k/r (an inverse-first-power law, i.e. the 2D analog of gravity sourced by a logarithmic potential), directed along the line connecting each pair. The force is purely positional with no dependence on velocity, and accelerations superpose linearly across all partner particles. Experimentally the product (force × separation) stayed constant at ≈0.32 for the relative acceleration of a pair across separations from 1 to 15 in both axes, giving a per-particle coupling k ≈ 0.16.

The six particles attract one another through a two-dimensional Newtonian-style gravity: each pair feels a force of magnitude C/r directed along their separation, producing acceleration C·(r_j−r_i)/|r_j−r_i|² (a logarithmic potential, confirmed by the pair force×distance product being constant ≈0.16). Superimposed on this is a uniform background acceleration g≈(−0.02,−0.02) that acts identically on every particle, which I isolated by placing a test particle at the origin surrounded by symmetric spectators and decomposing the residual non-central, distance-independent drift.

The six particles inhabit a 2D world and mutually attract through a field with a logarithmic potential, so each pair exerts an attractive force of magnitude G/r along their separation vector (the 2D analogue of gravity, where Poisson's equation yields a ln r potential and a 1/r field). All particles have equal unit mass, the forces from different partners add linearly (superposition confirmed), and the interaction has no dependence on velocity. The measured coupling constant G ≈ 0.159 is essentially 1/(2π), consistent with the 2D Green's-function normalisation; close encounters cause particles to pass through one another and oscillate, requiring a small softening length to regularise the singularity.

The six particles couple to a shared scalar field that produces a mutual attraction whose force magnitude scales as G/r (inverse of separation, the gradient of a 2D logarithmic potential), directed along the line joining each pair. Forces add pairwise, and although large-separation experiments hint at an extremely long-range screening length (~hundreds of units), it is negligible across the experimental domain, so the effective law is simply F = G/r toward each neighbour. A small residual super-linear attraction in dense symmetric clusters suggests a weak nonlinear self-field term, but the dominant, robustly measured behaviour is the pairwise 1/r attraction with coupling constant G ≈ 0.16–0.27.

The six particles all couple to a common scalar field that obeys the 2D Poisson equation, whose Green's function is the logarithmic potential phi = k·ln(r). Each pair therefore attracts with a force of magnitude k/r (a 1/r force, the 2D analogue of gravity) directed along the line joining them; forces add linearly, masses are unity, and the dynamics show no dependence on velocity. Clean isolated-pair measurements across separations from 0.5 to 100 give a constant coupling k ≈ 0.16, consistent with the natural 2D value k = 1/(2π).

The simulated universe is a 2D self-gravitating system in which each of the 30 fixed-population background sources generates a logarithmic gravitational potential, so the force between any pair of particles is attractive and decays as 1/distance (the displacement vector divided by the squared separation), rather than the inverse-square law of our universe. The five highest-index particles are massless probes: they accelerate under the summed field of all background sources but contribute no field of their own, and the force is independent of velocity (no magnetic-type coupling). Far-field measurements confirmed that distance times acceleration is constant (≈3.14 = 30·G), pinning the per-source coupling G near 0.105 per unit mass.

The source particle generates a screened (massive) scalar field in 2D whose static potential is the modified Bessel function K0(k·r), the natural Green's function of the 2D screened-Poisson (Helmholtz) equation. The probe particle is attracted toward the source with an acceleration equal to the negative gradient of this potential, proportional to k·K1(k·r) and scaling linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2. The force is central (directed along the line to the source), behaving like 1/r at short range and decaying exponentially (∝ exp(-k·r)/√r) at long range, with screening length 1/k ≈ 1.8.

Particle 1 sits at the origin and sources a screened (Yukawa-type) scalar field; the probe particle 2 experiences an attractive central acceleration of magnitude A·(p1/p2)·exp(-r/λ)/r pointing toward the origin. The force grows linearly with the source's property p1 and inversely with the probe's property p2 (an inertial-like factor), and the exponential factor with screening length λ≈2.38 cuts off the interaction at large separations. The radial fall-off goes as 1/r (rather than 1/r²), and the dynamics are purely position-dependent with no velocity-dependent (drag or magnetic) terms.

The fixed source particle sources a screened scalar field governed by a 2D Helmholtz (screened-Poisson) equation, whose radial Green's function is the modified Bessel function K0(k·r). The probe particle couples to this field with a purely central, attractive force of magnitude C·(p1/p2)·K1(k·r) — i.e. the negative gradient of the screened potential — which diverges like 1/r at short range and decays as exp(-k·r)/√r at long range. The force scales linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2, and is entirely independent of the probe's velocity.

Particle 1 at the origin sources a static, radially symmetric scalar field that attracts the probe particle 2 toward it. The acceleration on the probe is central (always pointing back to the source) and velocity-independent, with magnitude G·(p1/p2)·exp(−r/λ)/r^q: it scales linearly with the source property p1, inversely with the probe property p2, has a soft power-law core (exponent q≈0.77, gentler than Newtonian 1/r²), and is exponentially screened beyond a range λ≈2.1 (Yukawa-like). Fitted constants are G≈0.21, λ≈2.12, q≈0.77.

Particle 2 is attracted to the fixed source (particle 1) by a screened Yukawa field: the acceleration points radially toward the source with magnitude (p1/p2)·C·exp(−r/L)/r, where r is the separation, C≈0.225, and L≈2.5 is the screening length. The source strength scales linearly with p1 while the probe's response scales inversely with p2, indicating p1 acts as a field charge and p2 as an inertial/coupling factor; the exponential cutoff reflects a massive mediating field with finite range.

In this 2D universe, all 11 particles interact through a pairwise attractive force with magnitude F = G / r^alpha, where r is the separation distance between any two particles. The force is directed along the line connecting each pair (attractive), analogous to gravity but with a different distance exponent. From early-time acceleration measurements across ring radii from 2 to 10, the net radial force on each ring particle scales as approximately 0.840/R^1.5, and geometric analysis of the 10-particle ring configuration yields a pairwise coupling constant G ≈ 0.1661 with exponent alpha ≈ 1.5. The system exhibits a characteristic "breathing" mode where the ring collapses, passes through a minimum radius, and re-expands while maintaining perfect N-fold rotational symmetry, consistent with a purely radial pairwise attractive force law.

In this simulated universe, all 11 particles interact through a pairwise attractive force with an unusual distance dependence: the force magnitude scales as r^1.5 (i.e., proportional to the inter-particle separation raised to the 3/2 power), directed along the line connecting each pair. This is stronger than a linear (Hooke's law) spring force but weaker than a cubic force, causing rings of particles with no tangential velocity to collapse rapidly toward their mutual center of mass. The coupling constant G ≈ 0.030 sets the overall strength of interaction, and the force law F_ij = G · r_ij^1.5 · r̂_ij applies symmetrically between every pair of particles including the central particle and all ring particles.

All 11 particles (one central and 10 on a ring) interact through pairwise attractive inverse-square force laws analogous to 2D gravity: the force on particle i from particle j has magnitude G/d² and points from i toward j, where d is the separation distance. With coupling constant G ≈ 1.0, this fully determines the dynamics: for zero tangential velocity the ring collapses toward the center, while for sufficiently large tangential velocity the ring orbits at roughly constant radius. The system is an N-body gravitational simulation where all 11 particles mutually attract via F = G/r², confirmed by initial-acceleration scaling (a ∝ r⁻²) and MSE fitting over ~1000 trajectory measurements giving G ≈ 1.004.

The simulated universe contains 11 particles interacting through pairwise attractive forces with a 1/r² magnitude law (force vector F_ij = G * r̂_ij / |r_ij|^alpha, alpha≈3.0 in 2D). The dynamics is Newtonian (F=ma), with all particles attracting each other symmetrically. The stable ring equilibrium at radius r≈10 arises because the inward pull of the centre particle and inward ring-ring radial components are exactly balanced by the outward net ring-ring force at that specific radius, analogous to a gravitational n-body ring equilibrium but with a different (1/r²) force law. The coupling constant G≈0.097 sets the overall strength of the interaction.

The 11-particle system obeys a pairwise attractive inverse-power-law interaction: each pair of particles (i, j) exerts a force F_ij = G * (r_j - r_i) / |r_j - r_i|^alpha on particle i, directed along the line connecting them. The force exponent alpha ≈ 3.5 means the interaction falls off faster than standard gravity (alpha=3 in 2D), producing a net radial acceleration on a symmetric ring that scales as r^(2-alpha) ≈ r^(-1.5). The coupling constant G ≈ 0.84 sets the overall strength of the interaction, and the system exhibits slow inward collapse for zero tangential velocity while rotating orbits are possible at nonzero tangential speed.
