The discovered law governs particle 2's motion through an attractive Yukawa-type force from particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The acceleration magnitude follows a = G·p1·exp(−k·r)/r^n, directed toward particle 1, where r is the separation distance. The force scales linearly with particle 1's scalar property p1, is entirely independent of particle 2's property p2, and decays with distance via both a power-law factor (r^n, n≈2) and an exponential screening factor (exp(−kr), k≈0.248), resulting in a rapidly decaying interaction at large separations.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive inverse-power-law force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The acceleration magnitude is G·p1/r^n, where r is the separation distance, p1 is the scalar property of the source particle, and n≈2 (inverse-square law). The probe particle property p2 has no influence on the acceleration — only the source particle's property p1 matters, entering linearly. The constant G≈11.6 sets the overall strength of the interaction, analogous to a gravitational constant in a 2D universe where only the "mass" of the source particle drives the force.

Particle 2 experiences an inverse-cube attractive force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with acceleration magnitude a = K·p1/r², where r is the separation distance (yielding F ∝ 1/r³ in force). The scalar property p1 of the source particle scales the force linearly, while p2 has no effect on the dynamics. The fitted coupling constant K ≈ 1.597 governs the strength of the interaction, producing oscillatory bound orbits for particles starting at rest and precessing elliptical orbits for particles with initial angular momentum.

Particle 2 is subject to a radially attractive central force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The force law combines an inverse-square short-range term with a constant long-range acceleration: a = (p1/p2) × (k/r² + C) × r̂_inward, where r is the separation between the particles. The scalar property p1 of the source particle acts as a charge/mass amplifying the force, while p2 acts as the inertial mass of the probe particle. The constant term C ≈ 0.1 produces a uniform background attraction at large distances, while the k/r² term dominates at short range.

Particle 2 experiences a purely attractive force toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with acceleration magnitude a = G × p1 / r^n, where r is the separation distance, p1 is the scalar property of the source particle, and the exponent n ≈ 2/3. The force is completely independent of p2 (the probe particle's property), unlike standard Newtonian gravity. Clean velocity measurements at multiple distances (r=2,4,8) confirm that a×r^(2/3) ≈ constant ≈ 1.59×p1, establishing this as a sub-Newtonian power law with a fractional distance exponent rather than the standard inverse-square law.

In this 2D universe, particle 2 experiences an attractive radial force toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin) governed by F = -G·p1^α / r^β · r̂, where r is the separation distance. The scalar property p2 of the probe particle has no influence on its motion whatsoever, making the acceleration purely a function of p1 and distance. The fitted exponents (α ≈ 0.148, β ≈ 0.628) differ significantly from standard Newtonian gravity, yielding a sub-linear distance dependence (weaker than 1/r) and a very weak coupling to the source property p1.

The force on particle 2 acts exclusively along the x-axis: F_x = -K * p1^alpha * x^n when x > 0, and zero when x ≤ 0. This means the universe has a half-space geometry where only particles with positive x-coordinate experience a restoring force toward the origin, with the force magnitude scaling as a power law in the x-displacement. The source particle property p1 weakly modulates the force strength via a small power-law exponent (~0.15), while the probe property p2 and the y-coordinate have no influence on the dynamics.

Particle 2 experiences a linear (harmonic) restoring force directed toward particle 1, with magnitude proportional to the displacement vector between them: **a = -K · p1 · (pos2 - pos1)**. This is a 2D harmonic oscillator where particle 1 acts as the force center, and the spring constant scales linearly with p1 (K ≈ 0.395). The property p2 has no effect on the acceleration, and the motion traces elliptical orbits around particle 1 with period T = 2π/√(K·p1), consistent with observed fall times of ~3s from r=4 with p1=1.

In this simulated universe, particle 2 experiences a purely radial, velocity-independent attractive force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The force magnitude follows a = K · p1^(1/2) / r^(3/4), where r is the separation distance, p1 is the scalar property of the source particle, and K ≈ 86 is a coupling constant. The probe particle's property p2 has no effect on its own acceleration, indicating test-particle behaviour where only the source charge p1 matters. Unlike Newtonian gravity (which scales as r^(-2) and p1^1), this universe features a much softer spatial decay (r^(-3/4)) and a square-root coupling to the source charge.

Particle 2 in this universe experiences an instantaneous impulsive force at t=0 that immediately sets its velocity proportional to the displacement from particle 1, directed toward particle 1, then moves freely (zero force) forever after. The speed imparted scales linearly with p1 and grows with the initial separation r according to a (r−r₀) law, where r₀≈1 is a characteristic screening radius below which no kick is given. The scalar property p2 of particle 2 has no influence on the dynamics.

In this 2D universe, charged particles interact via a modified Coulomb-like force where the force on particle i due to particle j is proportional to the charge q_i of the receiving particle and the sign (not magnitude) of the source charge q_j, directed radially away from the source. The force magnitude scales as K*|q_i|/r^n where r is the inter-particle separation, with the best-fit exponent near n=3 (steeper than the standard 2D 1/r law), suggesting a dipole-like or derivative interaction rather than a simple monopole field. The system obeys pairwise superposition, and opposite-sign charges attract while same-sign charges repel, consistent with electrostatic analogy but with an unusual charge-coupling asymmetry where only the receiving particle's charge magnitude matters.

In this 2D universe, every pair of particles exerts a purely repulsive force on each other regardless of the sign of their charges — only the magnitude matters. The acceleration of particle i due to particle j is given by a_i = k × |q_i| × |q_j| / r^n × r̂_ij, where n ≈ 3 (an inverse-cube rather than inverse-square law), k ≈ 0.558 is a coupling constant, and r̂_ij points from j to i. This force law violates Newton's third law: when |q_i| ≠ |q_j|, the acceleration magnitudes differ (particle with larger charge magnitude accelerates more), implying the charge acts both as a "coupling strength" for the experienced force and not as inertial mass.

This universe obeys a Coulomb-like inverse-square force law in 2D: each particle i experiences an acceleration equal to the sum over all other particles j of K·q_j·(r_i−r_j)/|r_i−r_j|³, where q_j is the signed scalar charge of the source particle j and K≈1. Unlike standard electrostatics, the receiving particle's own charge does not scale the force it feels — only the source particle's charge matters for setting the field strength, making every particle a universal test particle of unit inertial mass. Same-sign charges repel and opposite-sign charges attract, with the force falling off as 1/r² (inverse-square) in the separation between the two particles.

In this 2D universe, all charged particles repel each other regardless of the sign of their charges — the interaction depends only on charge magnitudes. The force between particles i and j has magnitude F_ij = K·min(|q_i|, |q_j|)/r², so that the acceleration of particle i due to particle j is a_i = K·min(|q_i|, |q_j|)/(|q_i|·r²), directed radially outward. This is equivalent to each particle having an inertial mass proportional to its charge magnitude (m_i = |q_i|), with the interaction force given by the minimum of the two charges divided by r², making the weaker charge the "bottleneck" that limits the strength of the interaction; the coupling constant K ≈ 2000 sets the overall scale.

In this simulated universe, each of the 10 charged particles experiences a force from every other particle that depends on its own charge (not the product of both charges): F_i = k × |q_i| × sign(q_i × q_j) × (r_i - r_j) / |r_i - r_j|³. The force is repulsive when two particles share the same sign of charge and attractive when they have opposite signs, exactly mirroring the sign convention of classical electrostatics. However, unlike standard electromagnetism where the Coulomb force scales with both charges, here only the receiving particle's own charge determines the force magnitude, while the source particle's charge only sets the direction/sign of the interaction.

The universe exhibits a shear flow field where each particle acts as a source of a velocity (not force) field. Specifically, a source particle at position (xj, yj) imparts to any other particle at (xi, yi) a y-velocity contribution of -K*(xi - xj), while the x-velocity is unaffected (dvx = 0). This creates a linear shear: particles displaced in the +x direction from a source are pushed in the -y direction, and vice versa, with the strength proportional to the x-separation and independent of y-separation or distance. The dynamics are purely kinematic — each particle moves at a constant velocity (no acceleration) equal to the sum of field contributions from all other source particles, causing straight-line trajectories with slopes set by the initial configuration.

This universe obeys standard Newtonian inverse-square gravity in 2D: every particle exerts a gravitational acceleration on every other particle proportional to G/r², directed along the line joining them (equivalently, `a_i = G * Σ_{j≠i} (r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|^3`). The 20 background particles are arranged in an annular ring at radius ~10-14 units from the center and orbit under their mutual gravitational attraction with initial velocities of ~3 units/s. The neutral probe particles feel the same gravitational force from the background particles (and each other, though they are massless/non-interactive in terms of generating field), following second-order Newtonian equations of motion with G ≈ 1.035 and force exponent alpha ≈ 3.

The simulated universe contains 20 background particles and 5 neutral probe particles governed by an N-body gravitational system. The force between any two particles follows an inverse-square law: F = G * m / r², directed along the line connecting the particles, where G ≈ 1.0. Background particles interact gravitationally with each other (full N-body) and carry non-zero initial velocities that keep them in orbital motion at radii of approximately 10–14 units from the center. The probe particles act as pure test particles, feeling the gravitational attraction of all background particles but exerting no force themselves on either the background or on each other.

The simulated universe contains 20 fixed background particles arranged in an approximately circular ring at radius ~12-14 units. Each probe particle obeys Newtonian mechanics (second-order ODE) and feels an attractive inverse-square gravitational force from each background particle: F_i = Σⱼ k·(rⱼ − rᵢ)/|rⱼ − rᵢ|³, where k ≈ 0.678 is the gravitational coupling constant. The force law has the same functional form as Newtonian gravity in 3D (force ∝ 1/r²), but operating in a 2D spatial domain. The structured background arrangement creates a net field at the center of the domain that produces the observed complex probe trajectories including circular orbits and radial decay along certain symmetry directions.

The physical system consists of 20 background particles undergoing free (inertial) motion at constant velocity, plus 5 neutral probe particles that feel attractive gravitational forces from the background particles but do not generate any field themselves. The force on each probe from each background particle follows an inverse power law: F = G * (r_bg - r_probe) / |r_bg - r_probe|^(alpha+1), where alpha ≈ 3 (steeper than standard Newtonian gravity with alpha=2), meaning the force magnitude falls off as 1/r^3 with distance. The gravitational coupling constant G ≈ 3.0 is larger than unity, and the exponent alpha ≈ 3.04 was consistently recovered across multiple fitting experiments using early-time probe accelerations and trajectory matching.

The governing force law is an attractive central force F = G * p1 / r³ directed from particle 2 toward the fixed particle 1 at the origin. The force magnitude scales linearly with the source particle's scalar property p1 and falls off as the inverse cube of the separation distance r, analogous to a dipole field or a 3D Coulomb law projected onto a 2D universe. The probe particle's property p2 has no influence on its own acceleration, indicating that only the source charge p1 enters the coupling constant.

Particle 2 experiences a central, attractive force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), obeying an inverse-cube distance law: the acceleration is **a = −G·p1²/r³·r̂**, where r is the separation and r̂ is the unit vector from particle 2 toward particle 1. The force coupling scales as the square of particle 1's scalar property p1 and is entirely independent of particle 2's property p2. The constant G ≈ 0.9 sets the overall strength of the interaction, and the r⁻³ spatial dependence is steeper than Newtonian gravity (r⁻²) but shallower than a van-der-Waals-type r⁻⁶ force.

The discovered law is an attractive Yukawa-type central force where particle 2 experiences acceleration a = -G·p1²·exp(-r/λ)/r² directed toward particle 1 at the origin. The force is exponentially screened with a characteristic length scale λ ≈ 0.626, meaning it decays much faster than pure inverse-square gravity at large distances. The coupling to particle 1's property p1 is quadratic, while p2 (particle 2's property) has no influence on the motion.

The simulated universe contains a separable force field where particle 2 experiences acceleration in each Cartesian direction that depends only on the corresponding coordinate: F_i(q) = -A · sign(q) · exp(−|q|³/3). This is a Weibull-type force with a cubic exponent, meaning the force peaks near the origin and falls off super-exponentially with distance, giving particles a universal terminal speed regardless of starting position. The scalar properties p1 and p2 of the two particles have no influence on the force, and the two spatial dimensions are entirely decoupled, so the particle follows independent 1D motions in x and y.

Particle 2 is attracted toward the fixed particle 1 (at the origin) by a Yukawa-like screened force law, where the acceleration magnitude is a = C · p1 · exp(−r/λ) / r^n, directed radially inward toward particle 1. The source particle property p1 enters linearly as a coupling strength, while the probe property p2 has no effect on the dynamics. The fitting reveals n≈0, λ≈1.0, and C≈1388, indicating the force is dominated by an exponential screening factor exp(−r) rather than a power-law decay.

This universe obeys a 1/r (rather than 1/r²) gravitational force law: the force between two particles of masses m_i and m_j separated by distance r is F = G·m_i·m_j/r, directed along the line joining them, with G ≈ 7.93×10⁻¹⁵. The dominant source of attraction is the central anchor particle (mass ~10¹⁵), whose 1/r force produces circular orbits at fixed speed v = √(G·M) ≈ 2.82 independent of orbital radius — consistent with the observed ring orbiter velocities. Probe particles interact with all 26 background bodies but not with each other, and the full N-body system is integrated forward using RK45.

The universe obeys a 1/r force law (arising from a logarithmic gravitational potential in 2D), where the acceleration of particle i due to particle j is a = G × m_j / r_ij × r̂_ij directed attractively. The massive anchor particle (mass ~10^15) dominates, producing an effective radial acceleration G × M_anchor / r ≈ 7.96/r toward the origin, confirmed precisely across all distances tested (0.5 to 20 units). All 26 particles interact pairwise through this same law with a universal coupling constant G ≈ 7.96 × 10^{-15}, with the ring orbiters contributing small but nonzero perturbations proportional to their masses (1, 2, or 4).

In this 2D universe, gravity obeys a logarithmic potential law rather than the familiar 3D inverse-square law. The gravitational force between any two particles is F = G·m₁·m₂/r, directed along the line connecting them — this follows from applying Gauss's law in 2D where field lines spread over a circumference (2πr) rather than a spherical surface. The gravitational constant G ≈ 7.96×10⁻¹⁵ was confirmed both by measuring the initial acceleration of stationary probes at known distances from the massive anchor (a·r = G·M_anchor = 7.96 = constant) and by the circular orbit condition of the ring particles (v² = G·M_anchor = 2.82² ≈ 7.96). All 26 particles participate as full N-body gravitational sources, with the anchor (mass 1e15) dominating and the ring orbiters (masses cycling 1,2,4) contributing smaller perturbations.

In this 2D universe, gravity obeys a 1/r force law (F = G·mᵢ·mⱼ/r) rather than the familiar 1/r² law of three-dimensional space. This corresponds to the solution of the 2D Poisson equation ∇²φ = 2πGρ, yielding a logarithmic potential φ(r) = G·M·ln(r). A key signature is that circular orbit speed is distance-independent (v = √(G·M)), consistent with the observed ring-orbiter speed of ≈2.82 at r=5, giving G·M_anchor ≈ 7.96 with M_anchor = 10¹⁵. All 26 particles interact pairwise through this law, but the enormous anchor mass dominates the dynamics of the probe particles.

The simulated universe obeys a logarithmic (1/r) force law: every pair of particles (i, j) exerts an attractive force of magnitude F = G·mᵢ·mⱼ/rᵢⱼ, corresponding to a potential V = −G·mᵢ·mⱼ·ln(rᵢⱼ). This is confirmed by the measured acceleration satisfying a·r = constant ≈ 7.95 across all probe radii from r=1 to r=20, and by the ring orbiters' circular orbital speed v = √(G·M_anchor) ≈ 2.82. All 26 particles interact pairwise via this same law, with the anchor's enormous mass (10¹⁵) dominating the force field while the symmetric ring of orbiters contributes negligibly to the net radial force on probes due to geometric cancellation.

In this simulated universe, all particles interact via a 1/r force law (corresponding to a logarithmic gravitational potential, which is the natural 2D analogue of Newtonian gravity). The acceleration of particle i due to particle j is a_i = G × m_j / r_ij² × (r_j - r_i), i.e., proportional to m_j/r_ij directed toward j. The anchor particle has enormous mass (~10^15), giving G × M_anchor ≈ 8.0, while the ring orbiters (masses 1, 2, 4 in repeating pattern) contribute additional 1/r forces that are summed pairwise, producing a small but measurable net outward force on the y-axis probes consistent with the 1/r interaction law summed over the ring geometry.

In this simulated universe, gravity follows a 1/r force law rather than the familiar 1/r² law: the force between two particles of masses m_i and m_j separated by distance r is F = G·m_i·m_j/r, directed along the line joining them. This means the gravitational acceleration experienced by a probe particle is a = G·M_source/r, independent of the probe's own mass, and the product a·r is constant (~8.0 for the dominant anchor particle with mass 10¹⁵ and G≈8×10⁻¹⁵). The ring of 20 orbiter particles also contributes forces via the same 1/r law, but their masses (1–4 units) make their contribution negligible compared to the anchor; nonetheless, the full simulation integrates all 26 pairwise interactions using this 1/r coupling.

In this simulated 2D universe, all particles interact through a logarithmic gravitational potential φ(r) = G·M·ln(r), which is the natural 2D analog of the Newtonian gravitational potential. This yields a force of magnitude F = G·m_i·m_j/r directed along the line connecting the two particles (attractive), so the acceleration of particle i due to particle j is a = G·m_j·(r_j−r_i)/|r_j−r_i|² in vector form. The massive anchor particle (mass ~10^15) at the origin dominates probe dynamics, with the background ring orbiters (masses cycling 1,2,4) providing smaller perturbations through the same pairwise interaction; the effective coupling G·M_anchor ≈ 8.0 was measured by observing that probe accelerations at distance r satisfy |a| = 8/r to high precision across two decades of distance.

In this simulated universe, gravity follows a 1/r force law rather than the 1/r² law of our own universe. Specifically, the acceleration of particle i due to particle j has magnitude G·mⱼ/rᵢⱼ and points toward j, equivalent to a logarithmic gravitational potential Φ(r) = -G·M·ln(r). The massive anchor particle (mass ~10¹⁵) dominates all interactions with an effective coupling G·M_anchor ≈ 8, while the ring orbiters (masses 1–4) contribute negligibly (G·m_orbiter/r ~ 10⁻¹⁴). This 1/r law was confirmed by the precise proportionality between acceleration and 1/r across distances from 5 to 22 units, and by the orbital speed v = √(G·M) ≈ 2.83 matching the observed orbiter speeds.

In this simulated universe, all 26 particles (anchor, ring orbiters, and probes) interact via a universal gravitational law where the force magnitude scales as 1/r (rather than 1/r² as in standard Newtonian gravity): F_ij = G × m_i × m_j / r_ij. This means the acceleration of particle i due to particle j is G × m_j / r_ij² × r̂_ij (directed toward j), which is the same expression as Newtonian gravity but derived from a different potential (logarithmic rather than 1/r). The coupling constant G ≈ 8×10⁻¹⁵ was determined by measuring that the massive anchor (M ≈ 10¹⁵) produces acceleration G×M/r = 8/r on test probes along the x-axis, with the ring orbiters providing small but measurable corrections to forces in other directions.

The discovered law governs particle 2 under an attractive central force from particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with force magnitude F = C×p1/r directed radially inward, where r is the separation distance and p1 is the source particle's scalar property. This corresponds to the 2D analogue of gravity/Coulomb attraction, arising from a logarithmic scalar potential U(r) = C×p1×ln(r), which is the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian. The scalar property p2 of the probe particle does not affect its acceleration (akin to the equivalence principle), and the coupling constant C ≈ 0.168 was determined by fitting to trajectory data.

Particle 2 is governed by a classical inverse-square gravitational attraction toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The acceleration is a = -G·p1·r̂/r², where r is the separation vector from particle 1 to particle 2, p1 is the scalar "mass" or "charge" of the source particle, and G≈1 is the coupling constant. The probe particle's own property p2 has no effect on its trajectory, so only p1 enters the force law. This is precisely Newton's law of gravitation in 2D with unit coupling constant.

Particle 2 experiences a centripetal (attractive) acceleration directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with magnitude a = K · p1 / (p2 · r), where r is the distance between the particles. The force scales linearly with the source property p1 (analogous to charge or mass of the source), inversely with the probe property p2 (analogous to inertial mass or a screening charge), and inversely with the first power of the distance r — distinguishing this from standard Newtonian gravity (which falls off as 1/r²) or a Coulomb law. The coupling constant K ≈ 0.159 ≈ 1/(2π) is a universal constant of this universe, and p2 acts as an inertial-like factor that reduces the response of heavier probes.

The discovered law governs a 1D force field where particle 2 experiences a force exclusively in the x-direction, proportional to p1 (the scalar property of particle 1) and inversely proportional to the x-separation raised to the power n≈2. The y-component of velocity is entirely unaffected — there is no y-force regardless of y-separation, meaning the interaction is not spherically symmetric but rather acts like a "1D gravity" along the x-axis only. The force is attractive (directed from particle 2 toward particle 1's x-coordinate), scales linearly with p1, and is completely independent of p2.

The law governing particle 2 is a Morse-type radial force centered on particle 1 (at the origin). The acceleration of particle 2 is F = 2*D*a*(exp(-a*(r-r0)) - exp(-2*a*(r-r0))) directed radially, where r is the separation distance — this produces a repulsive force at short range (r < r0), zero force at the equilibrium radius r0, and an attractive force with a maximum at r = r0 + ln(2)/a before decaying exponentially at large separations. The equilibrium radius r0 = r0_ref * p1^(-beta) scales with the source particle's property p1 via a power law (beta ≈ 0.213), while the probe particle property p2 has no influence on the dynamics whatsoever.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive central force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with the acceleration given by a = -K · p1^α / (p2^β · r^γ) · r̂, where r is the separation distance and r̂ is the unit vector pointing from particle 2 toward particle 1. The fitted parameters are approximately K≈2, α≈0.3, β≈1, γ≈2, meaning the force grows with a fractional power of p1, is inversely proportional to p2, and falls off as an inverse-square law with distance. This resembles a modified gravitational interaction where the source charge scales sub-linearly with p1 while the probe particle's inertial response is enhanced by p2.

In this simulated universe, the force on particle 2 is spatially separable: the x-component of acceleration depends only on the x-coordinate, and the y-component only on the y-coordinate, with no coupling between axes. Each component takes the form a_i = -C · p1^β · exp(-α|q_i|) · sign(q_i), an exponentially screened attractive force directed toward the origin with screening length 1/α ≈ 1. The scalar property p2 of the probe particle plays no role in the dynamics, while p1 (source strength) scales the force as a power law p1^β ≈ p1^1.1. The separability means equipotential surfaces are squares rather than circles, and the force decays exponentially rather than as an inverse power.

Particle 2 moves in an overdamped (first-order) regime where its instantaneous velocity equals the gradient of an oscillatory 1/r potential field generated by particle 1: v = p1 * ∇Φ(r), where Φ(r) = -(A·cos(kr) + B·sin(kr))/r. The radial velocity component is v_r = p1·[A·(k·sin(kr)/r + cos(kr)/r²) + B·(-k·cos(kr)/r + sin(kr)/r²)], which represents the gradient of a Friedel-oscillation-type potential with wavenumber k ≈ π/5. The property p2 of the probe particle has no effect on its dynamics, while p1 scales the field strength linearly; the oscillatory structure produces alternating attractive and repulsive regions as a function of separation distance.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin) whose magnitude is proportional to (v_char² − |v|²), where v_char = C·p1^α is a characteristic speed set by particle 1's property p1. This force accelerates the particle from rest up to v_char, beyond which the force vanishes entirely and the particle coasts at constant velocity. The result is a velocity-gated attraction: any initial speed below v_char causes acceleration toward particle 1 until the characteristic speed is reached, while any initial speed at or above v_char produces force-free ballistic motion regardless of distance.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive central force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with magnitude proportional to 1/r^n where r is the separation distance and n≈0.5 (i.e., F ∝ 1/√r). The force law F = -C/r^(1/2) × r̂ is purely radial and does not depend on the scalar properties p1 or p2 of either particle. The coupling constant C≈4.25 sets the overall strength of this sub-gravitational (softer than Newtonian) attraction.

In this simulated universe, particle 2 experiences an attractive central force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with acceleration magnitude a = K·p1/r^γ where r is the separation distance, p1 is the scalar property of the source particle, and the exponent γ ≈ 2.5. The probe particle's own property p2 has no effect on its acceleration, analogous to the equivalence principle in gravity. The best-fit parameters are K ≈ 0.71 and γ ≈ 2.5, giving an inverse-five-halves power law rather than the familiar inverse-square law of Newtonian gravity.

The discovered law is a central, attractive, inverse-square force: particle 2 experiences an acceleration directed toward particle 1 (at the origin) with magnitude G·(p1/p2)/r², where r is the separation distance. The scalar property p1 of the source particle acts as a "charge" or "mass" that strengthens the attraction, while p2 of the probe particle acts as an inertial-like quantity that reduces the acceleration (analogous to F=ma with m=p2). The coupling constant G≈0.16 sets the overall strength of the interaction, making this a Newtonian gravity-like law with p1 playing the role of source mass and p2 playing the role of probe mass (or equivalently, this is a Coulomb-like force with p1/p2 as the charge-to-mass ratio).

Particle 2 experiences a purely attractive central force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with magnitude F = G × p1^α / r^β, where r is the separation between the two particles. The source property p1 sets the strength of the field while the probe property p2 has no influence on the dynamics. Experiments revealed that the force scales as approximately p1^3 / r^3, though the exact exponents α and β are left as fittable parameters for the optimizer to refine given the noisiness of position measurements; the best-fit values found during discovery were G ≈ 160–194, α ≈ 3.0, and β ≈ 3.0–3.34.

The governing force on particle 2 is an attractive, central (radial) acceleration directed toward particle 1, with magnitude a = G·p1·exp(−α·r)/r^n, where r is the separation distance. The force scales linearly with the scalar property p1 of the source particle and is entirely independent of p2. The best-fit functional form is a screened power law with exponent n≈2.6 and a mild exponential modulation (α≈−0.05, slightly anti-screening), with prefactor G≈1.91.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive Yukawa-type force from particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with acceleration a = -K·p1·exp(-r/λ)/r² · r̂, where r is the separation distance and r̂ is the unit vector from particle 1 to particle 2. The force is linear in the source property p1 and completely independent of the probe property p2. The spatial dependence combines an inverse-square falloff with exponential screening at length scale λ≈2.01, yielding a force that decays much faster than pure gravity or Coulomb interactions and vanishes effectively beyond a few screening lengths.

Particle 2 is subject to an attractive 1/r force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with acceleration magnitude a = K × p1 / (p2 × r), where K = 1/(2π) ≈ 0.1592. This is the 2D analog of gravitational or electrostatic interaction, arising from the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian (logarithmic potential), rather than the 1/r² law seen in 3D. The coupling scales linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2, analogous to mass ratios in Newtonian gravity.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive central force directed toward particle 1, with acceleration governed by a Yukawa-like screened power law: a = C · p1 · exp(−k·r) / r^n, where r is the separation between the particles. The force scales linearly with p1 (the source property) but is completely independent of p2 (equivalence principle holds). The combination of power-law repulsion at short range and exponential screening at large range was inferred from the strongly distance-dependent accelerations measured across separations r = 2 to 8, which could not be reconciled with either a pure power law or a pure exponential alone.

In this simulated universe, particle 2 experiences an attractive Yukawa-type force directed radially toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The acceleration magnitude follows a = K · p1 · exp(−λr) / r^α, where r is the separation distance, p1 is the scalar property of the source particle, and K, λ, α are constants. The force is strictly independent of the probe particle's own property p2, is radially symmetric, and has no velocity dependence — it is a purely conservative screened power-law interaction, where the exponential factor causes the force to decay faster than any pure power law at large distances while remaining finite (softened) at small separations.

Particle 2 experiences a repulsive radial force from particle 1 (fixed at the origin) with magnitude F = C × p1^(1/2) / r^n, where r is the separation distance, C ≈ 0.2, and n ≈ 1.3. This force is independent of p2. The equation of motion also includes a linear velocity damping term −γv (with γ ≈ 2), causing the particle to quickly reach a terminal velocity v_∞ = F(r₀)/(γ) directed radially outward from particle 1, after which it drifts at nearly constant speed. The system thus behaves as a strongly overdamped repulsive interaction where the terminal drift speed scales as √p1 and decays as a power law in distance with exponent ≈ 1.3.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive Newtonian force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with magnitude a = G × p1^α × p2^β / r^n, where r is the separation distance. The force scales approximately as 1/r^1.09 (close to inverse-linear rather than the familiar inverse-square gravity), and depends on the scalar properties of both particles via power-law exponents (α≈0.58 for the source, β≈0.20 for the probe). The dynamics follow standard second-order Newtonian mechanics (F = ma), with the coupling constant G≈0.217 and all exponents left as fittable parameters to be refined by the evaluator.

In this simulated universe, all 10 particles interact pairwise through a sign-based (L1/Manhattan) force law: the force on particle i from particle j has an x-component equal to -C·sign(xᵢ - xⱼ) and a y-component equal to -C·sign(yᵢ - yⱼ), where C ≈ 0.054 is the coupling constant. This means each coordinate axis is treated independently — particles are attracted toward each other along each axis with a constant force magnitude C regardless of their separation distance. The result is effectively a pairwise attraction based on the L1 (taxicab/Manhattan) norm rather than the Euclidean distance, producing constant-acceleration trajectories that depend only on the relative signs of coordinate differences between pairs.

This universe obeys a first-order (overdamped) dynamical law in which each particle's velocity at every instant equals the sum of pairwise repulsive forces from all other particles — there is no inertia or acceleration, only velocity-as-force. The pairwise interaction between any two particles separated by distance r takes the Yukawa form F(r) = K · exp(−α·r) / r^n, which combines a power-law divergence at short range with an exponential screening at long range; the fitted values are approximately K ≈ 2.2, α ≈ 0.24, n ≈ 2. This screened repulsion means particles strongly repel at close range but the force falls off faster than any pure power law at large separations, consistent with a Debye-screened electrostatic or short-range interaction field.

In this 2D universe, all 10 particles interact pairwise through an attractive force whose magnitude falls off as 1/r with distance — equivalent to a logarithmic potential V(r) = -G·ln(r), which is the natural 2D analog of Newtonian gravity. The force on particle i due to particle j is F_ij = G/r_ij × r_hat_ij (pointing from i toward j), with coupling constant G ≈ 0.16. Forces from all other particles superpose linearly, and the system evolves via Newton's second law with unit masses.

The simulated universe obeys an attractive pairwise force law where the force magnitude between any two particles falls off as 1/r (not the familiar 1/r² of gravity), corresponding to a logarithmic potential V(r) = -G·ln(r). This is the natural two-dimensional analogue of gravity, arising from a 2D Gauss's law. All 10 particles interact mutually via superposition of these pairwise forces, with a coupling constant G ≈ 0.16, confirmed across separations ranging from 0.25 to 40 units.

All 10 particles in this universe interact via a pairwise attractive inverse-square force law, analogous to Newtonian gravity in 2D. The force on particle i due to particle j has magnitude F = G/r², directed along the line joining them toward j, where r is their separation distance and G ≈ 213.6 is the coupling constant. The system conserves momentum and energy (in the pairwise interaction sense), with all particles accelerating toward one another and eventually colliding at their mutual center of mass.

This 2D universe obeys a power-law gravitational force F_ij = G·m_i·m_j/r^3 · r̂_ij between every pair of particles, where the force falls off as the cube of the distance rather than the square (as in standard Newtonian gravity). All 26 particles — the massive anchor, the 20 background ring orbiters, and the 5 probe particles — interact pairwise through this law, giving each particle an acceleration proportional to the sum of G·m_j/r³ contributions from all other particles. The fitted gravitational constant G ≈ 2.26×10⁻¹¹ is consistent with the ring orbiters' circular orbits at r=5 with speed v≈2.59, and the exponent α=3.0 was confirmed by systematic MSE fitting that showed dramatic loss reduction over the standard Newtonian (α=2) baseline.

This universe obeys standard two-dimensional Newtonian gravity: every pair of particles (i, j) exerts a mutual attractive force F = G·mᵢ·mⱼ/rᵢⱼ², directed along the line joining them. The resulting acceleration on particle i is mass-independent (a_i = G·mⱼ/rᵢⱼ²·r̂ᵢⱼ, summed over all other particles j), confirming the equivalence principle holds. The gravitational constant G = 1.0 and the inverse-square exponent α = 2.0 were confirmed by multiple experiments measuring probe accelerations at varying distances and by scipy-based parameter fitting, with the characteristic zero-crossing of the net force (attractive inside, repulsive outside the ring of orbiters) fully explained by superposition of the massive anchor and the surrounding ring particles.

This universe obeys standard 2D Newtonian gravity: every pair of particles (i, j) exerts a mutual attractive force F = G × mi × mj × (rj − ri) / |rj − ri|³, directed along the line joining them. The anchor particle (index 0) has enormous mass (~10¹⁵) and sits near the centre, while 20 ring orbiters orbit it at radius ~5 in circular motion. The 5 probe particles experience the gravitational field from all 21 background particles plus each other, resulting in complex, time-varying trajectories due to the orbiting ring. The coupling constant G ≈ 3.8×10⁻¹⁴ is very small because the anchor mass is so large.

This universe obeys a 2D logarithmic-potential gravity where the force between two particles scales as 1/r (rather than 1/r² as in standard 3D Newtonian gravity). The acceleration on particle i due to particle j is G·m_j·(r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|², which corresponds to a gravitational potential proportional to log(r). The massive anchor at the center dominates at small radii (producing inward acceleration ∝ 1/r), while the ring of 20 orbiting particles at r=5 contributes an outward force at larger radii, creating a zero-crossing in the net acceleration around r≈12-13.

In this simulated universe, all 26 particles (the massive anchor, the ring orbiters, and the 5 probes) interact through a modified gravitational force law F = G·mi·mj/r^α where the exponent α≈1 rather than the standard α=2 of Newtonian gravity. The gravitational coupling constant G≈7.92×10^-15 is very small, but the anchor's enormous mass (~10^15) still dominates the inner-region dynamics. The transition from attractive to repulsive net force observed around r≈12-13 arises naturally from N-body physics: inside the orbiter ring (r=5), the anchor's attraction dominates, while outside the ring, the collective repulsive "shell" effect of the 20 ring orbiters overcomes the anchor's weakened 1/r attraction, producing the observed sign change.

This universe obeys a 2D gravitational force law where each particle exerts an acceleration on every other particle proportional to the source mass and inversely proportional to the square of the separation distance: a_i = G × Σ_{j≠i} m_j × (r_j − r_i) / |r_j − r_i|². This is the natural gravity in 2D space, corresponding to a logarithmic gravitational potential φ = −G·M·ln(r), which is the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian. The coupling constant G ≈ 7.9×10⁻¹⁵ is extremely small relative to the anchor mass (~10¹⁵), making the effective gravitational acceleration from the anchor approximately 7.9/r, while the ring orbiters also contribute repulsive or attractive forces depending on their relative positions.

In this 2D universe, all particles interact via a pairwise attractive force whose magnitude scales as F = G·m_j/r (a 1/r force law corresponding to a 2D logarithmic gravitational potential), directed from each source particle toward the probe. The acceleration of any particle is independent of its own mass (confirmed experimentally), consistent with a gravitational-type coupling. The massive central anchor (mass ~10^15) dominates the dynamics at short range, pulling probes inward with acceleration ∝ 1/R; at larger radii the geometric arrangement of ring orbiters creates a net outward effective force that grows with radius, producing a zero-crossing in the radial acceleration around r≈12-13, all of which is reproduced by the same 1/r pairwise law summed over all 26 particles.

In this 2D simulated universe, particles interact via two distinct force laws. The massive anchor particle (index 0) exerts an attractive force on all other particles with magnitude proportional to 1/r (a 2D Coulomb/gravitational law), giving acceleration a = G_A × M_anchor / r directed toward the anchor. All non-anchor particles (the ring orbiters and probes) exert mutually repulsive forces where the force vector is directly proportional to the displacement vector between particles (a linear/spring-like repulsion), giving acceleration a = G_R × m_source × (r_probe - r_source). This combination of central 1/r attraction and linear inter-particle repulsion allows the ring orbiters to maintain stable orbits while creating a net outward linear field (∝ r) outside the ring that partially counteracts the anchor's attraction at large distances.

The simulated universe obeys standard pairwise Newtonian inverse-square gravity in 2D: every pair of particles i and j exerts a mutual attractive force F_ij = G·m_i·m_j/r² directed along the line connecting them, where r is their separation distance. The gravitational constant G ≈ 7.67×10⁻¹⁵ is universal — the same G governs anchor-probe, ring-probe, and ring-ring interactions. The massive anchor (M = 10¹⁵) dominates probe dynamics at small radii, producing the clean 1/r² acceleration scaling observed, while the ring orbiters create more complex forces at larger radii due to their discrete, non-uniform mass distribution (masses cycling through 1, 2, 4) which produces non-trivial net forces that can appear outward at certain radii outside the ring.

In this simulated universe, all particles interact via an attractive inverse-distance (1/r) force law — the 2D analog of Newtonian gravity with a logarithmic potential. The acceleration of particle i due to particle j is a_ij = G * m_j * (r_j − r_i) / |r_ij|², where the magnitude falls off as 1/r rather than the standard 1/r². The coupling constant G ≈ 7.9 × 10⁻¹⁵ was determined from the anchor particle (mass ≈ 10¹⁵) dominating at small separations, where a*r ≈ constant ≈ 7.9. The sign reversal of net force at large radii (r > ~13) arises from the ring of 20 orbiters at r = 5, whose collective attraction overcomes the central anchor's attraction at far distances due to geometry.

The universe contains 11 particles interacting pairwise through an attractive force law F = G * m_i * m_j / r^alpha, where r is the inter-particle distance. The center particle (index 0) carries a much larger "mass" M than the ring particles (mass normalized to 1), which explains the observed increase in effective force at smaller ring radii — the center particle dominates at all scales but the ring-ring interactions (each with unit mass) provide an additional contribution that is significant at small separations. The exponent alpha ≈ 1 governs the radial falloff at large separations, while the large center mass M accounts for the enhanced acceleration observed at small ring radii. Free parameters G (coupling), alpha (force exponent), and M (center mass) are fitted to the trajectory data.

This universe contains 11 particles governed by a pairwise attractive force law, but with two distinct interaction channels: the central particle (particle 0) interacts with ring particles via F = G/r (a 1/r force corresponding to a logarithmic potential), while ring particles interact with each other via F = G/r² (an inverse-square law). The coupling constant G ≈ 8 appears identical for both interaction types. The system exhibits gravitational collapse when ring particles are released from rest: they first fall inward under the attractive forces, pass through the central region, then fly outward at high asymptotic velocity set by energy conservation; when given sufficient tangential velocity, ring particles maintain stable orbital configurations.

In this simulated universe, all 11 particles (the central particle and the 10 ring particles) interact through a universal attractive force law F = G/d, where d is the distance between any two particles. Unlike standard Newtonian gravity (F∝1/d²), this 2D universe uses a 1/d force law, which in 2D corresponds to an interaction derived from a logarithmic potential (analogous to Coulomb's law in 2D). The coupling constant G ≈ 1.45 is universal for all particle pairs. This attractive force causes the ring particles to spiral inward toward the center, pass through, and then fly outward with nearly constant velocity, with the detailed trajectory depending sensitively on the initial ring radius and tangential velocity.

This universe contains 11 particles interacting via a purely attractive pairwise power-law force F_ij = G * (r_j - r_i) / |r_j - r_i|^(alpha+1), analogous to a generalized gravitational law. All particles (center and ring) have equal masses and attract one another; the force strength G and exponent alpha are free parameters fitted to the observed trajectories. The observed dynamics — ring particles collapsing inward then explosively rebounding outward after passing near the center — is consistent with this purely attractive force that grows stronger at close range.

This universe obeys a universal attractive force law between all particle pairs, with force magnitude F = G/r^alpha where r is the inter-particle separation and the force is directed toward the attracting particle. For the symmetric ring configuration, the center particle pulls all ring particles inward while the ring particles mutually repel each other in the radial direction due to their geometry — this competition causes the ring to either collapse to the center (if center attraction dominates) and then explosively rebound, or expand outward. The best-fit exponent is approximately alpha=1 (a 2D logarithmic-potential type force), with coupling constant G≈7.4, consistent with the observed initial accelerations scaling as a ≈ G/r + 4.5G/r² across all tested radii.

In this simulated universe, all 30 particles interact pairwise through an attractive force whose magnitude falls off as 1/r (rather than the 1/r² of standard Newtonian gravity), where r is the separation between particle pairs. The acceleration on each particle is the vector sum of contributions G*(r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|² from all other particles, equivalent to a force derived from a 2D logarithmic scalar potential. The coupling constant G ≈ 0.159 ≈ 1/(2π), which is the natural prefactor for the Green's function of the 2D Laplacian, suggesting this universe obeys a 2D Poisson-type field equation rather than the 3D inverse-square law of our own universe.

In this simulated universe, all 30 particles interact through a pairwise attractive force whose magnitude scales as the 3/2 power of the inter-particle separation: F_ij = G · |r_ij|^(3/2) directed from particle i toward particle j. This differs from Newtonian gravity (which uses r^(-2)) — here the force grows with distance (super-linear), causing particles to accelerate faster as they move apart, which produces oscillatory behavior where particles overshoot and return. The coupling constant G ≈ 0.0096 sets the overall strength, and the exponent alpha ≈ 1.5 was identified through MSE fitting of trajectory data from symmetric multi-particle configurations.

In this 2D universe, all 30 particles interact pairwise through an attractive force whose magnitude scales as F = G/r, where r is the separation distance between two particles (a softer, longer-range law than standard gravity's 1/r²). The acceleration of each particle is the vector sum of contributions G/r² × r̂_ij from every other particle, directed radially toward the source particle — the field thus has a logarithmic potential φ(r) ∝ -G ln(r) rather than the Newtonian -G/r. The coupling constant G ≈ 1.60 was determined from isolated two-particle experiments where doublings of separation produced exact halvings of acceleration, confirming the 1/r force dependence.

This universe obeys a pairwise attractive inverse-square force law analogous to Newtonian gravity in 2D: each pair of particles (i, j) exerts a mutual attractive force of magnitude G/r², directed along the line connecting them, where r is their separation distance. The acceleration of particle i is the vector sum of G*(rj-ri)/|rj-ri|³ over all other particles j. The coupling constant G is approximately 0.8 in simulation units, though the functional form (1/r² attraction) is what matters most, with G fitted by the optimizer.

In this 2D universe, all 30 particles interact pairwise through an inverse-linear attractive force field: the acceleration experienced by particle i due to particle j has magnitude G/r_ij (where r_ij is the Euclidean separation), directed along the unit vector from i toward j. This differs from Newtonian gravity (which goes as 1/r²) — here the force falls off more slowly with distance, as 1/r. The coupling constant G ≈ 0.16 was determined from isolated-pair experiments where all other particles were placed at large symmetric distances, ensuring their contributions cancelled by symmetry, allowing clean measurement of the pairwise acceleration as a function of separation.

Particle 2 undergoes simple harmonic motion in its radial velocity component relative to particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The radial velocity oscillates as v_r(t) = v0_r·cos(ωt) − A·sin(ωt), where the amplitude A = C·p1/r0 is proportional to p1 and inversely proportional to the initial separation r0, and the angular frequency ω = π/2 is a universal constant independent of all parameters. The tangential velocity component is unaffected by the interaction and drifts freely, while p2 has no influence on the dynamics whatsoever.

The governing force law is a logarithmic-potential attraction: particle 2 experiences a force F = −K·(p1/p2)·r̂/r directed toward particle 1, where r is the separation distance and K ≈ 0.2. This arises from a scalar potential V(r) = K·(p1/p2)·ln(r), which is the natural two-dimensional analogue of Newtonian gravity, following from Gauss's law applied to a 2D space. The coupling scales linearly with the source property p1 and inversely with the probe property p2, so heavier sources attract more strongly while more massive probes are less responsive to the field.

Particle 2 is subject to a radially-directed, time-oscillating force from particle 1 fixed at the origin. The acceleration is a = -C·(p1/p2)·sin(ω·t)/r · r̂, where r is the current distance between the particles, r̂ is the outward radial unit vector, ω = π (period T = 2), and C ≈ 0.510. The force is proportional to the source strength p1, inversely proportional to the probe mass p2, inversely proportional to the radial distance (1/r spatial decay), and oscillates sinusoidally in time — resembling the radial component of a cylindrical wave field with a fixed temporal frequency driving particle transport. The net effect combines oscillatory motion with a secular drift whose sign and magnitude depend on the particle's radial position.

Particle 2 experiences an inverse-square gravitational-like attractive force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), but only when the separation r exceeds a critical "dead zone" radius r_c ≈ 1.65. Inside r_c the force vanishes entirely and the particle moves inertially. The force magnitude is G/r² (with G ≈ 490) and is independent of the scalar properties p1 and p2. This creates a sharp inner boundary: particles placed outside r_c accelerate inward through origin and continue decelerating on the far side, while particles inside r_c remain stationary.

In this 2D universe, particle 2 experiences an oscillatory attractive force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with magnitude F = C · p1 · r^n · |cos(k · r²)|, where r is the separation distance and k ≈ π/8. The force scales linearly with p1 but is completely independent of p2. The |cos(k·r²)| factor creates a series of force zeros at r = 2, 2√3, 2√5, ... (where r² = π/(2k), 3π/(2k), ...), producing an unusual oscillatory radial dependence that is distinct from standard inverse-power or Yukawa laws, with the force strength modulated by a Gaussian-like envelope at short range and rapid oscillation at larger distances.

Particle 2 experiences a purely radial attractive force toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin), with acceleration magnitude a = G·p1/r^n. The strength scales linearly with the source property p1 (analogous to gravitational mass of the source), but the probe property p2 does not affect the acceleration (equivalence principle holds). The distance exponent n≈1.56 differs from the standard Newtonian value of 2, suggesting an anomalous gravitational-like interaction in this 2D universe with a sub-quadratic distance law.

Particle 2 in this universe obeys a velocity-field law rather than a Newtonian force law: the system enforces that the inward radial speed toward particle 1 is at least K·p1/r^α, where r is the separation distance. If the particle's existing radial velocity (toward particle 1) is already larger than this threshold, it is unaffected; otherwise, the radial velocity is rapidly driven up to the threshold value (implemented as a very stiff one-sided restoring acceleration with gain 1000). The transverse velocity component is fully preserved at all times, as is any pre-existing radial velocity exceeding the natural field value. The parameter p2 (the probe particle's property) has no influence on its dynamics whatsoever.

The force law governing particle 2 is an inverse-square-law gravitational attraction: particle 2 experiences an acceleration directed toward particle 1 with magnitude a = G·p1·p2/r², where r is the distance between the particles, p1 and p2 are the scalar properties of particles 1 and 2 respectively, and G ≈ 7.5 is the coupling constant. Both particle properties enter symmetrically and linearly into the force, analogous to how mass enters Newton's law of gravitation. The particle stops upon reaching the origin (absorbed by particle 1), and orbits are possible with tangential initial velocities — consistent with standard Keplerian mechanics but with an unusually large coupling constant G≈7.5.

The force on particle 2 is purely radial and proportional to p1 (the source particle's property), with p2 having no influence. The force law is F = p1*(A·r·exp(−α·r) − B)·r̂, where r̂ points from particle 1 toward particle 2. This produces a repulsive "hump" at intermediate separations (peaked near r = 1/α) and a constant attractive background force −p1·B at large distances, creating a stable equilibrium at the radius where A·r·exp(−α·r) = B. The constant long-range attraction (unlike the 1/r decay in normal 2D gravity) is the most unusual feature of this universe's physics.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive central force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The acceleration magnitude follows a power-law in the separation distance r: a = G × (p1/p2) / r^α, where α ≈ 1.5 (a 3/2-power law, distinct from standard gravity's 1/r²). The force scales linearly with p1 (source strength) and inversely with p2 (probe property, analogous to inertia or charge), and the coupling constant G varies between experimental runs suggesting a stochastic or run-dependent coupling in this universe.

Each of the 6 particles independently experiences a central restoring (harmonic-trap-like) force directed toward the coordinate origin, with magnitude F = min(k·r, F_max) that grows linearly at short range and saturates to a constant at large distances (~r > 4 units). In addition, each particle pair interacts through a pairwise force whose sign is determined solely by the "source charge" of the emitting particle — particles {0, 3, 5} carry positive source charge (+1) and attract all neighbors, while particles {1, 2, 4} carry negative source charge (−1) and repel all neighbors — with the pairwise force magnitude also following a capped-linear form. The central field dominates dynamics (F_max ≈ 0.60), while the pairwise couplings are weaker (F_max_p ≈ 0.06), producing the oscillatory infall toward the origin observed across all experiments.

Each of the 6 particles in this universe experiences an external radial force directed toward the origin (centre of the coordinate system), with no pairwise interaction between particles whatsoever. The force magnitude follows a saturating tanh law: F(r) = A·tanh((r/r₀)ⁿ), where r is the particle's distance from the origin, A≈26.3 is the saturation amplitude, r₀≈3.2 is the characteristic length scale, and n≈3 is the power governing the steepness of the transition. At small radii the force grows approximately as r³ (harmonic-cube confinement), while at large radii it saturates to a constant value A, giving bounded acceleration regardless of how far particles travel from the centre.

In this 2D universe, particles interact through a "boundary tension" field: along each Cartesian axis independently, only the two extreme particles (the one with the largest coordinate and the one with the smallest) experience a constant inward force of magnitude C ≈ 297, directed toward the interior of the particle cloud. All particles that are not axis-extremes feel zero force. This is analogous to a constant-tension elastic band stretched around the outermost particles along each axis, pulling them inward regardless of their separation distance. The force law is purely topological in the sense that it depends only on which particle is currently the axis-extreme (determined by rank ordering), not on any distance or field strength.

In this simulated universe, all 6 particles interact through a pairwise attractive force that falls off as the inverse of the separation distance: F(r) = G/r, directed along the line joining each pair of particles. This corresponds to a logarithmic potential V(r) = -G·ln(r), fundamentally different from the 1/r² gravitational force familiar in our universe. The coupling constant G ≈ 0.17 was determined by isolating clean two-particle interactions (placing the other four particles on the far y-axis to eliminate contamination) and observing that the product F·d remained constant across all tested separations from d=2 to d=12.

In this universe, each particle j generates a force field proportional to the component-wise signed square of its own position: g(r_j) = -G × (x_j|x_j|, y_j|y_j|). Every other particle i feels an acceleration equal to the sum of these fields from all N-1 other particles: a_i = Σ_{j≠i} g(r_j). This is a non-pairwise "broadcast" interaction where each particle radiates a field based purely on its own displacement from the origin (not the relative displacement between particles), and all other particles respond equally to this field regardless of their own positions. The quadratic component-wise dependence (rather than isotropic r²) means the force magnitude scales differently depending on the angle of the source particle's displacement.

This universe contains a 2D N-body gravitational system where 30 background particles interact mutually via a standard inverse-square force law: F_ij = G*(r_j - r_i)/|r_j - r_i|^3, equivalent to 1/r² gravitational attraction. The 5 neutral probe particles act as test masses — they feel the gravitational field generated by the background particles but exert no gravitational force back on the background. The gravitational constant G ≈ 5.2 is larger than in our universe, but the force exponent (alpha = 3, corresponding to 1/r²) matches standard Newtonian gravity in 2D.

The simulated universe contains 30 fixed (static) background particles that generate a gravitational field, plus 5 neutral probe particles that move under the influence of this field without generating any field themselves. The force on each probe from each background particle follows an inverse power law: F = G * r_vec / r^(alpha+1), where r_vec is the displacement vector from probe to source and alpha is the force exponent (near 2 for standard gravity, but potentially different in this universe). The background particles are held fixed (or evolve very slowly under mutual gravity), and the probes integrate second-order Newtonian dynamics (ẍ = ΣF) through their trajectories.

This universe obeys a pairwise gravitational-like force law where each particle pair (i,j) experiences a force proportional to G/r^alpha directed along the line joining them, where r is the separation distance. The 30 background particles form a self-interacting N-body system with mutual forces, while the 5 probe particles are neutral test particles that feel the gravitational field of the background but do not generate any field themselves. From fitting, the force parameters are consistent with standard gravity: coupling constant G≈1.0 and power-law exponent alpha≈2.0, meaning force falls off as 1/r² with distance.

The simulated universe contains 30 background particles and 5 neutral probe particles. All particles interact through an inverse-power gravitational force law: the force on particle i from particle j is F = G * (r_j - r_i) / |r_j - r_i|^(alpha+1), where alpha ≈ 2 corresponds to a standard inverse-square law in 2D. The key structural feature is that the interaction is one-way for probes: background particles interact mutually (evolving under their own gravity starting from rest), while the neutral probe particles feel gravitational attraction from all background particles but do not themselves generate any gravitational field. A softening parameter prevents singularities at close separations.

This universe contains a 2D N-body gravitational system with 35 particles total (30 fixed-configuration background particles plus 5 neutral probe particles). All particles interact pairwise through an attractive force law of the form F_i = G × Σ_j (r_j − r_i) / |r_j − r_i|^α, where the force magnitude falls off as 1/r^(α−1) with distance. The background particles are not truly fixed — they also evolve dynamically under mutual gravitational interactions, which causes the probes to experience a time-varying field. Based on initial acceleration measurements comparing observed probe motions to predicted forces from nearby background particles, the exponent α ≈ 2 (standard inverse-square force law) with coupling constant G ≈ 1 provides the best fit to the data.

Particle 2 experiences an attractive central force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin) with magnitude F = k·p1/r^n, where r is the separation distance, p1 is the source particle's scalar property, and p2 has no dynamical effect. The force law resembles a power-law gravity with exponent n≈1 (logarithmic potential), coupled with a small linear velocity damping term γv that accounts for the observed gradual energy dissipation in trajectories. The coupling constant k and damping γ are fittable parameters, with p1 entering linearly as a charge-like source strength.

The system exhibits overdamped (purely kinematic) dynamics where particle 2's velocity is directly determined by its current position rather than by integrating an acceleration. The velocity field takes the form v = -C·p1/r^α · r̂, an attractive drift directed radially toward particle 1 at the origin, with magnitude scaling as 1/r^α (approximately 1/√r with α≈0.5 and C≈0.60). The scalar property p2 has no effect on the motion, while p1 linearly scales the drift speed everywhere in the field.

Particle 2 is attracted toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin) by a central force whose magnitude falls off as the inverse 3/2 power of the separation distance: F = K·p1·r^(-3/2), directed radially inward. The scalar property p1 of the source particle acts as a charge-like coupling strength, while the property p2 of the probe particle has no influence on its acceleration (the force is purely kinematic in p2). The singularity of the potential at r=0 is strong enough that particles passing through the origin undergo an effective "corner turn," after which they travel at constant velocity, consistent with the force being conservative with a potential V(r) ∝ r^(-1/2) that diverges at the origin.

Particle 2 experiences a purely attractive force directed toward the fixed particle 1 (located at the origin), with acceleration magnitude given by a = G·p1/r², where r is the separation distance and G ≈ 3.0 is a coupling constant of this universe. The force depends linearly on p1 (the source property) but is entirely independent of p2 (the probe property), unlike Newtonian gravity where both masses enter. This inverse-square law has the same spatial form as standard gravity or Coulomb's law, but with a gravitational "constant" of G = 3.0 rather than the standard value.

In this simulated universe, particle 2 experiences an attractive central force directed toward particle 1 (fixed at the origin). The acceleration of particle 2 is governed by a = G × √p1 / r^(2/3) × (-r̂), where r is the distance between the particles and p1 is the scalar property of the source particle. Notably, the probe particle's own property p2 has no influence on its acceleration, making this analogous to a "gravitational-like" force but with a square-root coupling to the source charge and an unusual r^(-2/3) distance dependence (much weaker than standard 1/r² gravity). The fitted coupling constant is G ≈ 2.23.

The system follows a gravitational-like inverse-square law where each particle attracts every other particle with a force proportional to the vector difference divided by the cube of their distance. The dynamics are symmetric and conserve angular momentum, with stable orbits achievable when tangential velocity balances the centripetal force. The coupling strength G is a universal constant fitted from trajectory data.

The physical system is governed by an inverse-square attractive force between each ring particle and the central particle. The force magnitude is proportional to 1/r², where r is the distance to the centre, analogous to Newtonian gravity in two dimensions. The central particle acts as a fixed source, and the ring particles orbit or collapse depending on their initial tangential velocity relative to the escape threshold. The coupling constant G determines the strength of the interaction and is fitted from the data.

The system is governed by a central harmonic potential where every particle experiences a restoring force proportional to its distance from the origin, resulting in isotropic 2D harmonic oscillator dynamics. This explains both the stable rotating configurations when tangential velocity matches the harmonic frequency and the radial collapse or expansion otherwise. The symmetry of the ring and the linear scaling of stable velocity with radius confirm this quadratic potential.

The system is governed by a central inverse-square force field sourced by the particle at the origin. The ring particles move under this attraction alone, with no mutual interactions. Their trajectories are Keplerian: low tangential velocity leads to collapse, while sufficient angular momentum produces stable or expanding orbits. The central particle remains approximately fixed, indicating it dominates the field dynamics.

The system consists of 11 particles interacting through a pairwise attractive force that scales as $1/r^{1.5}$, where $r$ is the distance between particles. This force arises from a potential proportional to $1/\sqrt{r}$, leading to non-Newtonian dynamics. The exponent was determined by analyzing the radial acceleration during collapse experiments, which scaled as $1/r^{1.5}$ across different initial radii.

The motion of particle 2 is governed by a central force directed toward particle 1 at the origin, with acceleration magnitude proportional to p1/p2. The force has two components: an inverse-square term and a constant term, both attractive. This results in strong acceleration at small distances and a finite acceleration at large distances, leading to bounded or unbounded trajectories depending on initial conditions.

The motion of particle 2 is governed by a central force inversely proportional to its distance from particle 1, with strength proportional to the ratio of the source charge p1 to the probe charge p2. The acceleration diverges as the particle approaches the origin, causing strong attraction and oscillatory trajectories. This suggests a potential logarithmic in form, leading to a 1/r force law.

The physical system is governed by a central force field where particle 2 experiences an acceleration proportional to the product of the scalar charges p1 and p2, directed radially toward particle 1 at the origin. The magnitude of the acceleration scales as 1/sqrt(r), indicating a potential with a 1/sqrt(r) dependence, which is unusual for standard 2D or 3D field theories and suggests anomalous dimension or screening. The dynamics are conservative and result in bounded or unbounded orbits depending on initial conditions.

The motion of particle 2 is governed by a linear restoring force directed toward the origin, analogous to a harmonic oscillator. This results in an acceleration proportional to -(p1/p2) times the position vector.

The physical system is governed by an attractive central force between the two particles, where the acceleration of particle 2 is proportional to the product of their scalar properties (p1 and p2) and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. The force acts along the line connecting the particles, and the dynamics are analogous to gravitational attraction in three dimensions, but observed in a two-dimensional universe.

The physical system is governed by a velocity-dependent force that drives the particle to a terminal velocity whose magnitude is proportional to p1. The direction of the terminal velocity is determined by the initial position, with the particle accelerating toward the origin and then continuing in that direction with a constant speed. The approach to terminal velocity is exponential, suggesting a linear drag force. The scalar p2 does not influence the dynamics.

The system is governed by an attractive inverse-square law force between the two particles, where the force magnitude is proportional to the product of their properties and inversely proportional to the square of the distance. The inertial mass of particle 2 is assumed to be proportional to its property p2. The force is regularized at very small distances to avoid singularities.

The system is governed by a repulsive inverse-square force between two particles, where the force magnitude is proportional to the product of their scalar charges p1 and p2. The force acts along the line connecting the particles and accelerates the probe particle away from the source. The source particle remains approximately fixed, suggesting asymmetric mass or fixed background field interpretation.

The physical system is governed by a constant-magnitude radial force directed toward or away from the source particle, balanced by a linear drag force proportional to velocity. The magnitude of the force is proportional to the product of the scalar properties of the two particles. This results in a terminal velocity that is proportional to the force magnitude and independent of the initial distance, as observed in the experiments. The direction of the force (attractive or repulsive) depends on the product of the scalar properties, with the data suggesting alternating signs for different values, though the exact dependence was not fully resolved.

The physical system resembles a Lorentz force in two dimensions, where the force on particle 2 is perpendicular to its velocity and proportional to the product of the particle properties p1 and p2. The force magnitude is inversely proportional to the distance from the origin, suggesting a background field generated by particle 1 that couples to the velocity of particle 2. The dynamics are analogous to a charged particle moving in a magnetic field that is radial in strength but uniform in direction.

The system exhibits a pairwise attractive force between any two charged particles, regardless of the sign of their charges. This results in all charged particles attracting each other, with the interaction strength determined by the product of the square roots of their charge magnitudes.

The system follows a pairwise inverse-square force law in 2D, where the force between two particles is proportional to the product of their charges and inversely proportional to the square of their separation. The force is attractive for opposite charges and repulsive for like charges, with direction along the inter-particle axis. The dynamics obey superposition and Newton's third law, indicating a conservative central force field analogous to Coulomb's law but unexpectedly scaling as 1/r² in two spatial dimensions.

The system follows a Coulomb-like law in two dimensions where the force between particles is proportional to the product of their charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance. The force is central and pairwise additive, with opposite charges attracting and like charges repelling. Neutral particles experience no force, confirming linear coupling to charge.

The system follows a pairwise inverse-square force law in two dimensions, where the force between particles is proportional to the product of their signed charges and inversely proportional to the square of their separation. The force is attractive for opposite charges and repulsive for like charges, acting along the line connecting the particles. Particles with zero charge do not experience or exert forces, indicating a direct coupling between charge and interaction strength.

The system follows a 2D electrostatic-like law where the force between two particles is proportional to the product of their charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance, with direction along the line connecting them. Like charges attract and opposite charges repel, indicating an inverted sign convention compared to our universe. The force scales as 1/r² in magnitude, consistent with the gradient of a logarithmic potential in two dimensions.

The physical system is governed by an inverse-square law attraction between probe particles and a fixed background population. Each background particle generates a radial attractive field whose strength decays as the inverse square of distance. The total acceleration on a probe is the vector sum of contributions from all background particles. The physical system consists of a fixed background population of particles that generate a static gravitational-like field obeying an inverse-square law. The neutral probe particles move under the influence of this field, experiencing acceleration proportional to the vector sum of forces from each background particle. The force magnitude decreases with the square of the distance, and the background particles do not move or interact with each other. The system is conservative and deterministic.

The underlying physics is a static, exponentially screened attractive field generated by the fixed background population. Each background particle exerts a Yukawa-type force on the probes, proportional to exp(-r/λ)/r², pulling them toward its position. The force is additive, instantaneous, and depends only on distance, with no velocity coupling or background particle motion.

The system is governed by first-order dynamics where each probe's velocity is determined by its position within a smooth, background-generated vector field. The field is not gravitational but exhibits rotational and divergent components, suggesting a topological or emergent flow structure. Probes act as passive tracers with no inertia, instantly aligning with the local velocity.

The underlying physics is a 2D Lorentz-like force law where neutral probes experience an acceleration composed of a position-dependent electric-like field and a velocity-dependent force perpendicular to motion, analogous to a magnetic field. The background particles generate both fields through power-law interactions, with the electric component radial and the magnetic component scalar and isotropic. The dynamics are time-irreversible due to the velocity-coupling term.

The system features an attractive inverse-square force between the two particles, where the source strength is proportional to p1 and the inertial mass of the probe particle is proportional to p2. The acceleration of particle 2 is given by a = - (G p1 / p2) / r^2 in the radial direction, analogous to gravity with p1 as gravitational mass and p2 as inertial mass. The force on particle 2 is central, attractive, and exponentially screened, depending on the product of the scalar charges p1 and p2. The interaction strength decays as exp(-κr) with distance, indicating a short-range force mediated by a massive field.
